204

Recent Developments

Note an important point about all the empirical facts Joe and Sara can jointly
verify. For example, at snapshot A, their two clocks are adjacent to one another, so
they can jointly verify the time on those clocks as the clocks are right next to one
another. We can imagine they each take a photograph that shows both SC1 and
JC1 at snapshot A. Their photographs had better look the same, since they are
photos of the same moment in space and time. And indeed, their photos will both
show SC1 and JC1 reading 0.00 at snapshot A.
Likewise at snapshot B. Here, JC2 and SC1 are directly next to each other, and
so Sara and Joe can independently take photos of the two clocks. And again, the
two photos had best look the same, unless the universe is far stranger than we
think it is. And indeed, the two photos will both show SC1 reading 0.004444, and
JC2 reading 0.005555.
But although Sara and Joe agree on these facts they can jointly verify, they will
disagree substantially on what has occurred. From Joe’s point of view, 0.005555
seconds have elapsed for him between snapshot A and snapshot B, whereas only
0.004444 seconds have elapsed for Sara. Hence, from Joe’s point of view, he is the
older twin.
But from Sara’s point of view, 0.004444 seconds have elapsed for her between
snapshot A and snapshot B, whereas only 0.0035555 seconds have elapsed for Joe.
Hence, from her point of view, she is the older twin.
In short, Sara and Joe both say that they are the older twin. And from their
respective points of view, each is equally right.
What about their disagreements on what the other clocks read?
Before closing this section, it may help to consider some of the disagreements
between Sara and Joe. As we saw above, they agree on what the clocks read in the
situations (snapshot A and snapshot B) where they can jointly verify what certain
of the clocks read. What about their disagreements? In particular, in snapshot A,
Sara and Joe disagree on what Joe’s distant clock JC2 reads. Likewise at snapshot
B, they disagree on what JC1 reads.
We considered above the idea that Sara and Joe could take photos of the clocks
as they were directly next to each other. And we saw that their photos would
appear the same. What if they took long-distance photos of the other clocks
involved? Recall that, at snapshot A, Sara and Joe disagree on what Joe’s second
clock, JC2, reads. Joe thinks that at snapshot A, JC2 reads 0.000, whereas Sara
thinks that at snapshot A, JC2 reads 0.002. So suppose at snapshot A, Sara and Joe
both take a long-distance photo of Joe’s second clock, JC2. Such a long distance
photo would be technically difﬁcult, but not impossible. So would such a photo
reveal a contradiction between Sara and Joe’s points of view?
The answer will be no, but to understand this situation, we need to remind
ourselves of some facts about light and long-distance photography. First, remind
yourself that the speed of light is fast, but still ﬁnite. That is, it takes time for light
to travel from an object to your eye, or from an object to a camera. To illustrate
this fact, consider the light from the sun. The sun is about 93,000,000 miles, or
150,000,000 kilometers, from Earth. At 300,000 kilometers per second, light takes
about eight minutes to travel from the sun to the Earth. So when you see the sun,

23 The Special Theory of Relativity

the light hitting your eye (that is, the light that is causing you to have your visual
image of the sun) left the sun about eight minutes earlier. In other words, what
you are seeing is not the sun as it is at this moment; rather, you are seeing the sun
as it was about eight minutes ago.
This point is much more dramatic when you think about light from stars. For
example, the Andromeda Galaxy is the most distant object you can see with the
naked eye, and this galaxy is about 2,500,000 light years away. When you look at
this galaxy, what you are seeing is the Andromeda Galaxy as it was 2.5 million
years ago, not as it is now, and if you take a photograph of it, your photograph will
be a photo of the Andromeda Galaxy as it was 2.5 million years ago.
So if at snapshot A Sara and Joe take photographs of Joe’s distant clock, that is,
JC2, they must take into account the fact that it took some time for the light that
caused the photograph to travel from the clock to their cameras. When they take
this fact into account, here is what the situation looks like.
First point: when Sara and Joe take photos of JC2, their photos will both show
the same time on JC2. Speciﬁcally, their photos will both show JC2 reading
0.003333. Don’t miss the negative sign – the clock is reading 0.003333 seconds
before it reaches 0.00. When the two of them take into account the time it takes
the light to travel from JC2 to the camera, the situation is as follows.
From Joe’s point of view

Joe’s work is easy. From his point of view, the light traveled 1,000 (j) kilometers
from JC2 to his camera. At 300,000 kilometers per second, the light will have
taken 0.003333 seconds to reach his camera. Joe deduces, correctly (for him), that
at the moment the photo was taken, that is, at snapshot A, JC2 read 0.003333 +
0.003333 = 0.000. He concludes, correctly (for him), that at snapshot A, both of
his clocks read 0.000, and that his two clocks are synchronized.
From Sara’s point of view

Sara’s calculations are a bit more difﬁcult. Still, they require nothing more than
basic algebra. If you want to skip the details, you can trust my ﬁgures and skip to
the end of this section. But if you are curious, here is how Sara reasons.
From Sara’s point of view, Joe is in motion toward her at 180,000 kilometers per
second. So the light that produced her photograph had to have left JC2 when JC2
was much further away than the 800 (s) kilometers separating JC1 and JC2. That
is, the light from JC2 – the light that results in her photo – is traveling toward her
at 300,000 kilometers per second. Joe, on the other hand, is traveling toward her at
180,000 kilometers per second. So for the light and Joe to reach her at the same
time, that is, at snapshot A, the light that results in her photo left JC2 when JC2
was much further away than the merely 800 (s) kilometers separating JC1 and JC2.
To ﬁgure out how far this light has traveled, Sara reasons as follows. Suppose d
represents the distance the light traveled (again, the light that resulted in Sara’s
photo of JC2). Suppose t represents the time it took this light to travel, at 300,000
kilometers per second, from JC2 to her camera. Then Sara knows that
t

d

Keeping in mind that Joe is 800 (s) kilometers closer to her than is JC2, and that
Joe is traveling at 180,000 kilometers per second, Sara also knows that
t

d 800
180; 000

With a little algebra, from these facts Sara can deduce that the light that produced
the picture left JC2 when JC2 was 2,000 (s) kilometers away. So the light traveled
2,000 (s) kilometers at 300,000 kilometers per second, which would take 0.00667
(s) seconds. Since JC2 is moving, its time is moving more slowly. In particular,
when 0.00667 (s) seconds have elapsed, only 0.005333 (j) seconds will have
elapsed on JC2. So when the light reached her camera (that is, at snapshot A, when
Joe and his ﬁrst clock are directly below her), 0.005333 (j) seconds have passed
on JC2.
So Sara deduces, correctly (for her), that at the moment the photo was taken,
JC2 read 0.003333 + 0.005333 = 0.00200. She concludes, correctly (for her), that
Joe’s two clocks are not synchronized. Rather, Joe’s second clock (JC2) is 0.00200
seconds ahead of his ﬁrst clock (JC1).
Once again, notice that Sara and Joe agree on what their respective photos read
– both show, at snapshot A, that JC2 reads 0.003333. But they disagree on what
this piece of data shows. For Joe, it conﬁrms that his clocks are synchronized and
that he is the older twin. For Sara, it conﬁrms that Joe’s clocks are not synchro­
nized and that she is the older twin. Notice an emerging trend – Sara and Joe will
agree on all the direct empirical data. But they disagree on what that data shows.
In particular, they disagree on issues involving how much time has elapsed, how
far apart objects are, and whether events are simultaneous.
In summary, there is no contradiction in special relativity. Even though it
seems counterintuitive, in the sorts of scenarios we are considering Sara and Joe
will each (from their own point of view) be the older twin, and each is equally
correct.

Spacetime, Invariants, and the Geometrical Approach to
Relativity
Shortly after Einstein published the special theory of relativity, one of his earlier
mathematics teachers, Hermann Minkowski (1864–1909), recognized that what
is called the spacetime interval is an invariant property of special relativity.
Understanding the spacetime interval will allow us to get an idea of one of the
central notions associated with relativity, namely the notion of spacetime, as well
as allow us to understand the concept of variant and invariant properties. It will
also allow us to see a brief sketch of a common alternative approach to relativity,
that being the geometrical approach.
Occasionally, one hears claims that, according to Einstein’s relativity theory,
“everything is relative,” or sentiments to that effect. As we saw above, length, time,
and simultaneity do indeed differ between moving and stationary points of view,
and thus such properties are relative to a point of view. But it is far from correct to
think that all properties are relative.

23 The Special Theory of Relativity

We have already seen one property that is not relative – the speed of light.
According to the principle of the constancy of the velocity of light, the speed of
light (in a vacuum) will always be measured to be the same, regardless of what
point of view is involved. According to relativity, then, the speed of light is not
relative. Properties that are the same from every point of view, such as the speed of
light in relativity theory, are considered invariant properties.
Note that different theories often treat different properties as variant and
invariant. For example, length, time (that is, how much time has passed between
two events), and simultaneity (that is, whether two events are or are not
simultaneous) were considered invariant on the Newtonian view, but as we
saw earlier in this chapter, these properties are not invariant according to
relativity theory. On the other hand, the speed of light is invariant on relativity
theory, but was not considered invariant on the Newtonian view. (Recall the
Michelson–Morley experiment discussed in the previous chapter, which was
designed to detect differences in the speed of light. As noted in that chapter,
the expectation, given the usual Newtonian view on how light moved, was that the
speed of light should be different in different circumstances. In other words,
the speed of light was thought to be a variant property on the Newtonian view.)
Although the amount of time that has passed, and the distance between
locations, can differ from one point of view to another according to relativity,
Minkowski recognized that a certain property associated with a “combination” of
space and time was the same from every point of view. That is, the property that
Minkowski introduced, which is termed the spacetime interval, is invariant on
relativity theory. To understand the spacetime interval, we need to ﬁrst under­
stand the notion of spacetime. Although “spacetime” and the “spacetime contin­
uum” can sound like mysterious entities, the basic idea is quite straightforward.
To understand the notion of spacetime, let’s begin by considering a typical twodimensional Cartesian coordinate system, of the sort illustrated in Figure 23.4.
We often (though not always) understand the horizontal and vertical axes as
representing positions in space. For example, suppose we take the point (0,0) to be
the center of a soccer ﬁeld. If we take our units to be meters, then a point such as
Figure 23.4 A typical Cartesian coordinate
system.

207

208

Recent Developments

(8,11) might represent a point that is 8 meters from the center of the ﬁeld in one
direction, let’s say in the direction of one of the sidelines, and 11 meters from the
center of the ﬁeld in another direction, say in the direction of one of the goals.
Next, suppose we again let the horizontal axis represent positions in space. In
particular, suppose this axis again represents distance in the direction of the
sidelines on our soccer ﬁeld. But now instead of letting the vertical axis represent
another spatial dimension, we will instead let the vertical axis represent time.
Suppose we have an individual who is at the center of the ﬁeld, and who at time 0
begins walking toward the sideline, moving 2 meters every second. Then (0,0),
(2,1), (4,2), (6,3), and so on, would represent this individual’s position, at 1-second
intervals, in space and time. That is, (0,0) represents that the individual is
located at point 0 at time 0, (2,1) represents that the individual is located at
point 2 at time 1, at point 4 at time 2, and so on.
At bottom, this is all there is to the notion of spacetime. It is simply a way of
representing positions in both space and time. A situation such as we just
described, in which there is only one spatial dimension along with time, would
be a two-dimensional spacetime. Including all three spatial dimensions, along
with time, results in a four-dimensional spacetime, where any point in this
spacetime can be represented as a 4-tuple (x, y, z, t), where x, y, and z represent the
usual three spatial dimensions, and t represents time.
Now that we have a handle on the notion of spacetime, let’s turn to the notion of
the spacetime interval. Consider again the situation with Sara and Joe pictured in
Figure 23.1. We can easily envision a spacetime coordinate system associated with
Joe’s point of view. We might, say, let the center of Joe’s ﬁrst clock be the origin of the
spatial components of this coordinate system, and we can let the moment that Joe’s
ﬁrst clock reads 0.00 be time 0. We can then say that the event of Joe’s ﬁrst clock
reading 0.00 occurred at spacetime coordinate (0,0,0,0). Suppose we take the x-axis
to be the direction of travel, and take the spatial units of the coordinate system to be
kilometers. If, as usual, we assume that (from Joe’s point of view) Joe’s two clocks are
synchronized, then we can say that the event of Joe’s second clock reading 0.00
occurred at spacetime coordinate (1000,0,0,0).
Now let’s consider the spacetime interval between these two events, that is, the
spacetime interval between the event of Joe’s ﬁrst clock reading 0.00 and the event
of Joe’s second clock reading 0.00. We can see that the space interval along the xaxis is 1000, the space intervals along the y- and z- axes are 0, and the time interval
is 0. If we let Δx, Δy, and Δz represent the space intervals between the two events
along the x-, y-, and z- axes, and let Δt represent the time interval between the two
events, then the spacetime interval s between the two events is given by the
following formula:
s2  c2 Δt 2

Δx2

Δy2

Δz2

So in this case, the spacetime interval between the two events is
pﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ
c2 02 10002 02 02
(Incidentally, in this case the result will be an imaginary number, that is, one
involving the square root of negative 1. Imaginary numbers are not as widely

23 The Special Theory of Relativity

known as, say, the natural numbers or the rational numbers, but imaginary
numbers are a well-understood and commonly used type of number in a variety of
ﬁelds, most notably mathematics and some branches of physics.)
In a sense, the spacetime interval is a sort of “distance” between events. Not the
distance the events are separated by in space, nor the distance they are separated
by in time, but rather the “distance” they are separated by using a measure
involving both space and time.
As mentioned above, the spacetime interval is an invariant property in
relativity. To illustrate this, let’s bring Sara back into the picture. We saw above
that we could specify a spacetime coordinate system associated with Joe’s point of
view. We can, of course, also associate a spacetime coordinate system with Sara’s
point of view. For convenience (we need not do this, but it makes the discussion
easier), we will assume that the origin point of Sara’s coordinate system is the
same as the origin point of Joe’s coordinate system. Note that, from Joe’s point of
view, the coordinate system associated with Sara is a moving coordinate system.
And since the coordinate system is moving, we know from our discussion earlier
in this chapter that times, distances, and simultaneity will be affected.
For example, we saw above that in Joe’s coordinate system, the event of his ﬁrst
clock reading 0.00, and the event of his second clock reading 0.00, occurred at
coordinates (0,0,0,0) and (1000,0,0,0) respectively. But in Sara’s coordinate
system, those events will not occur 1000 kilometers apart, nor will they be
simultaneous. In general, events will not have the same spacetime coordinates in
Sara’s coordinate system as they have in Joe’s.
However, there are straightforward equations, known as the Lorentz transfor­
mations, that allow one to transform coordinates from a stationary spacetime
coordinate system into a moving spacetime coordinate system. (I do not give
these equations in the body of this chapter, but if you are curious, they can be
found in the Chapter Notes at the end of the book. Also, here, as always in this
chapter, it is assumed that the coordinate systems are moving relative to one
another in a straight line with uniform motion.) When the coordinates (0,0,0,0)
and (1000,0,0,0) from Joe’s coordinate system are translated, using the Lorentz
transformations, into the corresponding coordinates in Sara’s coordinate system,
they come out to be (0,0,0,0) and (1250,0,0, 0.0025) respectively.
If we use the formula above to calculate the spacetime interval s between these
events in Sara’s coordinate system, we will see that the value comes out the same
as it did in Joe’s coordinate system. And in general, the spacetime interval between
any events in coordinate systems that are in straight-line, uniform motion relative
to one another will be the same. So although the space interval and time interval
between events will vary from coordinate system to coordinate system, the
spacetime interval will not. Again, this is just to say that the spacetime interval
is an invariant property in relativity theory.
In this section, then, we have gotten an idea of the notion of spacetime, and we
have seen one of the more important invariant properties associated with
spacetime, that being the spacetime interval. As a ﬁnal note in this section, it
is worth noting that this “geometrical” approach, that is, thinking of points of view
as four-dimensional spacetime coordinate systems that are moving relative to one
another, and using the Lorentz transformations to translate coordinates from one

209

210

Recent Developments

coordinate system to another, is a common way of approaching relativity. For
many purposes, this geometrical approach provides a convenient way of picturing
issues involving relativity. And of course, on this geometrical approach, one ﬁnds
the same relativistic effects we discussed earlier, that is, time dilation, space
contraction, and the relativity of simultaneity.

Concluding Remarks
In this chapter, we have looked at Einstein’s special theory of relativity, and we
have seen that the theory has nontrivial implications for our common-sense
beliefs about space, time, and simultaneity. With Einstein’s special theory of
relativity, we can see that some of the beliefs we have long had, and that most of us
took as obvious empirical facts, turn out to be mistaken. In this way, relativity
theory forces us to rethink some of these long-held beliefs. In the next chapter, we
look brieﬂy at the general theory of relativity, noting that it too has interesting
implications for common views, especially common views on the nature of
gravity.

211

24
The General Theory of Relativity
Between 1907 and 1916, Einstein spent considerable time and effort developing
the general theory of relativity. As mentioned earlier, general relativity is
substantially more complex than special relativity. Our main goal in this chapter
is to get a sense of the main aspects of general relativity, and to see some of the
main implications of the theory. We will begin by looking at the basic principles
on which general relativity is based.

Basic Principles
In the previous chapter, we saw that the special theory of relativity is based on two
basic principles, the principle of relativity and the principle of the constancy of the
velocity of light. In an important sense, the general theory of relativity is likewise
based on two basic principles, generally termed the principle of general covariance
and the principle of equivalence.
The principle of general covariance is usually summarized by the statement
that the laws of physics are the same in all reference frames. This principle is most
easily explained by comparing it with the principle of relativity from the previous
chapter. Recall that the principle of relativity states that, if two labs differ only in
that they are moving relative to one another in a straight line with uniform
velocity, then any experiment carried out in one lab will have an identical result if
carried out in the other lab. So, for example, in Figure 23.1, if the only difference
between Sara and Joe is that they are in straight-line, uniform motion relative to
one another, then if they carry out identical experiments, they will get identical
results.
In the chapter on special relativity I tended to speak of “points of view,” for
example, describing a situation ﬁrst from Joe’s point of view and then from Sara’s.
Such points of view are often referred to as frames of reference, or just reference
frames. Reference frames such as those of Sara and Joe, which involve only
uniform, straight-line motion, are referred to as inertial reference frames (or just
inertial frames). Using the notion of an inertial reference frame, the principle of
relativity can be phrased more succinctly to say that in all inertial reference
frames, identical experiments will have identical results. Or to say the same thing
Worldviews: An Introduction to the History and Philosophy of Science, Third Edition.
Richard DeWitt.
 2018 Richard DeWitt. Published 2018 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.

212

Recent Developments

in another way, the laws of physics are the same in all inertial reference frames.
And indeed, the principle of relativity is often phrased in just this way.
Note that, when rephrased this way, we can see that the principle of general
covariance is a generalized version of the principle of relativity (in fact, although
the principle is now usually referred to as the principle of general covariance,
Einstein often referred to it as the “general principle of relativity”). So whereas the
principle of relativity says that, in essence, the laws of physics are the same in all
inertial frames, the principle of general covariance says that the laws of physics are
the same in all reference frames, regardless of how those reference frames might
be moving relative to one another. This is exactly the sense in which general
relativity is a general theory. Whereas special relativity applies so long as special
circumstances are met – in particular, so long as we are dealing with inertial
reference frames – general relativity removes this restriction, and applies to all
frames of reference.
Let us now turn to the other basic principle of general relativity, that being the
principle of equivalence. This principle states that effects due to acceleration, on
the one hand, and effects due to gravity, on the other, are indistinguishable. There
is probably no better way to illustrate this principle than by borrowing Einstein’s
usual example, which is the following.
Suppose you are in an enclosed room, about the size and shape of an elevator,
which is such that you have no way to see outside the room. In the ﬁrst case,
suppose (unknown to you) that this “elevator” is sitting on the surface of the Earth,
so that you are feeling the effects of the gravitational ﬁeld of the Earth. What
effects will you feel? Most notably, you will notice effects such as the feeling of
being “pulled” toward the ﬂoor of the elevator, and you will notice also that
dropped objects accelerate toward the ﬂoor at 9.8 meters per second per second.
Now suppose (again unknown to you) that you and the elevator are in a more or
less empty region of space (and so not being inﬂuenced in any substantial way by
any gravitational ﬁelds), but that the elevator is being accelerated “up” (that is, in
the direction of a line drawn from the ﬂoor through the ceiling) at 9.8 meters per
second per second. What effects will you feel as a result of this acceleration? Once
again, you will notice the feeling of being “pulled” toward the ﬂoor of the elevator,
and you will notice that dropped objects accelerate toward the ﬂoor at 9.8 meters
per second per second.
The key point to note is that the effects due to gravity, in the ﬁrst case, and the
effects due to acceleration, in the second case, are indistinguishable from one
another. This close relationship between effects due to gravity and effects due to
acceleration was recognized as far back as Newton. Nonetheless, in Newton’s
physics they are treated as separate phenomena, and the close relationship between
the two appears to be essentially a coincidence. But with general relativity, the
principle of equivalence is stating that, in essence, there is no difference between the
two types of effects – the effects are indistinguishable from one another.
In summary, then, the general theory of relativity, as with the special theory of
relativity, is in an important sense based on two basic principles. With this in
mind, we will move on to a brief discussion of the equations that are at the heart of
general relativity, as well as look at some of the conﬁrming evidence for that
theory.

24 The General Theory of Relativity

The Einstein Field Equations and Predictions
of General Relativity
As noted, the general theory of relativity is, in an important sense, based on the
principle of general covariance and the principle of equivalence. We saw in the
previous chapter that the special theory of relativity is likewise based on two basic
principles. And as we saw in that chapter, the mathematical “picture” that
accompanies the two basic principles of special relativity involved some surpris­
ing effects for length, time, and simultaneity. Also as noted, the mathematical
picture that accompanies the basic principles was not terribly difﬁcult to work out
(in that chapter we did not actually derive the effects on length, time, and
simultaneity from the basic principles, but I noted that the derivation could be
accomplished with no more than high-school algebra, and although the deriva­
tion is not trivial, neither is it particularly difﬁcult).
The situation with general relativity is quite different. Although the two basic
principles of general relativity are not difﬁcult to state, it turned out to be quite
difﬁcult to formulate the desired mathematical equations that would respect these
principles. Einstein spent several years working on these equations, with many
preliminary results that later had to be taken back or substantially revised. In
short, the mathematical account required by the two basic principles took an
enormous amount of work to ﬁgure out. And the equations themselves, unlike the
equations that accompany special relativity, are quite complex.
By 1916, however, Einstein had worked out the equations he sought, and he
presented them in a 1916 paper titled “The Foundation of the General Theory of
Relativity.” These equations are now referred to as Einstein’s ﬁeld equations, and
they are the mathematical core of general relativity. The general idea is that
solutions to these equations indicate how space, time, and matter inﬂuence each
other. For example, one solution shows the way space and time should be affected
by the presence of a body such as our sun. Another solution shows how space and
time should be affected when a massive star collapses to form an extraordinarily
dense remnant – what is generally termed a black hole. (As a brief aside, black
holes were long thought to be mere theoretical possibilities, but not actually
existing objects. This changed in the second half of the twentieth century,
however, and it is now widely accepted that black holes are not at all uncommon,
and likely are present at the center of most galaxies.)
As with special relativity, general relativity also makes some unique predictions.
I will mention a few of these very brieﬂy, and then spend a bit more time on
another consequence of general relativity, that being the curvature of spacetime.
At the beginning of Chapter 8, we brieﬂy discussed the fact that for some
decades, some peculiarities about the orbit of Mercury had been observed. Recall
that planets orbit in ellipses, and this is all in keeping with the predictions of
Newtonian science. Now envision the point of Mercury’s orbit that is closest to
the sun. This closest point of an orbit is termed the perihelion, and what had been
observed, for some decades during the mid- to late 1800s, was that the perihelion
of Mercury moved slightly during each orbit, such that the point was very, very
slowly rotating around the sun. The amount the perihelion of Mercury moved

213

214

Recent Developments

each year was extremely small, but it was measurable, and moreover it was not
what one would expect from the Newtonian account of planetary motion. In his
1916 paper, however, Einstein showed that his equations predicted that the
perihelion of Mercury should advance each year, and the amount predicted by
Einstein’s general relativity was just the amount that had been observed. This,
then, was a reasonably straightforward example of conﬁrming evidence for
general relativity, of the sort discussed in Chapter 4.
Likewise, in the 1916 paper Einstein showed that if general relativity is correct,
then the wavelength of light moving away from a strong gravitational ﬁeld should
be shifted toward the red end of the spectrum. This effect is known as gravita­
tional redshift. Since a star has a strong gravitational ﬁeld, light leaving a star, for
example, light from the sun, should be redshifted. The redshift predicted by
general relativity is not easy to test, but in the experiments that have been
conducted, the observed redshift matches well with the predications of general
relativity, again providing conﬁrming evidence for the theory. Light moving away
from a less massive body such as the Earth should also be redshifted, and although
the predicted effect is quite small, this too has been measured, and it also ﬁts well
with the predictions of general relativity.
In the previous chapter, in discussing the special theory of relativity, we saw
that motion affects both space and time. In general relativity, motion also has
similar effects on space and time. Moreover, gravitational forces (or equivalently,
the effects of acceleration and deceleration) also affect space and time. For
example, in the presence of a strong gravitational ﬁeld, or, equivalently, in an
accelerating reference frame, time moves more slowly. Importantly, unlike special
relativity, there is a sense in which these effects are not symmetric. For example,
suppose Joe remains on the surface of the Earth, and Sara leaves on a high-speed
space mission. Suppose she accelerates for a period of time, then decelerates when
she reaches her destination, turns around and accelerates again for a period of
time as she begins the return trip to Earth, and then again decelerates when she
approaches the Earth. During her trip she will experience the effects of accelera­
tion and deceleration, and these will be effects not experienced by Joe. In this
situation, general relativity predicts that less time will have passed for Sara than
for Joe, and both Sara and Joe will agree on this.
Given the extremely accurate timekeeping devices we currently have, these
sorts of effects on time are not difﬁcult to test, and the effects predicted by general
relativity have been well conﬁrmed. Even the very small gravitational differences
between the ground ﬂoor and top ﬂoor of a tall building should, according to
general relativity, produce small differences (very small) in the rate that time
passes on the top ﬂoor of a tall building compared to the rate that time passes on
the ground ﬂoor. Even these very small differences in time have been measured,
and are what general relativity predicts. In short, there are numerous cases of
conﬁrming evidence for general relativity.
As mentioned at the beginning of Chapter 4, the observed bending of starlight
during the solar eclipse of 1919 provided some early and well-publicized
conﬁrming evidence for general relativity. This observation takes us into one
of the more interesting aspects of general relativity, that being the curvature of
spacetime. Hence this is worth going through a bit more slowly.

24 The General Theory of Relativity

Figure 24.1 Magnetic ﬁeld lines.

To get a sense of the curvature of spacetime predicted by general relativity, it
might help to begin with an example that has little to do with relativity, but that
will provide a convenient example with which to contrast relativity. Suppose we
have a bar magnet, and suppose we put a piece of paper over the magnet. We then
put iron ﬁlings on the paper, and shake the ﬁlings around a bit. In this situation,
the iron ﬁlings will arrange themselves in a particular pattern, with this pattern
reﬂecting the magnetic ﬁeld surrounding the magnet. The magnetic ﬁeld itself is
often pictured as it appears in Figure 24.1. The lines in such a picture are
commonly referred to as ﬁeld lines, and in this case, they represent the strength
and direction of the magnetic ﬁeld. For example, where the ﬁeld is stronger, the
lines are closer together; where weaker, the lines are further apart. (For ease of
presentation, Figure 24.1 is somewhat simpler than most diagrams of ﬁeld lines.
Typically, such diagrams will include more detailed ﬁeld lines, as well as arrows
indicating the direction of the forces involved.) Now, notice an important feature
of the diagram. The ﬁeld lines suggest forces that exist in space and, presumably,
also in time. That is, the ﬁeld lines suggest that, say, in a particular region of space
near the magnet, an iron ﬁling would be under the inﬂuence of certain magnetic
forces and, perhaps, would tend to move a particular way through space and time.
In short, space and time typically provide a backdrop for such ﬁeld lines, or to put
it another way, such ﬁeld lines appear to exist in space and time.
Now consider Figure 24.2, the sort of diagram often found in discussions of
general relativity. On the face of it, the ﬁeld lines in such a diagram look similar to
the ﬁeld lines in Figure 24.1. But there is a crucial difference: these ﬁeld lines do
not represent a ﬁeld in space and time; rather, the ﬁeld lines represent a curvature
of spacetime itself. (Incidentally, as we saw in the previous chapter, spacetime is a
four-dimensional continuum involving the usual three spatial dimensions
together with a dimension representing time. Figures such as 24.2 typically
represent two-dimensional “slices” through the four-dimensional spacetime.)

215

216

Recent Developments

Figure 24.2 Typical ﬁeld lines in general relativity.

According to general relativity, the presence of a massive body results in a
curvature of spacetime. The ﬁeld lines in diagrams like Figure 24.2 represent such
a curvature of spacetime in the presence of a body such as the sun. In this sort of
diagram, if an object travels along the surface of the “slice,” the shortest path
between two points will be a curved line (such a shortest path is termed a
geodesic). Since light will take the shortest path, light passing near a large body
such as the sun should follow what appears to be a curved path. In short, if general
relativity is correct, a massive body such as the sun should result in a curvature of
spacetime, and hence we should be able to observe the bending of starlight as it
passes near a body such as the sun.
In his paper of 1916, Einstein provided the amounts by which one would expect
starlight to be bent near the sun. And as discussed in Chapter 4, the bending of
starlight observed during the 1919 solar eclipse was in good agreement with
Einstein’s predictions, again providing important conﬁrming evidence for general
relativity.
Concerning spacetime, and the curvature of spacetime, one ﬁnal and quite
recent experimental conﬁrmation of relativity is worth mentioning. Early on,
Einstein and others recognized that general relativity predicts the existence of
what are termed gravitational waves. Gravitational waves are, in a sense, ripples in
spacetime. Consider again ﬁgure 24.2, and imagine a sort of ripple moving
through this slice of spacetime. That would be a gravitational wave. Whereas
ripples in the surface of a pond radiate out from where perhaps a pebble was
dropped, gravitational waves would radiate out from the source of an enormous
release of energy (for example, energy released from a supernova, which is an
explosion of a massive star near the end of the life of the star).
Gravitational waves passing through our region of spacetime should not be at
all uncommon; however, detecting them had proved to be extremely difﬁcult.
Much as a ripple in a pond will diminish in size the further it is from its source, so
too with gravitational waves. Gravitational waves may well be quite substantial at
their origin. But after traveling through spacetime at the speed of light over
enormous distances for millions or billions of years, they will be quite difﬁcult to
detect by the time they reach the Earth. However, in late 2015, and just in time for
the hundredth anniversary of the publication of Einstein’s paper on general
relativity, a pair of specially designed detectors independently recorded the
passage of a gravitational wave. The wave appears to have originated a little
over a billion years ago, and stemmed from the collision of two large black holes
over a billion light years away. Although indirect evidence of gravitational waves

24 The General Theory of Relativity

dates back several decades, this was the ﬁrst direct (and dramatic) conﬁrmation of
this prediction of general relativity. (Incidentally, the detection of the waves was
important in itself, but it also looks quite promising in terms of ushering in a new
way of gathering information about deep space, via what is now sometimes
referred to as “gravitational wave astronomy.”)
In summary, general relativity makes a number of unusual predictions, such as
those discussed above. Observations have been in agreement with these predic­
tions, and, overall, general relativity is widely considered to be a well-conﬁrmed
theory.

Philosophical Reﬂections: General Relativity and Gravity
Before closing this chapter, one ﬁnal issue closely tied to general relativity is worth
discussing, that being the account of gravity provided by this theory. To say that
the account of gravity is an issue tied to general relativity is perhaps a bit of an
understatement, in that general relativity is often construed primarily as a theory
of gravity.
As we saw in the discussion above, light travels along the shortest path
available. In general relativity, bodies not under the inﬂuence of any forces
will also move along the shortest path, that is, bodies generally move on geodesics.
Importantly – and this is a crucial difference from the way gravity is typically
conceived of on the Newtonian account – a body such as a planet does not move
as it does because of any attractive force. Mars, for example, does not move in an
elliptical orbit around the sun because of anything to do with a mutually attractive
force – a gravitational force – between Mars and the sun. Rather, Mars, like any
body in motion, moves in a straight line.
But a “straight line” in a curved space is a geodesic. As we have seen, according
to general relativity, a body such as the sun will result in a curvature of spacetime.
And according to the equations of general relativity, this curvature is such that the
geodesic on which Mars should move would be what appears to be an ellipse
about the sun. In other words, on general relativity, there is no attractive “force”
between bodies such as Mars and the sun. Rather, Mars is simply moving in a
straight line, but due to the curvature of spacetime, that “straight line” appears to
be an ellipse about the sun.
Note that this is a very different treatment of gravity on general relativity, as
opposed to the treatment gravity receives in Newtonian science. On the Newto­
nian picture, gravity is typically taken as an attractive force between objects. As we
noted at the end of Chapter 20, such a force, if taken with a realist attitude, seems
to be a case of action at a distance. And as discussed in Chapter 20, this suggestion
of action at a distance was troublesome for Newton, and as a result, he often
professed to take an instrumentalist attitude toward gravity.
In spite of Newton’s own claims on the matter, most people raised in the
Newtonian worldview tend to take a realist attitude toward gravity. To use an
example we saw earlier, if I drop a pen, and ask “Why did the pen fall?” the
standard response will be that it fell because of the force of gravity. And if asked
whether this force is real, the usual response is that of course it is. That is, people

217

218

Recent Developments

by and large tend to think of gravity as a really existing, attractive force between
objects. In short, gravity is typically taken, in the Newtonian view, with a realist
attitude.
But now note an interesting consequence of the treatment of gravity on general
relativity. As noted above, general relativity is a well-conﬁrmed theory. And if we
take general relativity with a realist attitude, it essentially forces us to adopt an
instrumentalist attitude toward the Newtonian concept of gravity. That is, if
objects fall toward the Earth or planets move about the sun in elliptical orbits
because of the curvature of spacetime and not because of any attractive forces
between objects, then talk of gravity as an attractive force is at best a convenient,
but not literally correct, way of speaking.
In summary, general relativity is a well-conﬁrmed theory. Notably, with respect
to prediction and explanation (of the movement of the perihelion of Mercury, or
the bending of starlight, to name just two), general relativity does a better job than
Newtonian theory. Newtonian theory is still a very useful theory, but if we want to
consider which theory more accurately accounts for the known data, there is not
much question that relativity does the better job.
As a consequence of this, if we are inclined to take a realist attitude toward
theories in physics, then we ought to be realists about relativity theory and
instrumentalists about Newtonian theory (Newtonian physics is, after all, still
useful, although not, strictly speaking, a correct picture). But note that this forces
us to adopt an instrumentalist attitude toward the notion of gravity as an
attractive force – in other words, general relativity forces us to reassess an
attitude (that is, a realist conception of gravity as an attractive force) that most
tend to take for granted. In short, general relativity, as with special relativity,
forces us to rethink some commonly held views.

Concluding Remarks
In this and the previous chapter, we have seen that the special and general theories
of relativity have interesting implications for beliefs most of us have long held as
basic, commonsensical beliefs. These include beliefs about length, time intervals,
and simultaneity, as well as common beliefs about the nature of gravity. In
particular, with respect to gravity, general relativity forces us to adopt an
instrumentalist attitude toward the usual notion of gravity as an attractive force.
Recall that in the 1600s, new discoveries forced changes in the usual views of
the world. We are likewise seeing that new discoveries are forcing us to reassess
some of our common beliefs about the world. In the ﬁnal chapter, we will return
to a discussion of some of the implications Einstein’s theory of relativity has for
the Newtonian worldview. First, though, we explore the other major branch of
twentieth-century physics, that being quantum theory.

219

25
Philosophical Interlude: Are (Some) Scientiﬁc
Theories Incommensurable?
My main goal in this chapter is to provide an overview of issues involving
incommensurability, and to provide enough of an understanding of the issues to
serve as a background for further explorations. The term is probably not widely
known outside of the history and philosophy of science, so our ﬁrst task, in a
moment, will be to take a preliminary look of what it means to say that certain
scientiﬁc terms or theories are incommensurable.
Issues surrounding incommensurability, as with other issues we have explored,
are controversial. There is a rich body of writings exploring and arguing for
various positions, and as with a number of controversial topics concerning the
history and philosophy of science, no consensus has emerged. As usual, for those
wishing to explore this issue further, the Chapter Notes at the end of the book
provide additional reading. We will begin with a section aimed at communicating
the general issues involved in incommensurability, and then move on to discuss
some of the more detailed and complicating issues surrounding this topic.

Preliminary Considerations
To get a ﬁrst approximation of the way “incommensurability” is currently used,
consider the term “mass,” which plays a prominent role within both the older
Newtonian physics and the newer relativistic physics. Some have argued that,
within each theory, the term inhabits such conceptually different environments,
and plays such conceptually different roles, as to make it impossible to accurately
capture the Newtonian meaning of the term within the framework of relativistic
physics. If this argument is correct – that is, if there is no adequate, acceptably
accurate way to translate the Newtonian use and meaning of the term using the
terms and concepts of our newer physics – then we would say that “mass” as used
in Newtonian physics is incommensurable with “mass” as used in the newer
relativistic physics. If one extends this view to the theories themselves, we would
say that Newtonian physics and relativistic physics are incommensurable, that is,
there is no way to adequately capture – to accurately express, or accurately
understand – the old theory in terms of the newer theory.

Worldviews: An Introduction to the History and Philosophy of Science, Third Edition.
Richard DeWitt.
 2018 Richard DeWitt. Published 2018 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.

220

Recent Developments

Why might one think terms from different scientiﬁc theories, or even the entire
theories themselves, might be incommensurable? In the remainder of this section
I will try to provide a preliminary answer to this question. Then in the next section
we will turn to different ways incommensurability has been argued to exist, as well
as ﬂeshing out some of the complexities surrounding incommensurability.
Let’s begin with what seems like one of our most everyday, simple, and
straightforward concepts, that of weight, and consider how the term is used
within the Aristotelian tradition and the Newtonian tradition. It is worth noting
that although modern physics – in particular relativistic physics – has implica­
tions for our understanding of weight, for most practical purposes we still today
use what is essentially the Newtonian concept. I’ll thus often refer to this as the
modern concept of weight.
At least since Galileo, Aristotle has been roundly criticized for holding the
(presumably badly mistaken) belief that the speed at which an object falls is
proportional to its weight, and that in particular an object with twice as much
weight as another will fall twice as fast. The following sort of lab exercise, which
presumably shows Aristotle’s views on falling bodies to be mistaken, can be found
in numerous physics texts, lab manuals, history of science books, and the like.
First, one is directed to weigh two objects, and determine that one weighs twice as
much as the other. The objects are then dropped at the same time from the same
height, and it is presumably established that Aristotle is not merely wrong, but
badly wrong.
The view that Aristotle held the “twice the weight, twice as fast” belief, and that
this belief is badly mistaken, is quite widespread. But if we look more closely at this
issue, we can quickly see that the situation is not so simple. And we can quickly see
that the concept of weight as it functions within Aristotle’s physics has striking
differences from the concept of weight as it functions in modern physics.
Following along with this lab exercise, suppose we weigh the two objects with a
balance scale – such balance scales were common in Aristotle’s time as well – and
we determine that the one object weighs twice as much as the other. So far, so
good. Aristotle would have agreed that, in the context of putting these two objects
on a balance scale and determining their weight, the one object would have twice
the weight of the other. Notably, and this will be important shortly, Aristotle
would not have deﬁned weight in terms of an object’s inﬂuence on a balance scale
(nor do we deﬁne weight that way in modern physics), but he would have accepted
that an object’s inﬂuence on a scale does reﬂect something about its weight.
Now we move to the next step in the lab exercise, where we remove the objects
from the scale, and drop them from the same height. And we note that the one
object – the one that presumably has twice the weight – most assuredly does not
fall twice as fast. We thus conclude that Aristotle’s “twice the weight, twice as fast”
view was mistaken.
But there is a crucial presupposition in this argument. You may wish to pause
for a moment to see if you can identify that presupposition.
Ready? In order to draw the conclusion, it is necessary to presuppose that the
objects continue to have the same weight throughout the experiment. Or to put it
slightly differently, we have to presuppose, having determined in the context of
weighing the objects on a scale that one has twice the weight, that when we move

25 Are (Some) Scientiﬁc Theories Incommensurable?

the objects to a new context – of being dropped – they continue to have the same
weight as they did when on the scale.
Within modern physics, this presupposition is unproblematic. There are some
extremely minor effects on weight stemming from relativistic physics, but for all
practical purposes it is unproblematic, within the context of modern physics, to
view these objects as having the same weight throughout the scenario of the lab
experiment. (By the way, it is sometimes thought, in the context of modern
science, that an object in free fall is weightless. But generally speaking, that’s a
mistake. It is correct that an object in free fall will act as if it is weightless, and this
is sometimes described as saying an object in free fall has no “apparent weight.”
But the actual weight of an object, as deﬁned within modern physics, for all
practical purposes remains the same whether it is on a scale or falling.)
But would Aristotle agree that the objects have the same weight throughout
this lab scenario? The answer is straightforward: no. In the Aristotelian scientiﬁc
tradition, context is critical, and the context of an object resting in the pan of a
balance scale and the context of that object falling are two different contexts. And
in the Aristotelian view, that context makes a difference to the object’s weight.
Consider the fact that a falling object will inﬂuence a balance scale in a
substantially different way than that object will inﬂuence the scale when merely
resting in the pan of the scale. We understand this difference in terms of
Newtonian concepts such as force, mass, and acceleration. But within the
Aristotelian tradition, there are no concepts that play roles anything like the
roles these concepts play within Newtonian physics. In contrast, Aristotle would
have understood the difference in the way an object affects a balance scale when
falling versus when resting in the pan as reﬂecting that the object’s weight in the
one context is different than in the other.
None of what we’ve just discussed demonstrates conclusively that Aristotle’s
concept of weight and the modern concept of weight are incommensurable. We
will explore this further in the next section. But although we have only scratched
the surface of this example involving weight, we can already draw some general
conclusions. First, we have to be careful about imposing presuppositions that are
appropriate and natural in the context of our science onto a context involving a
different scientiﬁc tradition. And as a close corollary, we can also see that it can be
a mistake – a substantial mistake – to impose modern understandings of a term or
concept on the way that term or concept is used and understood in a different
scientiﬁc tradition.
Considerations such as these, as well as other considerations, have led some to
conclude that at least some terms and concepts from different scientiﬁc theories,
and perhaps the entire theories themselves, are genuinely incommensurable.
With this in mind, let’s move on to explore incommensurability more deeply.

Exploring Incommensurability
Historically, the concept of incommensurability dates back to the ancient Greek
mathematicians, who came to understand the concept primarily in terms of
whether two lengths could be, roughly, “co-measured.” Consider two rods, one 4

221

222

Recent Developments

meters in length and the other 6 meters. A 2-meter measuring stick will divide
evenly into each of those two rods, and thus the lengths of those two rods are co­
measurable in the sense that there is a third length that divides evenly into them.
Being co-measurable in this sense, lengths of 4 meters and 6 meters would be
commensurable lengths. (Equivalently, one could deﬁne this sort of commensu­
rability in terms of the ratio of the lengths, which in this example is 2/3. We could
say that two lengths are commensurable if the ratio of their lengths can be
expressed as a rational number, that is, a number of the form a/b, where a and b
are integers.)
In contrast, and to use an example that played an important role in the
development of ancient Greek mathematics, consider a square and the diagonal of
that square. Suppose the sides of the square are 1 meter in length. Then
p we know
from the Pythagorean theorem that the diagonal of the square will be 2. It is not
difﬁcult to give a proof (as far as we know, the ancient Greek mathematicians were
the ﬁrst to do so)pthat there is no length, no measuring
stick, that will divide evenly
p
into both 1 and 2, and thus lengths of 1 and 2 are incommensurable lengths.
(Incidentally, the proof that these lengths are incommensurable led to the
important discovery that in addition to integers and rational numbers, there
must also be irrational numbers. If you are interested in the history of mathe­
matics, the Chapter Notes provide references to enable you to pursue this
further.)
In the late nineteenth and early twentieth century, the mathematical concept of
incommensurability began to be used somewhat more metaphorically, for
example in the works of Pierre Duhem (1861–1916; we ﬁrst explored some of
Duhem’s contributions in Chapter 5) in areas concerning the history and
philosophy of science. These nonmathematical uses of the term remained rooted
in the idea of non-co-measurability, but the concept became more broadly
conceived, more along the lines of “co-deﬁnability” or “co-understandability.”
Later in the twentieth century, the concept of incommensurability began to
play an increasingly prominent role. This is due primarily to the work of Thomas
Kuhn (1922–96), on the one hand, and Paul Feyerabend (1924–94) on the other.
From the 1960s and throughout the remainder of their careers, Kuhn and
Feyerabend explored in some depth various ways incommensurability could
be understood, and various ways incommensurability might affect our under­
standing of science. While not attempting to characterize in detail the nuanced
views of Kuhn, Feyerabend, and others – doing so would be beyond the scope of
this chapter – we can certainly explore some of the ways incommensurability has
been argued to arise when considering different scientiﬁc traditions.
Let’s continue to use traditions we have explored earlier in this book, namely,
the different scientiﬁc traditions of Aristotelian physics and Newtonian physics.
The next three subsections focus on different though overlapping ways that
certain features of these traditions have been argued to be incommensurable.
Terminological incommensurability
At least one aspect of what is often termed terminological incommensurability
was illustrated in the ﬁrst main section of this chapter. In that section we saw that

25 Are (Some) Scientiﬁc Theories Incommensurable?

understanding Aristotle’s concept of weight in terms of the Newtonian concept of
weight, which is frequently done, is a misunderstanding. Whatever Aristotle’s
concept of weight was, it was not the Newtonian concept. Nor, it seems, can the
Aristotelian concept of weight be translated into any sort of a straightforward
Newtonian equivalent. Part of the reason for this is that Newtonian concepts such
as mass, force and acceleration, which play a key role in the Newtonian concept of
weight, were concepts that had no counterparts in the context of Aristotelian
physics.
It is worth noting that the lack of such conceptual counterparts does not by
itself rule out being able to give at least a rough translation of terms from one
tradition to another. For example, if Aristotle had deﬁned weight in terms of the
way an object affects a balance scale, then, given that we understand the effect on a
balance scale in terms of force, mass, and acceleration, we probably could have
given a reasonably accurate characterization of Aristotle’s concept of weight in
modern terms.
But as noted, Aristotle did not deﬁne weight in this way. And when we look
further into passages that shed some light on how he did understand weight, we
quickly run into an issue central to terminological incommensurability, namely,
that terms and concepts within a scientiﬁc tradition are usually deﬁned or
characterized by reference to other terms and concepts within that same tradition.
Kuhn in particular argued that terms within a tradition can generally only be
understood by relations to other terms within that tradition. And those other terms
and concepts face the same issue, that is, those other terms and concepts generally
resist translation into the terms and concepts of a different tradition.
To get a clearer sense of Kuhn’s point, let’s continue with our example involving
Aristotle’s conception of weight. In the writings that have come down to us,
Aristotle never provides a deﬁnition or clear characterization of what he and his
followers understood weight to be. We can, though, piece together at least some
uncontroversial aspects of Aristotle’s understanding of weight.
First, it is clear that Aristotle sees weight as closely tied to his concept of
dunamis, which is generally translated as “potency” or “potentiality.” Potency is
one of the most central concepts within Aristotle’s natural science, but
unfortunately – and you can probably see this coming – there is no consensus
on how Aristotle’s concept of potency ought to be understood. In some broad
sense, the sort of matter an acorn is composed of, and the arrangement of that
matter, gives the acorn an internal, goal-directed potency, a potential, under the
right circumstances, to develop into a mature oak tree. And this captures some
aspects of the Aristotelian concept of potency.
But Aristotle’s understanding of potency, and the role potency plays in his
natural science, is more subtle and nuanced than the overly simplistic account just
given. There has been an enormous amount written, especially in recent years,
and a great deal of argumentation, and a good deal of sometimes quite funda­
mental disagreement, on how we ought to understand the more nuanced aspects
of Aristotle’s concept of potency. However, nothing close to a consensus has
emerged.
You can quickly see the problem. Aristotle viewed an object’s weight as closely
tied to aspects of that object’s potencies. But potency itself is not a term easily

223

224

Recent Developments

translatable into any modern concepts. And Aristotle’s understanding of potency
is in turn closely tied to other central Aristotelian concepts such as activity or
actuality, form, causes of motion, and much more. And, no big surprise, those
Aristotelian concepts likewise resist anything resembling straightforward trans­
lations into modern concepts.
Kuhn in particular argued at length that, to return to a metaphor used often in
this book, concepts from a scientiﬁc tradition, such as Aristotelian science, are
best viewed as existing within an interconnected web of concepts, or perhaps an
interconnected conceptual jigsaw puzzle. And thus, according to the adherents of
this sort of incommensurability, these sorts of concepts cannot be deﬁned or
adequately characterized within the framework of a different web of concepts,
such as that of modern science. Rather, if such concepts can be adequately
understood, it can only be done by understanding the role they play within the
context of the conceptual environment in which they live.
Methodological incommensurability
In addition to the sort of terminological incommensurability discussed in the
previous section, Kuhn and others have argued that there is a broader way in
which different scientiﬁc traditions tend to be incommensurable. This additional
type of incommensurability, it is argued, goes beyond a mere inability to
adequately translate terms from one tradition to another. Rather, and especially
in cases where one major tradition has replaced another (for example, in the way
Newtonian physics supplanted Aristotelian physics), the claim is that the meth­
odology in the new tradition is often radically different from the methodology of
the other.
A key idea behind this sort of incommensurability is that different scientiﬁc
traditions often disagree on fundamental issues regarding, broadly speaking, the
proper conduct of science. Different scientiﬁc traditions see fundamentally
different questions as being the key questions the science ought to address;
different traditions bring fundamentally different assumptions and presupposi­
tions to their work; different traditions have fundamentally different views on the
proper way scientiﬁc explanation ought to proceed; and more.
To illustrate some aspects of this sort of incommensurability, consider again
the Aristotelian and Newtonian traditions, and how they approach issues involv­
ing motion. Recall a central view of motion before the 1600s, that is, prior to the
emergence of Newtonian science. This was what we earlier (in Chapter 12)
termed the “pre-1600s view of motion,” namely, that an object in motion will
come to a halt, unless something keeps it moving. This view of motion would have
been a central (and at the time, empirically well-supported) view of motion during
the period when Aristotelian science was dominant.
Given this view, everything that moves must have a source of motion. And not
just a source that gets the motion started, but additionally, a source that is
continually active throughout the duration of the motion. To sum this up, within
the Aristotelian tradition, the following would have been a fundamental tenet, a
sort of guiding principle: Everything that moves is moved by something, and that
something has to be continually active throughout the duration of the motion.

25 Are (Some) Scientiﬁc Theories Incommensurable?

As a result of this fundamental tenet, within the Aristotelian tradition a central
question, and central research topic, was discovering the sources for a wide variety
of motions. For example, in On the Motion of Animals (as well as in many other
works), Aristotle explores in depth the source of the wide variety of movements
found in animals. Notably, Aristotle’s concept of motion is much broader than
ours, and includes, for example, growth, nourishment, embryological develop­
ment, the way an insult may cause one to redden with anger, internal bodily
changes arising from sensory input, and much more. All of these sorts of changes,
of motions, must have a source, something that is responsible for initiating the
movement and that is active throughout the duration of the motion. Thus,
discovering these sources of motion is a fundamentally important question for
Aristotle and the Aristotelian tradition.
A similar story holds for writings such as Aristotle’s On the Heavens (as well as
others), in which he spends considerable time exploring the perpetual source of
motion for the heavenly bodies. Likewise in the Physics (and elsewhere), consid­
erable time is spent on natural and forced motions, such as ﬁnding the initiating
and continuing source of motion responsible for a rock falling downward
(natural) or the initiating and continuing source of motion of a projectile in
ﬂight (forced). I could go on, but you get the picture. Because of the key tenet
noted above, within the Aristotelian tradition, ﬁnding the initial and continuing
sources, for an enormously wide variety of motions, is a recurring and funda­
mental question.
Now consider what happens once the Newtonian approach is accepted. Recall
that one of the fundamental tenets of Newtonian science is what we term the
principle of inertia – a body in motion remains in motion, unless acted upon by an
outside force. Once this fundamental principle becomes accepted, notice that a
huge swath of what would have been key, fundamental questions within the
Aristotelian tradition just go poof! Within the point of view of the new science,
these simply become nonquestions.
To use a concrete example, consider the motion of a heavenly body such as
Mars. Within the Aristotelian tradition, there had to be a continual source of
motion, some source that is active every microsecond of every day, that is
responsible for Mars’ continual motion. What could provide a continual and
(for Aristotle at least) eternal source of motion was an extraordinarily difﬁcult
question (we saw at least an overview of Aristotle’s answer in Chapter 12). And as
noted, Aristotle spent a considerable amount of writing addressing this question.
But now look at this from within the Newtonian tradition. Within this tradition,
the natural tendency of an object in motion is to remain in motion. So there is no
need to look for any active source of motion for the continual movement of Mars.
That is just what bodies in motion do – they remain in motion. No further
explanation is needed; there are no such questions to address. To put it slightly
differently, questions about the source of Mars’ motion, and the sources of a huge
variety of other types of motion, simply go away within the context of the new
science.
I could give many more examples of other topics that were of central
importance within the Aristotelian tradition that similarly simply disappear
within the context of the new Newtonian tradition. But again, I think you get

225

226

Recent Developments

the picture. Those who argue that this sort of methodological incommensurability
exists argue that the approaches, the key questions arising from the basic tenets of
traditions such as Aristotelian physics and Newtonian physics, are so radically
different as to make it impossible to adequately capture, to adequately under­
stand, the central issues of the one tradition within the context of the other
tradition. That is, the claim is that basic approaches, the basic questions, the basic
methodology, of the two traditions are incommensurable.
Different worlds incommensurability
The phrase I am using here, different worlds incommensurability, is not a
standard phrase. In fact, although this sort of incommensurability has been
widely discussed, there is no standard label for it. It is clear that this sort of
(alleged) incommensurability is difﬁcult to characterize precisely (Kuhn himself
said as much), and it is also clear that this sort of incommensurability is less widely
accepted than are the types discussed in the preceding two sections. Given this, I
will in this section attempt only to convey a ﬂavor of what advocates of this sort of
incommensurability have in mind.
Different worlds incommensurability revolves around the idea that scientists in
different traditions, such as the Aristotelian and Newtonian traditions, “see” the
world differently. The idea, or at least the idea behind the more interesting (and
more controversial) version of this type of incommensurability, is not that
scientists from different traditions interpret the world differently; rather, the
claim is that such scientists see the world differently. As Kuhn put it, scientists in
such different traditions “practice their trades in different worlds” (Kuhn 1962,
p. 150).
To get a better sense of this, let’s consider an oft-cited example that originates
with a classic experiment from about 1950. Most of us have experience, from an
early age, with ordinary playing cards, the sort of cards we might, say, play poker
with. Such cards have two red suits, hearts and diamonds, and two black suits,
clubs and spades. It is well established that most of us can correctly identify
speciﬁc playing cards even when a card is ﬂashed before us very brieﬂy, in as little
as 30 milliseconds (30/1000 of a second – a very brief time).
But what happens if subjects are exposed, without being told, to anomalous
playing cards, ones in which the usual red suits of hearts and diamonds are instead
black, and the usual black suits are red? The answer is that such cards are seen as
normal cards. For example, a black 7 of diamonds will reliably be seen as either a
normal (black) 7 of spades or as a normal (red) 7 of diamonds. The implication is
that, in this experiment, we see what we expect to see.
Kuhn and others use the results of this experiment as a sort of jumping off
point, suggesting that these results extend to much broader domains of “seeing.”
To again use a common example, suppose we have a small rock attached to a
string of, say, 1 meter in length, with the end of the string tied to a tree branch so
the rock can swing freely. Suppose we, while keeping the string taut, raise the rock
and then release it so that it swings freely.
Advocates of what I am calling different worlds incommensurability argue that
a scientist in the Aristotelian tradition will see in this case a natural object with an

25 Are (Some) Scientiﬁc Theories Incommensurable?

internal, goal-directed essential nature attempting to move toward its natural
place in the universe but with its motion inhibited by the string. In contrast, a
scientist in the Newtonian tradition will see a pendulum.
Again, and importantly, the claim is not that the Aristotelian scientist is
interpreting this in one way while the Newtonian scientist is interpreting it in
another way. Rather, the claim is that the Aristotelian is seeing one thing, the
Newtonian another.
The view extends beyond individual examples such as rocks attached to strings,
and applies, it is argued, much more broadly. As an Aristotelian, I would look out
my window right now – it is fall in New England – and see a world teeming with
goal-directed changes, changes originating from the trees and squirrels and deer
actualizing potencies that exist in them as a result of their essential, internal,
teleological natures. As a Newtonian, and one trained primarily in biology, I
would look out my window and see a world behaving in a push-pull, mechanistic
sort of way, with that behavior governed by external, quantitative physical,
chemical, biological principles – chemical changes in the chlorophyll of the
leaves that are governed by quantitative biochemical principles being the cause of
changing colors, and so on.
As noted earlier, this sort of broader incommensurability, with members of
different scientiﬁc traditions inhabiting different worlds, is much more contro­
versial than other forms of incommensurability. And even advocates of this sort of
incommensurability tend to agree that arguments that are broadly convincing are
difﬁcult to come by. Certainly in some cases (for example, the anomalous playing
cards case) we do, in an important sense, see parts of the world in a way strongly
conditioned by our backgrounds. Whether this sort of effect on how we see things
extends more broadly, in the way suggested by advocates of different worlds
incommensurability, is a much more contentious issue.

Discussion: Incommensurability and Scientiﬁc Progress
The main focus of this ﬁnal section is to explore, admittedly brieﬂy, a central
alleged implication of incommensurability. This particular implication involves
the question of whether incommensurability entails that there can be no
important sense whereby we can say there is genuine progress in science. The
issues involved are probably the most complex and contentious of any issues
surrounding incommensurability. A great deal has been written on this, but
nothing close to a consensus has emerged.
It has been argued that accepting incommensurability, especially terminologi­
cal and methodological incommensurability, entails that there is no way to
adequately compare different scientiﬁc theories. And, if we cannot adequately
compare competing theories, for example Newtonian physics and the more
recent relativistic physics, then there can be no uniform, principled criteria by
which we can claim that one theory is better than the other. Thus theory choice –
say preferring relativistic physics over Newtonian physics – is at bottom not based
on any rational criteria. In short, the claim is that accepting incommensurability
entails that theory choice is not a rational process.

227

228

Recent Developments

Closely related to this is the claim that there can be no meaningful sense of progress
in science. Given the considerations in the above paragraph, theory change – for
example, the change from Aristotelian physics to Newtonian physics, or from
Newtonian physics to relativistic physics – is not a matter of science making
progress toward some goal, perhaps toward some ideal scientiﬁc theory. Rather,
theory change begins to look more like a replacement of one tradition with
another tradition, with the new tradition focusing on different questions and
using different methodologies. In short, the claim is that there is no meaningful
sense of progress involved when we move from one tradition to another. Rather,
we are merely replacing one tradition with a different, incomparable tradition.
Incommensurability does, at least on the face of it, seem incompatible with one
view of scientiﬁc progress (though I should note that even this is contentious).
Consider the view that progress in science consists of a type of linear progression,
where successive scientiﬁc traditions are seen as moving toward and converging
on an ideal science, and moving closer to and converging on “the truth.”
We ﬁrst saw in Chapters 1 and 2 some misconceptions involving common
views of the relation between facts, truth, and science. Here we can see that in
addition to the considerations we looked at in those chapters, if scientiﬁc
traditions are incommensurable, then this conception of scientiﬁc progress –
the conception of progress as moving in a linear way toward some ideal theory –
seems like it cannot be correct, or at the very least is problematic. Methodological
incommensurability in particular entails that different traditions, such as the
Aristotelian and the Newtonian, are not even addressing the same questions,
much less speaking the same language. Given this, it is difﬁcult to see how they
could be directed toward the same goal, if directed at any goal at all.
But this does not mean that incommensurability is incompatible with any
meaningful sense of progress in science. In what follows I will not attempt an
extensive listing of all the alternative senses of scientiﬁc progress compatible with
incommensurability that have been explored. Rather, I will outline only one such
sense.
Let’s begin with an uncontroversial observation, namely, that incommensurability
does not imply that there are no meaningful ways to make comparisons. Consider a
simple example: there is no question that the side and diagonal of a square are
incommensurable in the mathematical sense discussed earlier in this chapter. We
can, though, still compare the lengths of the side and diagonal of a square. One such
comparison is simple: for a square having a side 1 meter in length, the diagonal is a bit
less than one and a half times the length of the side. The side and diagonal may be
incommensurable, but that does not mean they cannot be compared.
Likewise, even if the Aristotelian and Newtonian traditions are incommensurable,
we can still, in some important ways, compare them. For example, consider an
instrumentalist point of view, especially one where we focus on the usefulness of
traditions. From this point of view, it seems safe to say that Newtonian physics is
preferable to Aristotelian physics. With Newtonian physics we can and have sent
people to the moon and rovers to Mars, we have been able to put into orbit and
maintain for a long period of time the International Space Station, and countless
other things that we could never have done with Aristotelian physics. Similarly, with
the newer relativistic physics we can do things we could never have done with

25 Are (Some) Scientiﬁc Theories Incommensurable?

Newtonian physics – our GPS (Global Positioning System) mapping and navigation
systems provide one such example among many. In short, a practical, instrumen­
talist-grounded view gives us one way to provide a sense of progress that seems
compatible with incommensurability. So while incommensurability does have
implications for views of scientiﬁc progress, we can see that incommensurability
does not rule out all meaningful senses of progress.
Whether there are deeper and more substantive notions of progress in science
that go beyond the practical account just outlined is a matter of debate. Issues
surrounding progress in science are as complex and contentious as any involving
incommensurability, and in this brief section I have tried to outline only the most
straightforward and least controversial concept of progress. As usual, suggestions
for pursuing these issues further can be found in the Chapter Notes.

Concluding Remarks
It is difﬁcult to overstate how much has been written on the subject of
incommensurability, as well as how much has been written on related issues
involving progress in science. As noted, issues surrounding incommensurability
