are as complex and contentious as any involving the history and philosophy of
science. What is included above should give a sense of the basics of, and potential
implications of, incommensurability and, I would hope, provide enough of a
background for productive further explorations of these topics.

229

230

26
Introduction to Quantum Theory: Basic Empirical Facts
and the Mathematics of Quantum Theory
In previous chapters we explored the special and general theories of relativity, and
saw that these theories have interesting implications, primarily for views on the
nature of space and time and the nature of gravity. In this chapter we turn to
another important branch of modern physics, that being quantum theory. We will
soon see that recent discoveries involving quantum theory likewise have sub­
stantial implications.
Quantum theory is a slippery topic, and one that requires careful going if we are
to get an accurate overview. Our strategy will be to ﬁrst explain a key distinction
between (i) empirical facts involving “quantum entities,” (ii) quantum theory
itself, that is, the mathematical core of quantum theory, and (iii) issues related to
interpretations of quantum theory. After these three issues are clariﬁed, we will
then go on, in subsequent sections of this chapter, to explore (i) and (ii), saving (iii)
for a separate chapter.

Facts, Theory, and Interpretation
As noted above, in any nontechnical discussion of quantum theory, there are at
least three separate issues that need to be kept distinct, these being (i) the
quantum facts, that is, empirical facts involving quantum entities, (ii) quantum
theory itself, by which I will mean the mathematical core of quantum theory, and
(iii) interpretations of quantum theory, which largely involve philosophical
questions about what sort of reality could give rise to the quantum facts and
that, presumably, would be consistent with the mathematics of quantum theory.
Unfortunately, in popular, nontechnical discussions of quantum theory, as often
as not these issues come all mushed together. For example, it is not unusual to see
claims that quantum theory shows that western science and certain eastern
philosophies are converging on the same view of the universe. But this is a
mistake. Certain interpretations of quantum theory suggest such a convergence,
but the interpretation of a theory and the theory itself are issues that should be
kept separate. As one other example, it is likewise not unusual to see claims that
quantum theory shows that the universe is constantly splitting into multiple,
parallel universes. But again, certain interpretations of quantum theory suggest
this, but quantum theory itself does not.
Worldviews: An Introduction to the History and Philosophy of Science, Third Edition.
Richard DeWitt.
 2018 Richard DeWitt. Published 2018 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.

26 Introduction to Quantum Theory

Issues surrounding quantum theory are admittedly complex. But if we
approach the subject slowly and carefully, we can arrive at a good – and
importantly, an accurate – overview of quantum theory and related issues.
Our ﬁrst step will be to describe brieﬂy the distinction between the three issues
mentioned above.
The quantum facts
When I speak of quantum facts, I mean simply empirical facts involving quantum
entities. Such facts would include the outcome of experiments involving elec­
trons, neutrons, protons, and other subatomic particles; experiments involving
photons, that is, “units” of light; experiments involving particles (for example,
alpha and beta particles) emitted during radioactive decay, and so on.
As we will see later in this chapter, these facts are surprising, but they are not
controversial. In particular, there is no disagreement on what these facts are.
There is substantial disagreement on how to interpret the facts, for example, on
what sort of reality could produce such unusual facts. But again, issues involving
interpretation need to be kept separate from accounts of the quantum facts
themselves.
Before moving on, two items are worth brieﬂy noting. First, I am leaving
intentionally vague what precisely counts as a quantum entity. The entities
mentioned above – subatomic particles such as electrons, protons and the
like, photons, and particles involved in radioactive decay – are clearly quantum
entities. So in most of the upcoming discussion, the quantum facts we discuss will
be facts about such entities. But keep in mind that all objects – you, me, our desks
and chairs, and so on – are composed of these smaller entities, but that whether
ordinary-sized objects should be considered quantum entities is a point of some
dispute. Hence, in what follows I will emphasize mainly quantum facts that
involve uncontroversial quantum entities, such as those mentioned above.
Second, it is common to use the word “particle” to speak of quantum entities such
as photons, electrons, entities emitted during radioactive decay, and the like. And
indeed, in some contexts quantum entities act as if they are particles. But as we will
see shortly, in other contexts quantum entities act as if they are waves. As it turns
out, neither “particle” nor “wave” is quite right when speaking of quantum entities. I
will continue, as is standard, to use the word “particle,” but nothing should be read
into this with respect to the question of what quantum entities really are.
Quantum theory itself
Quantum theory, like much of physics since the 1600s, is a mathematically based
theory, and when I speak of “quantum theory itself,” I primarily have in mind the
mathematics at the heart of quantum theory. The key mathematical components
of quantum theory were discovered in the late 1920s, and that mathematics is
used much as the mathematics in other branches of physics is used. Most notably,
the mathematics of quantum theory is used to predict and explain phenomena
involving quantum entities, including the sorts of quantum facts mentioned
above.

231

232

Recent Developments

As a ﬁnal brief point, the mathematics of quantum theory has been enormously
successful. The mathematics of quantum theory has been around in roughly the
same form for over 80 years, and it has yet to make an incorrect prediction. With
respect to prediction and explanation, quantum theory is arguably the most
successful theory we have ever had.
Interpretations of quantum theory
The interpretation of quantum theory is essentially a philosophical topic centered
around the nature of reality. In particular, the various interpretations of quantum
theory center around the question of what sort of reality is consistent with both the
quantum facts and quantum theory itself. That is, presumably the quantum facts are
the result of some sort of reality “out there,” and given the success of the mathematics
of quantum theory in predicting and explaining these facts, it is not unreasonable to
think that the mathematics is in some way touching base with that reality. The
interpretation question, then, is what sort of reality, consistent with the known facts
and with the mathematics of quantum theory, could be producing those facts?
In the following sections we will look more closely at some basic quantum facts
and the mathematics of quantum theory, and in the next chapter we will turn to
an exploration of various reality issues, most notably the so-called measurement
problem, and various interpretations of quantum theory. As noted, the quantum
facts, quantum theory itself, and issues involving the interpretation of quantum
theory are easy to conﬂate, and doing so can (and does) lead to serious confusions
and misunderstandings about issues surrounding quantum theory, as well as to
confusions and misunderstandings about potential implications of quantum
theory. In what follows, I cannot encourage you too much to keep clear on
the distinction between the quantum facts, quantum theory itself, and the
interpretation of quantum theory.

Some Quantum Facts
In this section, we look at some reasonably straightforward empirical results of
experiments involving quantum entities such as electrons, photons, and the like.
The experiments described below, or ones similar to them, are commonly used to
convey some of the oddness associated with quantum facts. These experiments
primarily involve electrons and photons. Electrons are components of atoms, and
can easily be produced by an electron gun. Electron guns are devices that produce
a stream of electrons, and such devices are common. For example, the bulky
older-style televisions and computer monitors (not ﬂat-screen models) have an
electron gun in the back, and the image on the screen is produced by guiding the
electrons produced by this gun to appropriate locations on the screen. Photons,
on the other hand, are “units” of light, and of course can be produced by any
number of means, such as ﬂashlights.
For ease of discussion, I want to move away, for a moment, from consideration
of just quantum facts, that is, mere outcomes of experiments, and take a brief
excursion into a reality discussion. Note this will brieﬂy take us into issues

26 Introduction to Quantum Theory

involving interpretation, but doing so will make the ensuing discussion easier. We
will quickly return to a straightforward consideration of just the facts.
A brief excursion into a reality issue
As we will see in a minute, some experimental outcomes involving quantum
entities are most consistent with the view that quantum entities are waves, while
some experimental outcomes are most consistent with the view that quantum
entities are particles. Suppose we consider the reality question: Are electrons,
photons, and the like really particles, or are they really waves?
First, take a moment to consider the fact that waves and particles are quite
different. Consider particles ﬁrst. A baseball is a good example. Particles are
discrete objects, with reasonably well-deﬁned locations in space and time.
Particles interact with one another in typical particle-like ways, for example,
by bouncing off one another, or perhaps by breaking into smaller particles.
A wave, on the other hand, is better viewed as a phenomenon rather than as a
discrete object, and waves are typically spread out over a fairly large region, rather
than being conﬁned to a relatively small, well-deﬁned location in space and time.
A wave at a beach, for example, is not located in one particular location, but rather
is spread out over a large area. Moreover, waves interact with one another in quite
different ways than do particles. Two waves will sometimes interact with one
another so as to form a larger wave; sometimes two waves will interact in such a
way as to essentially cancel one another out; and sometimes two waves will pass
through one another, emerging unchanged from the interaction.
In keeping with the different nature of particles and waves, the two produce
very different experimental effects. So one would think that determining whether
electrons are particles or waves would be relatively easy. For example, suppose we
use a source that provides a steady stream of particles, say, a paintball gun (these
are guns that shoot small pellets containing paint, with the paint marking where
the pellets hit). Suppose moreover that we shoot a steady stream of paintballs
toward two small open windows. If we ask “What sort of pattern of hits will we
expect?” the answer is easy. Many of the paintballs will hit the wall containing the
windows, and those that pass through the windows will pile up on the inside wall,
behind the windows. That is, we should expect to see a pattern of hits, on the
inside wall, that correspond to where the windows are located.
Likewise, suppose for a moment that electrons are particles, and that we shoot
thousands of electrons at a barrier with two slits, with a piece of photographic
paper on the far side of the slits. Just as when we shot paintballs toward the two
windows, if electrons are particles, then many of the electrons will hit the barrier,
but those that pass through should hit the photographic paper in the areas behind
the slits. Electrons can be recorded on photographic paper, so in this case we
should expect to record what looks like thousands of individual particle hits, piled
up on areas of the photographic paper behind the two slits. (Incidentally,
photographic paper will not directly record electrons, but when coupled with
a phosphor screen, which emits light when struck by electrons, photographic
paper works well as an electron detector. For ease of discussion, we will continue
to speak of the photographic paper itself as recording the electrons.)

233

234

Recent Developments

Figure 26.1 Electrons as particles.

Pictorially, this scenario would look like Figure 26.1. Importantly, do not
overlook the fact that this picture is going well beyond the mere facts. In
particular, since we cannot detect or otherwise observe electrons before they
interact with some sort of measuring device, such as a piece of photographic
paper, the depiction of the electrons between the electron gun and the paper is an
interpretation, and not any sort of straightforward empirical fact. Again, this is a
picture, an interpretation, of what reality is presumably like if electrons are
particles. Keeping that in mind, Figure 26.1 is a picture of this scenario.
Notice the piling up pattern of electrons on the photographic paper. Again, this
is what we would expect if electrons are particles, and this piling up pattern is
what we will refer to as the “particle effect.”
On the other hand, suppose electrons are waves, and suppose we again pass
them through the same apparatus, with two slits and photographic paper. In this
case, the two slits will split the wave into two waves. These two waves will then
interact with one another, and the result will be a typical interference pattern
produced by the interaction of two waves. In this particular case, we would expect
the interaction of the waves to produce alternating light and dark bands on the
photographic paper, with the light and dark bands indicating the areas where the
waves reinforced one another, and areas where the waves canceled each other.
Such interference patterns are well known, having been studied as far back as the
early 1800s.
So if electrons are waves, the two-slit apparatus should produce an effect
similar to that shown in Figure 26.2. Once again, I emphasize that such waves
cannot be directly observed, and so the picture is again an interpretation of what
the underlying reality might be like. With that in mind, Figure 26.2, which I will
refer to as the “wave effect,” is what we should expect if electrons are waves.
In short, if electrons are particles, they should produce one sort of outcome, the
particle effect, and if electrons are waves, they should produce a quite different
sort of outcome, the wave effect. The particle effect and wave effect are
summarized in Figure 26.3.

26 Introduction to Quantum Theory

Figure 26.2 Electrons as waves.

We will now move on to describe a few experiments involving electrons. At this
point, we are returning from our brief excursion into interpretative/reality issues,
and will now simply describe the facts. That is, in the remainder of this section, we
will describe some experimental setups involving quantum entities, and describe
the results of those experiments.
Four experiments
The experiments described below are fairly standard examples, widely used to
illustrate some of the puzzling features of quantum facts. The ﬁrst experiment is
that pictured in Figure 26.1 and 26.2 above. That is, we take an electron gun, shoot
electrons at a barrier with two slits, and record the results on a piece of
photographic paper.
Given this experimental setup, the outcome is a clear wave effect. That is, the
photographic paper will have on it alternating dark and light bands. One more
time, note again the sense in which this is a straightforward quantum fact. We are
describing nothing more than straightforward observational results: if one sets up
Figure 26.3 The particle effect and the wave
effect.

235

236

Recent Developments

a basic two slit apparatus, as described above, the result is a piece of photographic
paper with alternating dark and light bands.
For experiment 2, let’s modify the ﬁrst experiment slightly. In particular,
suppose we have a setup exactly as for the ﬁrst experiment, except that we
will include passive electron detectors to monitor each of the slits. That is, behind
the top slit we put one electron detector, which we will call detector A, which will
record any electron passing through that top slit. Next to the other slit we will
place a second detector, which we will call detector B, which will monitor that
bottom slit. The setup, then, looks as shown in Figure 26.4.
Here is the idea behind including the detectors: if electrons are waves, then the
wave should pass through both slits simultaneously, and hence both detectors
should always ﬁre simultaneously, and it should never happen that only one or the
other detector ﬁres by itself. If, on the other hand, electrons are particles, then
each will at most pass through one or the other slit, and hence only one detector at
a time should register the presence of an electron, and they should never ﬁre
simultaneously.
Recall that, in experiment 1, the result was a clear wave effect. The experiment we
are considering now is exactly like experiment 1, except for the presence of the
detectors. Since the detectors are passive detectors – that is, we would not expect
them to interfere with the electron, but merely note whether an electron is present –
our initial inclination would be to expect this experiment to also result in a wave
effect. But instead, the result of this experiment is a clear particle effect. And in
keeping with this, only one detector at a time ﬁres. The detectors never register
electrons in both slits simultaneously, as would be expected if electrons were waves.
It is as if, in the presence of detectors, the electrons are acting as particles.
Moreover, suppose we hook up an on/off switch – say, something like a light
switch – to the electron detectors, so that we can turn them on and off whenever
we wish. We’ll then be able to switch between the wave effect and the particle
effect by simply moving the switch to the on or off position. When off, we will see
the wave effect, and when on, the particle effect, and we can instantly change

Figure 26.4 Two slit experiment with electron detectors.

26 Introduction to Quantum Theory

between the two, as often and as rapidly as we wish, by merely moving the switch
back and forth.
These results are surprising, because it is difﬁcult to imagine how such
detectors could so substantially alter the outcome of the experiment. But again,
this is just a fact about experiments involving quantum entities – if the experi­
ment is set up as described in experiment 1, a wave effect is the result. And if we
add electron detectors as in experiment 2, the outcome is a particle effect. And, as
described above, we can switch between a wave effect and a particle effect by
merely turning the detectors off or on.
For experiment 3, we will use a photon gun rather than an electron gun. A
photon gun is a device that produces “units” of light. We will set the
experiment up with a beam splitter (which is essentially just a partially
silvered mirror), a beam recombiner (essentially just a two-way mirror),
two normal mirrors, and a piece of photographic paper to record the results.
Once again, we will put two photon detectors into the setup, but for experi­
ment 3, we will have the detectors turned off. In summary, the experimental
setup looks as shown in Figure 26.5.
Here is the idea behind this experiment: suppose the photon is a wave. The
photon gun emits the wave toward the beam splitter, which splits the wave into
two waves. One wave continues straight, toward the mirror in the upper right of
the diagram, and the other wave is reﬂected down, toward the mirror in the lower
left. The mirrors reﬂect the wave, which recombine at the beam recombiner,
which also directs the wave toward the photographic paper. Since there are two
waves in this situation, they will interfere and produce a wave interference
pattern, that is, a wave effect, on the paper.

Figure 26.5 Beam splitter experiment.

237

238

Recent Developments

On the other hand, if photons are particles, then each photon will travel either
through the upper/right route or through the lower/left route. Here there are no
waves to interfere, and hence we would expect a particle effect.
Although we have included photon detectors in the diagram, for experiment 3
these detectors are turned off and hence play no role. And when we run this
experiment, the result is a clear wave effect, as if photons were waves.
For experiment 4, we will use the exact same setup as experiment 3, except that
we will turn the detectors on. By this point you can probably guess, correctly, that
something odd happens if we turn the detectors on. Again, these detectors are
presumably playing a passive role as did the detectors in experiment 2. And again,
if photons are waves, as the previous experiment suggests, then we would expect
both detectors to ﬁre simultaneously. After all, experiment 3 suggests that
photons are waves, and hence a wave should be present at both detectors
simultaneously.
But only one or the other detector goes off at a time, which is what we would
expect if photons were particles, not waves. And the result on the photographic
paper, in spite of the fact that this experiment is almost identical to the previous
experiment, is a clear particle effect.
And exactly as with experiment 2, we can hook up an on/off switch to the
detectors, and switch whenever we wish between the wave effect and the particle
effect by merely turning the detectors off or on. Let’s take a minute to think about
how odd this seems. It seems, in experiment 3, that we could only get these results
if photons really are waves. And it seems, for experiment 4, that we could only get
these results if photons really are particles.
These are only four experimental results out of thousands we could consider.
But these four should sufﬁce to convey some of the oddness of the quantum facts.
Before closing this subsection, let me offer two more brief considerations.
First, here is a rough guide to predicting the outcome of experiments involving
quantum entities. When a detection or measurement of a quantum entity is made,
what will be detected seems to be a particle. That is, it is as if, when detected,
quantum entities are particles. But when not being detected or measured,
quantum entities seem to behave as if they are waves. So as a rough guide to
predicting the outcome of experiments, ask when the ﬁrst measurement or
detection is made. In experiment 1, the ﬁrst measuring device is the photographic
paper. Before this measurement, think of the quantum entity as behaving as if it
were a wave. Since no detection is made until after the slits, it will be as if there is
wave interference and hence we would expect the interference pattern typical of
the wave effect. On the other hand, in experiment 2, the ﬁrst measurement takes
place at the detectors, before there is a chance for wave interference. And so on for
the other experiments.
Be careful not to misunderstand my point in the above paragraph. I am not
saying that quantum entities really are particles when detected, and really are
waves when not detected. Rather, I am being agnostic as to what is really going on,
and merely offering a rough guide to predicting the outcome of such experiments.
Treat quantum entities as if they are particles when detected, and as if they are
waves when not being detected, and you will have a way of anticipating the
outcomes of experiments such as those above.

26 Introduction to Quantum Theory

As a second but related consideration, notice that acts of measurement or
detection seem to play a curious role when quantum entities are involved. For
example, the electron and photon detectors in the above experiments are
measuring devices that measure the presence of an electron or photon. Such
measuring devices seem to affect what happens, for example, whether we see a
wave or particle effect. And this is very puzzling. How could an electron, photon,
or other quantum entity “know” whether there is a detector or other measure­
ment device nearby? And for that matter, what exactly counts as a measurement?
These are very difﬁcult questions, and are part of what is often referred to as the
measurement problem. We will explore the measurement problem in the next
chapter, but for now, I just wanted to introduce these issues about measurements,
and the curious role measurements play in quantum theory.

Overview of the Mathematics of Quantum Theory
The mathematics of quantum theory is sufﬁciently difﬁcult as to make it
impossible, in a text such as this, to present it in any detail while at the same
time remaining accurate and comprehensible. But while it may be impossible to
explain here the details of the mathematics of quantum theory, it is not
particularly difﬁcult to give a good idea of what that mathematics is like, all
the while remaining accurate and accessible.
My strategy will be to describe the mathematics in two separate, though
overlapping, ways. I will begin with a section providing a very general descriptive
overview of the mathematics of quantum theory. I will then provide a section that
is still fairly general and descriptive, although with a sizeable amount more detail.
For those wishing more detail yet, in the Chapter Notes at the end of this book is a
section providing yet more detail.
Descriptive overview of the mathematics of quantum theory
Quantum theory is, at bottom, a “wave” sort of mathematics, as distinguished
from a particle sort of math. A few notes on wave math versus particle math may
be helpful.
In physics, we ﬁnd both “particle” mathematics and “wave” mathematics. By
this I mean simply that a certain type of mathematics is used when the situation
involves discrete objects (“particles”), and another type of mathematics is used
when the situation involves waves. For example, if we drop a bowling ball off the
roof of a building, the object involved is a discrete object (the ball) seemingly
under the inﬂuence of various forces (gravitational forces, for example). The
mathematics appropriate for this situation would be an example of what I am
referring to as particle mathematics.
Waves, however, differ from particles (some of the key differences were
discussed above), and hence the math appropriate for particles is not appropriate
for situations involving waves. There are, however, well-established mathematics
for dealing with waves. And in physics, wave mathematics is used just as particle
mathematics is used. In particular, wave mathematics allows us to make

239

240

Recent Developments

predictions about what attributes of a system we will detect (for example, the
amount of energy a wave is carrying), as well as how the system will evolve over
time (for example, where the crest of the wave will be located at a future time).
Again, quantum theory is a wave sort of mathematics. But as mentioned, there
is nothing unusual about this. Wave mathematics are everywhere in physics, and
quantum theory is simply a particular version of a type of mathematics with which
physicists are well acquainted.
There is one common question I want to address before ending this section, but
otherwise, this concludes this very general descriptive overview of the mathe­
matics of quantum theory. In summary, the math of quantum theory is a wave sort
of mathematics, and the mathematics of quantum theory is used in the same way
that other mathematics is used in physics. In particular, given the current state of
a system, one can use the mathematics of quantum theory to make predictions
about what attributes of the system might be observed, and to make predictions
about what state the system will be in at a future time.
If the mathematics of quantum theory is a familiar sort of wave mathematics, why do
we often hear that quantum theory is such an unusual theory?

One can easily get the impression, from various writings on quantum theory, that
quantum theory is somehow profoundly different from past theories in physics.
Indeed, viewed a certain way, I think this sentiment is correct. For example, issues
surrounding quantum theory force us to rethink some of the basic assumptions
we have had about the world ever since the time of the ancient Greeks. But given
that the mathematics of quantum theory is a familiar sort of wave mathematics, in
what sense is quantum theory different from other theories in physics?
One minor difference, though one worth mentioning, is that the mathematics
of quantum theory usually provides probabilistic predictions rather than deﬁnite
predictions. For example, if we use the mathematics of quantum theory to predict,
say, the position of an electron, the mathematics will provide us with the
probability of detecting the electron at various positions. In contrast, in the
case of the bowling ball dropped from the roof, the mathematics will provide us
with a deﬁnite prediction. In short, the predictions of other branches of physics
tend to be deﬁnite (“the ball will be detected in this location”), whereas the
predictions of quantum theory are typically probabilistic (“there is such and such
a probability of detecting the electron at this location”).
But this is a fairly minor difference with other branches of physics. The major
difference I have in mind concerns the interpretation of the mathematics. Since
issues involving interpretation are a central topic of the next chapter, I will discuss
it only brieﬂy here.
The ﬁrst point to note is this: the mathematics used in physics is, at bottom, just
mathematics. As such, the mathematics has no necessary or inherent ties to the
world. This is a point that is easy to overlook, but is central in understanding the
sense in which quantum theory is an unusual theory. To understand this better,
consider again a simple case such as the dropped bowling ball. In the mathematics
used to make predictions about the falling ball, there is nothing that requires that
chunk of mathematics to be interpreted as being about a falling object. The
equation used is just an equation, just a piece of mathematics, just a collection of

26 Introduction to Quantum Theory

symbols joined together and manipulated according to the rules of the mathe­
matics involved.
The fact is we interpret that chunk of mathematics in a certain way (for
example, as being about a falling ball), and such interpretations have been
extremely fruitful and useful (useful, for example, for making predictions). In
addition, we tend to interpret the chunk of mathematics involving the falling ball
in more or less the same way. For example, there is general agreement that this
part of the equation represents the falling ball, this part of the equation represents
time, this part represents the starting location of the ball, and so on. In short, there
is general agreement on how that equation describes, or “paints a picture of,” the
situation involving the falling ball.
In cases such as the equation involved with the falling ball, it is precisely the fact
that we tend to agree on the interpretation that disguises the fact that we are even
making an interpretation. In other words, we are indeed interpreting the math,
but we all interpret it the same way, and have done so for several hundred years.
As a result, we tend not to recognize that using mathematics to make predictions
about the world requires that we interpret the math as being about the world. But
at bottom, the way we tie up the math with the world is not an inherent part of the
mathematics; rather, it is an interpretation of the mathematics.
Immediately above, I tried to draw attention to the fact that there is general
agreement on how to tie up the mathematics involved with the falling ball with the
world. (“This part of the equation represents the ball,” and so on.) This is exactly
where a major difference with the mathematics of quantum theory arises: there is
no consensus on how to tie up the mathematics involved in quantum theory with
the world.
I need to be careful here so as not to be misunderstood. Consider an example
where quantum theory is used to make a prediction about the location of an
electron. Almost everyone agrees that the mathematics involved does tie up with
the world, at least in a very general sense. For example, most agree that there are
such things as electrons, that electrons can inﬂuence objects we consider to
be measuring devices we use to record the position of electrons, and that the
mathematics of quantum theory enable us to make predictions about how
those measuring devices will behave in certain situations involving electrons.
So in these very general terms, almost all agree that the mathematics of quantum
theory does indeed “tie up” with things such as electrons and measuring devices.
If one tries to go beyond this general level, the “picture” of reality suggested by
the mathematics of quantum theory seems to be a thoroughly bizarre picture. The
sense in which the picture is bizarre will be explored more fully in the next
chapter. So for now, I will simply leave it at that: the picture is a bizarre one, and
that is why one often hears that quantum theory is such a strange theory.
In closing this section, it is worth emphasizing once again that the mathematics
of quantum theory is not at all strange; rather, the strangeness arises in the
interpretation of that mathematics. And, incidentally, this is a good place to recall
a point made earlier in this text (from the discussion on instrumentalism and
realism in Chapter 8). One need not do this sort of interpretation at all. That is,
taking an instrumentalist attitude toward a theory – in this case, quantum
theory – is a common and respectable attitude. In the case of quantum theory,

241

242

Recent Developments

an instrumentalist attitude would amount to taking the following stance: we have
the mathematics of quantum theory; we have experts highly proﬁcient in the use
of that mathematics; that mathematics allows us to make highly accurate and
reliable predictions. Who could ask for anything more?
A somewhat more detailed, but still descriptive, overview of the
mathematics of quantum theory
As described above, the mathematics of quantum theory is a wave sort of
mathematics. Let’s begin this section with a few facts about waves and wave
mathematics.
First, a fairly unremarkable fact, though one you may not have thought about
explicitly: waves come in families. For example, the waves produced by stringed
instruments (for example, guitars and banjos) have similarities not shared by
waves produced by reed instruments (for example, clarinets and saxophones),
which in turn have similarities not shared by waves produced by percussion
instruments (for example, bass and bongo drums). This is not much different
from the fact that you and other members of your family have similarities not
shared by myself and other members of my family. In short, we can group waves
into families.
Given that waves come in families, it is not surprising that the mathematics that
apply to waves can likewise be grouped into families. Suppose, then, that
Figure 26.6 represents the various families of wave mathematics.
In Figure 26.6, the family icons at the left represent the families of waves, which
I will simply refer to as family A, B, C, D, and so on. This is similar to the way that
your last name might represent your entire family. The icons to the right of the
equal signs represent the individual members of each family, which I will refer to
as a1, a2, a3, and so on. And again, this is similar to the way that your name, and
the names of your parents, sisters, and brothers, might represent the individual
members of your family.
Now, in contrast to the unremarkable fact that waves come in families, here is a
quite surprising fact: any particular wave can be produced by adding together
appropriate members of any family of waves. For example, take a particular wave,
say, one produced by plucking the top string of a guitar. Suppose Figure 26.7
represents the equation that characterizes this wave.
Now, pick any family from the chart of families in Figure 26.6, let’s say family A.
There will be members of family A such that, when those members are added
together, they will produce the wave in question. That is, this wave can be
produced by adding together appropriate members of family A. Schematically,
this would look as shown in Figure 26.8.
Notably, that same wave equation can be produced by adding together
appropriate members of any other family. So, for example, there will be appro­
priate members of family C that can be added together to produce the same wave
equation, as shown in Figure 26.9.
And in general, any given wave can be produced by adding together appropriate
members of any given family of waves. As mentioned, this is a rather remarkable
fact about waves, and one that has been extensively studied and has proven quite

26 Introduction to Quantum Theory

Figure 26.6 Families of wave mathematics.

Figure 26.7 Representation of a wave equation.

Figure 26.8 Adding family members to produce a particular wave.

Figure 26.9 Members of another family can produce the same wave.

243

244

Recent Developments

fruitful over the past century. It is, for example, this fact that makes it possible to
reproduce music on electronic devices. (Think about it: Isn’t it rather astounding
that the almost exact sounds of guitars, drums, horns, and so on, can be produced
by small electronic devices that have almost nothing in common with guitars,
drums, or horns? The key to how this works is in the above facts about waves. In
outline, the waves produced by, say, the earbuds you use to listen to music form a
family of waves, and thus, because of the facts above, any wave of a guitar,
trumpet, human voice, or whatever, can be produced by adding together
appropriate members of the “earbud” family of waves.)
At bottom, this one unremarkable fact (that waves come in families), and the
one remarkable fact (that any given wave can be produced by adding together
appropriate members of any given family), are all the facts needed to get a better
idea of the way the mathematics of quantum theory work. The last task of this
section, then, is to explain how to relate these facts about wave mathematics to
quantum theory.
The relation between these facts about wave mathematics and quantum theory
can be stated quite brieﬂy, although explaining this clearly will take a bit more
work. Here is a summary of the bottom line:
1) A state of a quantum system is represented by a particular piece of wave
mathematics, usually called the wave function for that system.
2) Each type of measurement one might perform on a quantum system is
associated with a particular family of waves.
3) Predictions about the outcome of a measurement on the quantum system are
arrived at by ﬁnding the members of the family of waves (the family associated
with that measurement) that, when added together, produce the wave
function.
Let’s work on unpacking what this means, beginning with an explanation of (1)
above. Consider a quantum system, say a particular electron in a particular
setting. In a way similar to the way a wave produced by a guitar string can be
represented by a particular piece of wave mathematics, so too the electron in this
particular setting can be represented by a piece of wave mathematics. Again, this
piece of mathematics is called the wave function for this system.
Let’s suppose the wave function for the electron is as shown in Figure 26.10. So
understanding (1) is relatively straightforward. A quantum system, such as an
electron in a particular setting, can be represented by a wave function.
Understanding (2) is a bit more involved, but still fairly straightforward.
Recall there are families of waves, and that the mathematics associated with
waves also comes in families. In the mathematics of quantum theory, each such
family is associated with a particular type of measurement. For example, the
position of an electron is one measurement one might perform on the electron
mentioned above. And among the various families of wave mathematics, one

Figure 26.10 Representation of the wave function for an electron in a
particular setting.

26 Introduction to Quantum Theory

family will be associated with measurements of position. Another family will be
associated with measurements of the momentum of the electron, another
family with the spin of the electron, and so on for the various possible
measurements we might perform on the electron. In short, as stated in (2),
each family of wave mathematics is associated with a possible measurement one
might perform on a quantum system.
Pictorially, this would look as shown in Figure 26.11. The ﬁgure shows only
three types of measurements, but there are actually inﬁnitely many types of
measurements one might perform on a quantum system. And as suggested by
Figure 26.11, and described in (2) above, each such measurement will be
associated with a particular wave family. (Incidentally, I’ve chosen the easy to
remember P, M, and S, but it should be noted that these are not the standard
abbreviations used by physicists to represent position, momentum, and spin.)
Understanding (3) is probably the trickiest part of this story. Let’s approach this
by way of example. Suppose we have a particular electron in a particular situation,
and we want to make predictions about what will happen if we perform a
measurement on the position of the electron. For simplicity, suppose for this
example there are only two possible locations where the electron might be found.
Now, from (1) we know that there is a wave function associated with that electron
(see Figure 26.12) and from (2) we know that, among the various families
of mathematics associated with waves, there will be one family, let’s say this is
family P, that is associated with measurements of position (see Figure 26.13).
Now recall the remarkable fact above, that appropriate members of any wave
family can be added together to produce any particular wave function. So in
particular, there will be appropriate members of family P that, when added
together, produce the wave function for the electron. Let’s suppose that p8 and
p11 are the members of family P that add together to produce the wave function
for the electron. Schematically, this is shown in Figure 26.14.

Figure 26.11 Families associated with measurements.

245

246

Recent Developments

Figure 26.12 Wave function for electron.

Figure 26.13 Family P associated with measurements of position.

Figure 26.14 Wave function decomposed
into members of family P.

With this information in mind, we are in a position to explain (3). Recall that we
are interested in making a prediction about the position of the electron, that P is
the family associated with measurements of position, and that p8 and p11 are the
members of P that add together to produce the wave function that represents the
electron.
These two family members, p8 and p11, will enable us to make the desired
predictions. In this example, there were two areas where the electron might be
found. As it turns out, some straightforward and standard mathematical manip­
ulation of p8 will result in a number between 0 and 1, and this number represents
the probability of ﬁnding the electron in the ﬁrst area. Likewise, the same
straightforward mathematical manipulation of p11 will also result in a number
between 0 and 1, and this number represents the probability of ﬁnding the
electron in the second area. This, then, is how p8 and p11 lead to the predictions
about the position of the electron.
(It is not crucial for understanding this discussion, but if you are curious, here is
an overview of the mathematical manipulation just mentioned: p8 is a piece of
wave mathematics associated with a particular wave. All waves have amplitudes
associated with them, and so p8 will likewise have an amplitude associated with it.
The mathematical manipulation involves squaring the amplitude of p8. Because
of the nature of the mathematics involved, this will always result in a number
between 0 and 1. As mentioned, that number will represent the probability of
ﬁnding the electron in the ﬁrst area. Do likewise for p11 – that is, square the
amplitude associated with p11 – and that number will be the probability of ﬁnding
the electron in the second area.)
Thus, (1), (2), and (3) describe how the mathematics of quantum theory is used
to predict the probabilities of observing the possible outcomes of measurements
performed on quantum systems. In summary: (1) states of a quantum system are
represented by the wave function for that system; (2) wave families are associated
with types of measurements; and (3) the members of a family that add up to the
wave function allow us to make predictions concerning the outcomes of mea­
surements associated with that wave family.

26 Introduction to Quantum Theory

The evolution of states over time

Before closing this section, a brief note on the evolution of states over time is in
order. Thus far, we have only discussed the outcomes of measurements on a
quantum system in a given state. As noted, physics is generally concerned not only
with the outcome of measurements at a particular time, but also with predicting
the outcome of measurements we might make in the future.
In quantum theory, the evolution of a system over time is predicted by
Schrödinger’s equation. Recall from (1) above that the current state of a system
is represented by the wave function for that system. In very brief outline,
Schrödinger’s equation allows us to calculate, from the wave function represent­
ing the current state of the system, what state the system will be in at future times.
Schrödinger’s equation plays a role in many ways analogous to equations from
other branches of science. To return to the example of the ball dropped from the
roof of a building, within Newtonian physics there are well-known equations that
allow us not only to predict the outcome of measurements at the present time, but
also to predict how that system will change over time. In a way similar to how
these equations in Newtonian physics govern the evolution of states over time, in
quantum theory Schrödinger’s equation likewise governs the evolution of quan­
tum states over time.

Concluding Remarks
Early in the chapter we saw an overview of some basic quantum facts. There is no
question about what the empirical quantum facts are, though in a certain sense
they are odd facts. But their oddness stems more from a reality perspective; in
particular, the oddness emerges primarily when we try to imagine what sort of
underlying reality could give rise to these facts. As I’ve stressed from the outset,
the reality question, the interpretation issue, is an issue that needs to be kept
distinct from the issues discussed in this chapter. We turn to this in the next
chapter.
One ﬁnal note before closing. In the discussion of the mathematics above, I
have primarily stressed the way that the mathematics of quantum theory, at
bottom, performs much the same role as the mathematics we have had in physics
for hundreds of years. In particular, the mathematics of quantum theory, as
summarized in the sections above, allow us to predict the outcome of measure­
ments at a particular time, and via Schrödinger’s equation, allow us to predict
what state the system will be in at a future time.
But there is an important sense in which Schrödinger’s equation, in conjunc­
tion with what is often termed the projection postulate (discussed in the next
chapter), is used differently from equations found in other branches of science.
But this difference is best understood in the context of issues involving interpre­
tation/reality questions. In the next chapter we turn to a careful discussion of such
issues.

247

248

27
The Reality Question: The Measurement Problem and
Interpretations of Quantum Theory
In this chapter we explore two interrelated issues surrounding quantum theory.
We will look ﬁrst at what has come to be called the measurement problem. The
measurement problem is an issue that arises with quantum theory in a way that it
has never arisen with earlier scientiﬁc theories and, as it is closely tied to the
question of what is really going on in experiments and phenomena involving
quantum entities, it is a good topic to explore in a chapter such as this. In addition,
understanding the measurement problem will allow us to better understand
various issues and difﬁculties surrounding proposed interpretations of quantum
theory.

The Measurement Problem
There are a variety of ways to describe the measurement problem, with some
accounts being more technical in nature, some less so. In this section I want to
approach the problem in a way that is nontechnical but still conveys some of the
deeply puzzling aspects surrounding the role of measurement in quantum theory.
It will help to ﬁrst identify a feature of measurements that is, I think, generally not
widely appreciated.
A brief terminological point: in the remainder of this chapter when I speak of
the “standard approach to quantum theory,” I will have in mind the usual
approach to the mathematics of quantum theory, that is, the mathematics
described in the previous chapter. This is the mathematics that is found in
almost all textbooks and college-level courses (those courses aimed at physics
majors) on the subject, and the mathematics used primarily by physicists working
with systems involving quantum entities.
What is a measurement?
Consider a particular measuring device, say the sort of outdoor thermometer
commonly found on back porches. I have in mind here a sort of largish, roundfaced thermometer, with a dial pointing to numbers representing air tempera­
tures, with those numbers arranged around the perimeter of the face.
Worldviews: An Introduction to the History and Philosophy of Science, Third Edition.
Richard DeWitt.
 2018 Richard DeWitt. Published 2018 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.

27 The Reality Question

Such a thermometer is a paradigm example of something we consider a
measuring device, in this case, one used to measure air temperature. In outline,
here is how this particular sort of measuring device works (or more precisely, how
one type of design works). Inside the housing, near the center, is a thin coiled strip
composed of two different types of metal (a bimetallic strip). The two types of
metal will expand and contract at different rates when warmed or cooled, and this
causes the coil to tighten or loosen as the air temperature gets cooler or warmer. If
we attach a pointer to the center of the coil, the changes in the coil will cause the
pointer to move. If we choose the right sorts of metals, and the right length, width,
and initial tension on the coil, and the right initial pointer position, we can get a
device where physical changes in it will cause the pointer to move reliably through
a range of numbers corresponding to air temperatures.
Now contrast this example with something we do not consider to be a
measuring device. Consider a piece of deck furniture, say a particular deck chair,
perhaps sitting next to the thermometer and having a metal frame with a fabric
seat and back.
Notice this chair will go through physical changes corresponding to air
temperature in much the same way the thermometer goes through physical
changes corresponding to air temperature. Just as the metal coil in the thermom­
eter expands and contracts due to air temperature, so also will the metal frame of
the chair expand and contract. And as changes in the coil in turn cause other
changes, such as the position of the dial, so too do the changes in the metal frame
cause other changes in the chair, such as a tightening or loosening of the fabric
composing the seat and back.
Importantly – and this is central in understanding a key aspect of the measure­
ment problem – there is no fundamental physical difference between the thermom­
eter and the chair. Both are, at bottom, just things in the world, just physical objects
undergoing physical changes as they interact with and respond to features of their
environment. But if there is no fundamental physical difference between these two
objects, a key question is this: Why do we consider one to be a measuring device and
the other not to be a measuring device?
It seems the only honest, accurate answer we can give is that the difference lies
in us. Certain things are of interest to us in ways others are not. I and others ﬁnd it
of interest to know the outside air temperature in the morning, so as, for example,
to choose suitable clothing for the day. Thus the physical changes in the device we
call a thermometer are of interest to me. In contrast, I cannot ﬁnd much reason to
be interested in the same sorts of physical changes taking place in the deck chair
located next to the thermometer.
Pause to think about other types of measuring devices, and notice that the same
point applies. Other than our interests, there is no fundamental difference
between, say, a wooden yardstick we use to measure lengths and an identical
wooden yardstick that is used to stir paint. In my shop I in fact have two such
yardsticks, one I’ve used for some years to measure lengths, and the other I’ve
used almost exclusively for stirring paint. In both contexts, the measuring context
and the paint-stirring context, in terms of their physical characteristics these
objects are essentially identical. Yet the one we consider to be a measuring device
and the other a paint stirrer.

249

250

Recent Developments

In general, there are no physical features of objects we consider to be measuring
devices that are at bottom any different from the physical features of objects we do
not consider to be measuring devices. Thus there seems to be no principled way, no
objective way that is independent of human interests, to distinguish objects we
consider measuring devices from objects we do not take to be measuring devices. As
we will see in a moment, in the past this has never been of any major consequence.
However, this aspect of measurements – that there seems to be no objective,
independent way to distinguish between measuring devices and nonmeasuring
devices, and that whether something is or is not a measuring device seems to
depend on us – comes importantly into play when considering the standard
approach to quantum theory. Before turning to the role measurement plays in
quantum theory, it will ﬁrst help to consider the role (or non-role) measurement
plays in other sciences, for example, Newtonian science.
The role of measurement in Newtonian science
Suppose we decide to drop an object, for example a baseball, off the roof of a
reasonably tall building. We know from Newtonian physics that once we drop the
ball, its acceleration (until it hits the ground) will be about 10 meters per second
per second. So for each additional second the ball is falling its velocity will increase
by about 10 meters per second. Thus, if we decide to make a measurement of the
velocity of the ball any time between releasing it and it hitting the ground,
Newtonian physics will tell us what to expect from that measurement. Likewise, if
we decide to measure the position of the ball between the time we release it and
the time it hits the ground, Newtonian physics will again tell us what to expect
from that measurement.
Importantly – and this also will be key in understanding the measurement
problem as it relates to quantum physics – in this example measurement plays no
role in what is happening. Maybe we choose to make a measurement of the ball’s
velocity at some point in its fall, or maybe we choose not to make such a
measurement. Whether we do or do not makes no difference in the evolution of
the system.
As such, measurements in Newtonian science and other branches of basic
sciences raise no particular problems or issues. This is simply because in such
theories, while measurements might tell us about something we are interested in,
measurements do not affect or inﬂuence those sciences’ account of how a system
evolves. In short, in our previous sciences and our current basic sciences other
than quantum theory, measurement plays no unusual role. Thus there are no
problematic issues raised by measurements, that is, there is no measurement
problem. But as you no doubt can guess, in the standard approach to quantum
theory measurement plays a different role. We turn now to that topic.
The role of measurement in quantum theory
At the end of the last chapter, we saw that within quantum theory the evolution of
states over time is governed by Schrödinger’s equation. That is, in a way analogous
to the way we can use the Newtonian equations discussed above to predict how a

27 The Reality Question

Figure 27.1 Beam splitter arrangement.

system involving a ball dropped from the roof of a building will evolve over time,
within quantum theory Schrödinger’s equation allows us to predict how a
quantum system will evolve over time.
Consider an experimental setup similar to some we discussed in the previous
chapter. In particular, suppose we have a photon gun capable of emitting single
photons. We send the photons toward a beam splitter, that is, a partially silvered
mirror that will reﬂect roughly half the photons that encounter it and will allow
about half to pass through. We will place two photon detectors after the beam
splitter, and for ease of discussion, let’s suppose the detectors beep whenever they
detect a photon. The setup is as pictured in Figure 27.1.
Suppose we have a button, and each time we press the button, a photon is
emitted toward the beam splitter. There is no question about what we seem to
observe in this case: each time we press the button, we hear either detector A or
detector B beep, but never both at the same time. And moreover, over a long run
of button presses and photon detections, about 50% of the time we hear detector
A beep and about 50% of the time we hear detector B beep.
Let’s look more closely at how the standard mathematics of quantum theory
represents this setup. Just after the button is pressed, the state of the system is
represented by a wave function (this was discussed more thoroughly in the
previous chapter), in particular, a wave function that represents a wave moving
toward the beam splitter.
Immediately after the photon reaches the beam splitter, Schrödinger’s equation
represents the photon as being in what is termed a superposition of states. To say
the photon is represented as being in a superposition of states is to say that (in this
example at least) it is represented as being in sort of a combination of two
different states. In this case, the superposition consists of one state representing
the photon as a wave traveling toward detector A, with the other state repre­
senting the photon as a wave traveling toward detector B.
Superpositions are everywhere in the standard approach to quantum theory.
Generally speaking, any time there is a physical interaction that results in there

251

252

Recent Developments

being two or more possible states, such as is the case with the beam splitter setup,
Schrödinger’s equation will represent the situation as involving a superposition,
again a sort of combination, of those possible states.
With this in mind, let’s consider what the situation looks like once the photon
has had enough time to reach the detectors. Recall the discussion from above –
the photon detector is just a physical object not in principle different from other
physical objects, such as the beam splitter. So when a photon interacts with a
photon detector, at bottom this is just another physical interaction. And it is a
physical interaction that seems to result in two different possible states, but now
where one state consists of detector A beeping and the other state consists of
detector B beeping.
And indeed, Schrödinger’s equation, if left to run its course, would represent
the setup at this point as consisting of a superposition of states, but now a
superposition that consists of a sort of combination of detector A beeping and
detector B beeping.
If something is beginning to sound odd here, it should. Recall that we never
observe the detectors ﬁring simultaneously. Instead, we observe that either
detector A beeps or detector B beeps. So Schrödinger’s equation, on its own,
is representing the situation in a way that differs substantially from what we seem
to observe. So how does the standard approach handle this discrepancy between
our experience and what Schrödinger’s equation represents?
The standard approach to the mathematics of quantum theory handles this by
including an additional component, an additional basic postulate, beyond the
basic mathematical components discussed in the previous chapter. This addi­
tional component is generally referred to as the projection postulate (or, equally
commonly, as the collapse postulate).
The idea behind the projection postulate is that when we observe the outcome
of a measurement, we essentially insert whatever measurement outcome we
observe into the mathematics. In other words, when we observe a measurement
outcome we put an end to the continually evolving superpositions of states
represented by Schrödinger’s equation; we insert a wave function representing the
outcome we observed; and then (if the system is such that it will continue to
evolve) we restart Schrödinger’s equation and represent the system as evolving
according to that equation until, perhaps, another measurement takes place (in
which case we repeat the procedure).
A terminological note: this point in the mathematical representation of the
scenario – using the projection postulate, following a measurement, to put an end
to the superposition of states – is generally referred to as the “collapse of the wave
function,” or sometimes as the “reduction of the wave packet.” The phrasing
might suggest that the collapse of the wave function is viewed as an actual physical
event, something occurring at the micro level, and indeed some do speak that way.
But for our purposes, at this point it is better to think of this instrumentally, as a
convenient way to describe how the situation is represented mathematically.
So within the standard approach to the mathematics of quantum theory,
measurements play a role unlike the role they play in any previous science or in
any other current basic science. Recall our discussion in the previous section,
where we saw that within Newtonian physics, measurement plays no unusual role.

27 The Reality Question

That is, whether we choose to make a measurement of the position of the falling
baseball or not makes no difference to the system, nor does it make any difference
in the mathematical representation of the system.
But within the standard approach to quantum theory, making a measurement
changes the system (or more precisely, it changes the way the mathematics
represents the system). This unusual role of measurements raises difﬁcult
questions. Even if taken instrumentally, the role of measurement is unusual,
in that measurement changes the mathematical representation of the system
(again, contrast this with the Newtonian example above, where measurement
does not affect the way the system is represented).
But if we take the standard mathematics for quantum theory realistically, the
situation seems very odd. In particular, if we take the standard mathematics
realistically, measurements change the way the world is. If we choose to make a
measurement, the world will be one way. If we choose not to make a measurement,
the world will be another way. In short, in the standard approach to quantum theory
measurement plays a role unlike the role played in any previous science or in other
branches of current science. And as noted, this raises difﬁcult questions.
In a moment we will elaborate on some of these difﬁcult issues, but ﬁrst, it will
be helpful to explore a common and closely related topic found in most
discussions of quantum theory. From what has been said thus far, one might
be tempted to assume that the oddness involving quantum theory – for example
superpositions and the effect of measurements in ending superpositions –
primarily involves only the micro level of entities such as photons, electrons,
and the like, and not in the macro level world of you, me, and our houses and cars,
and so on. To indicate that the situation is not quite so simple, let’s consider
Schrödinger’s cat, which is a well-known creature that can be found lurking
around most discussions of quantum theory. Schrödinger’s cat is worth being
familiar with: it is a well-known example in the history of quantum theory, and it
also helps illustrate the oddness of superpositions of states, shows that such
oddness is not necessarily conﬁned to the micro level, serves as a good example to
ﬂesh out issues involved with the measurement problem, and will later in the
chapter help illustrate some key features of the interpretations of quantum theory
that we will consider shortly.
Schrödinger’s cat
In the late 1920s, Schrödinger himself became uncomfortable with some of the
unusual aspects of the mathematical approach that he had a central role in
developing. He presented a thought experiment which further illustrates some of
the oddness of the quantum picture. Incidentally, a “thought experiment” is just
what the name suggests, that is, an experiment we are asked to think through
rather than actually perform.
Schrödinger asks us to imagine a cat placed in a sealed box, along with a mild
source of radioactivity. The radioactive source is such that, over the course of one
hour, there is a 50% chance that it will emit a radioactive particle. If such a particle
is emitted, it will trigger a detector that in turn is rigged to break open a vial of
poison, which would be fatal to the cat.

253

254

Recent Developments

Schrödinger’s point, of course, was not to abuse cats – again, this is a thought
experiment, not one intended to actually be carried out (nor, if you think about it,
would doing the experiment yield any interesting data). Rather, his point was to
connect up the oddness of the micro-level to macro-level events, as well as to
illustrate the oddness of the superpositions of states that are so common in the
mathematics of quantum theory.
To understand the Schrödinger’s cat thought experiment, consider a slightly
altered setup similar to the beam splitter arrangement pictured in Figure 27.1.
Imagine placing the experimental setup described in Figure 27.1 into a large
opaque box. Along with the setup we will place a cat. We will also rig photon
detector A, but not detector B, to a vial of poison, just as in Schrödinger’s original
thought experiment. That is, if detector A registers a photon, it will open the vial
of poison, which again would be fatal to the cat. On the other hand, if photon
detector B registers a photon, then nothing happens. The setup would then look
as shown in Figure 27.2.
Suppose the entire apparatus, cat and all, is in the sealed box, so that we cannot
see or hear anything that goes on inside the box. We do, however, keep the button
that causes the photon gun to emit a photon outside the box.
Now, given this setup, suppose we push the button once. Recall that the
evolution of states over time is governed by Schrödinger’s equation, and that once
the photon has had time to reach the beam splitter, the equation represents the
situation as being in a superposition of states. Schrödinger’s equation would ﬁrst
represent the situation as a superposition of states, with one state representing a
wave moving toward detector A and the other representing a wave moving toward
detector B. A fraction of a second later, when the photon has had time to reach the

Figure 27.2 Schrödinger’s cat.

27 The Reality Question

detectors, the superposition would involve one state representing that detector A
has ﬁred, and the other state representing detector B as having ﬁred.
Recall that the detection of a photon at A will result in the poison being
released, and hence will result in a dead cat. A detection of a photon at B, on the
other hand, will leave the cat alive and well. So the superposition, as represented
by Schrödinger’s equation, would next move into a superposition of two states,
one in which the cat is dead and the other in which the cat is alive and well. In
other words, up until the time we choose to open the box and make an
observation, the standard approach to the mathematics of quantum theory is
representing the cat as existing in a superposition of cat-dead and cat-alive states.
Again, this is not in principle different from the superposition of the states of
the photon discussed in regard to Figure 27.1 above. As noted, however,
Schrödinger has moved the oddness from the micro to the macro level. The
example also vividly highlights the oddness of the notion of superpositions as used
in quantum theory, as well as providing a useful example to highlight aspects of
the measurement problem as well as aspects of various interpretations of
quantum theory. We turn next to discussions of these topics.
The Measurement Problem
If from the discussions above you have a sense of the unusual role measurement
plays in the standard approach to quantum theory, and a sense of the difﬁculties in
giving an account of what if anything distinguishes measurement devices from
nonmeasurement devices, then you already have a good general sense of the
measurement problem. My main goal here is to ﬂesh out this problem, and give an
idea of the different ways the measurement problem is often presented.
Subjectivity vs. objectivity

We saw above that measurement plays a central role in the standard mathemati­
cal approach to quantum theory, and a role unlike any that measurement plays in
any other basic science. As noted earlier, even if viewed purely instrumentally, the
role of measurement in the standard approach is a bit unusual. But from an
instrumentalist perspective, measurement is not a major problem, since there is
no question that the standard approach to quantum theory is extremely good at
making accurate predictions.
But if we try to interpret the standard approach realistically, we run into a
problem – or rather, we run into one way of looking at the problem. Recall from
the discussion above that whether something counts as a measurement – whether
something is or is not considered a measuring device – seems inherently
subjective, that is, it depends on our interests. But for the longest time we
have viewed our basic sciences – physics, chemistry, and the like – as sciences of
an objective world, of a world that is independent of us, a world that proceeds in
an objective manner independent of our interests. But with the standard approach
to quantum theory, it now seems we have a science (at least if viewed realistically)
in which our interests, our measurements, affect the way the world is. As I put it
earlier, if we choose to make a measurement the world will be one way, and if we
choose not to make a measurement the world will be a different way. And, again at

255

256

Recent Developments

least from a realist perspective, there seems to be something deeply troubling
about this.
The physicist John Bell (1928–90; we will look closely at one of Bell’s major
discoveries in the next chapter) put it as follows in a paper aptly titled “Against
‘Measurement’.” Incidentally, when Bell speaks of a “jump” he is referring to what,
above, we termed the collapse of the wave function (again, in the standard
approach, this occurs when a measurement takes place.)
What exactly qualiﬁes some physical systems to play the role of ‘measurer’?
Was the wavefunction of the world waiting to jump for thousands of
millions of years until a single-celled living creature appeared? Or did it
have to wait a little longer, for some better qualiﬁed system . . . with a PhD?
(Bell 1990, p. 34)
Bell is being somewhat sarcastic, of course, but his point should be clear. A
realistic interpretation of the standard approach seems to suggest that without
measurement, for example before the appearance of creatures capable of per­
forming measurements, the world would not have existed in any deﬁnite state.
And that a world with deﬁnite characteristics only came about once such
creatures arrived, and the world (or parts of it) is deﬁnite only when measure­
ments or observations are being made. Again, this runs strongly counter to widely
held views that our science is a science of an objective world, of an objective world
that exists and proceeds independently of the observations of creatures such as us.
Measurement contexts vs. nonmeasurement contexts

Another common way to present the measurement problem is in terms of
providing (or being unable to provide) a principled way of distinguishing
measurement contexts from nonmeasurement contexts. Recall that in a nonmeasurement context we apply Schrödinger’s equation, whereas in a measure­
ment context we cease applying Schrödinger’s equation and instead invoke the
collapse postulate. So the standard approach to quantum theory requires making
a distinction between measurement processes and nonmeasurement processes.
But recall our discussion from earlier in this chapter, where we emphasized that
there are no fundamental physical differences between objects we consider to be
measuring devices and those we do not consider to be measuring devices. In the
same way, there are no fundamental physical differences between contexts we
consider to be measurement contexts and those we do not. So the standard
approach to the mathematics of quantum theory relies on a distinction – that
between measurement contexts and nonmeasurement contexts – for which we
can provide no principled, objective justiﬁcation.
System vs. apparatus; macroscopic vs. microscopic levels

Another (though closely related) way to phrase the measurement problem is by
emphasizing that the standard approach presupposes a distinction between
system and apparatus. That is, it presupposes a distinction between the system
involving quantum entities, and the apparatus being used to perform a measure­
ment on that system. In the standard approach, the system is represented as

27 The Reality Question

evolving according to Schrödinger’s equation, whereas the measuring apparatus is
not represented as evolving according to this equation, but rather is viewed as
proceeding in a classical, Newtonian sort of way.
Or in slightly different terms, in a measurement involving a quantum system
the standard approach requires we distinguish between a micro-level system
consisting of quantum entities such as electrons, photons, and the like, and
macro-level measuring devices consisting of pointers, digital readouts, and the
like. Again, the former is represented as proceeding according to Schrödinger’s
equation; the latter is not.
But the same problem arises as we saw above, namely, there are no fundamental
physical differences between system and apparatus, or between micro and macro
levels. So there seems to be no objective, principled way of making the distinction
the standard approach requires.
Universality

A ﬁnal and closely related issue is worth mentioning brieﬂy, involving universal­
ity. Recall from earlier chapters that one of the shortcomings of Einstein’s special
theory of relativity was that it was not a universally applicable theory, but instead
could be applied only so long as certain special circumstances were met (the
situation had to involve only straight-line motion at uniform speed). And we saw
that Einstein spent the next 10 years overcoming this limitation, and eventually
developed a highly successful theory, the theory of general relativity, that applied
to all situations.
Likewise, one of the advantages of Newtonian physics over Aristotelian physics
was its universal applicability. Recall that within Aristotelian physics the account
of the movement of sublunar elements (the straight-line motion to an element’s
natural place) was different from the account of superlunar motion (the uniform,
circular motion of the element ether). The principles involved in Newtonian
physics, in contrast, were universally applicable principles. Universal gravitation is
called that precisely because it was viewed as applying universally, everywhere in
the universe. Likewise for the basic laws of motion. The universal applicability of
the basic principles of Newtonian physics was, and still is, viewed as one of the
major ways in which the Newtonian approach was so much better than the
Aristotelian approach.
This view that the principles of our fundamental theories should be universally
applicable has come to be seen as something we want from our basic theories. But I
think you can see the problem as it relates to the standard approach to quantum
theory. Schrödinger’s equation, which is at the heart of quantum theory, is not used
as a universal principle. Rather, it is applicable only within certain contexts. As we
saw above, we can describe these contexts in a variety of ways – as nonmeasurement
contexts, or as micro-level contexts, or as contexts involving systems as opposed to
devices used to measure those systems. But the bottom line is that in the standard
approach to quantum theory, Schrödinger’s equation, unlike the basic principles of
our fundamental sciences since Newton, does not universally apply. Or to put it in
slightly different terms, the standard approach gives up one of the most basic
aspects of our fundamental sciences – that the principles should apply universally –
that we have had since the advent of Newtonian science.

257

258

Recent Developments

Concluding thoughts on the measurement problem

The measurement problem is a deeply puzzling aspect of the standard approach
to quantum theory. As we saw above, there are a variety of ways to view the
measurement problem. But they all revolve around the same issue, namely, that in
the standard approach measurement plays a role unlike any that it has played in
any other basic science. And it plays a role that seems inappropriate for a basic
science. To again quote Bell:
Here are some words which, however legitimate and necessary in applica­
tion, have no place in a formulation with any pretension to physical
precision: system, apparatus, environment, microscopic, macroscopic,
reversible, irreversible, observable, information, measurement. (Bell 1990,
p. 34; emphasis his)
Note that Bell is not being critical of the standard approach when that approach
is taken instrumentally. Bell himself was a physicist whose daily work relied on
using the standard approach to quantum theory, and these words and concepts
are, he notes, necessary in applying quantum theory, for example, in solving
problems and making predictions about situations involving quantum entities.
And again, as a predictive science, the standard approach has been phenomenally
successful. Bell’s point, instead, is that if we want to take our basic sciences
realistically, such words and concepts should not be involved in the basic
formulation of any such science.
Recall that the measurement problem does not arise if one takes an instru­
mentalist attitude toward the standard approach to quantum theory. But if one
wishes to think about quantum theory through a realist lens, the measurement
