way to exploit the Bell/Aspect setup to allow for the sending of any information
between the locations. To see why, it might help to focus on a concrete example.
Suppose you are at detector A, perhaps in Tulsa, Oklahoma, and I am at
detector B, perhaps 2,500,000 light years away in the Andromeda galaxy. Suppose
I keep the dial on my detector in the R position. Looking back at scenarios 3 and 4
above, we know that, if you have the dial on your detector in its M position, there
is only a 25% chance that what my detector registers will be different from what
your detector registers. But if you put your dial in the L position, the odds of my
detector reading differently than yours jump to 75%. In short, you can substan­
tially inﬂuence, and instantaneously inﬂuence, the odds of whether our detectors
match or fail to match simply by switching your dial between M and L. Since you
have so much inﬂuence on what happens at my detector, it seems you should be
able to exploit this inﬂuence to send information to me instantaneously, thereby
showing Informational Locality to be false.
However, here is the problem. To send me a message, you need to be able to
inﬂuence whether I receive a D or a U on my tape. Even if you could only
inﬂuence the probability of my receiving a D or a U, this would be sufﬁcient to
send me a message. So no question about it: if you could manipulate your dial so
as to inﬂuence whether I received a D or a U, then you could send me a message.

28 EPR, Bell’s Theorem, and the Aspect Experiments

The problem is, you do not have this sort of inﬂuence. Recall that in this setup,
the readings on your detector as well as those on my detector will be a random
50:50 mix of Ds and Us. So you cannot inﬂuence the odds of a D or U appearing on
your detector, nor the odds of a D or U appearing on my detector (again, those
odds will always be 50:50). You do have instantaneous inﬂuence on my detector,
but that inﬂuence is restricted to inﬂuencing the probability of whether what my
detector says matches what your detector says. And that kind of inﬂuence is not
sufﬁcient to send a message.
In general, there seems to be no way to exploit the inﬂuence of Bell/Aspect
setups to send information to a distant location. So contrary to how it initially
seems, the Bell/Aspect experiments give us no reason to think that Informational
Locality is false.
As a ﬁnal note in this section, during much of the twentieth century it was
widely accepted that relativity ruled out any sort of faster-than-light inﬂuences. So
the results of the Bell/Aspect experiments seemed to contradict this aspect of
relativity. When those results were announced, researchers turned to a more
careful analysis of what exactly relativity prohibited. It turns out that, strictly
speaking, relativity only prohibits faster-than-light inﬂuences that can be used to
send information. So the fact that the Bell/Aspect results do not violate Infor­
mational Locality is what keeps these results from contradicting relativity.
Notably, the fact that, strictly speaking, these results do not contradict relativity
would have made little difference to Einstein. When Einstein spoke of spooky
action at a distance, his concern was with any sort of faster-than-light inﬂuence.
He was convinced, as probably most of us have been since at least the ancient
Greeks, that we inhabit a world that does not allow for action at a distance, that is,
that we live in a world that respects all forms of locality. As it turns out, we have
been wrong about this.

Concluding Remarks
In summary, the Bell/Aspect experiments clearly demonstrate that Einstein
Locality is mistaken. That is, the experiments show that there can be some
sort of inﬂuence between events at distant locations. These results likewise seem
to indicate that there can be instantaneous causal inﬂuence between events at
distant locations. However, although there is some sort of inﬂuence involved, it
seems that the Bell/Aspect results do not give us any reason to think that the
inﬂuence can be used to send information between distant locations.
This leaves us, then, with the question of what kind of inﬂuence it is. This is a
question for which we can, at last, give a precise answer. The answer to the
question “What kind of inﬂuence is it?” is this: no one has the foggiest idea.

285

286

29
Overview of the Theory of Evolution
In recent chapters we looked at ways in which new developments, most notably
involving relativity theory and quantum theory, are requiring us to rethink
substantially some basic and long-held assumptions about the sort of universe
we live in. In this chapter and the next we will be exploring relatively recent work
(from the mid-1800s to the present) concerning evolutionary theory. As with the
discoveries discussed in previous chapters, evolutionary theory also forces us to
rethink some common and long-held views.
The chapter is divided into two main sections. The main goal of the ﬁrst section
is to understand the basics of evolutionary theory. Included in this discussion are
the original discoveries of Darwin and Wallace in the mid-1800s, an overview of
more recent discoveries concerning evolution, and a discussion of one of the most
common misconceptions about evolution. The second main section is largely
historical, exploring the route by which Darwin and Wallace independently
arrived at the key insights behind evolutionary theory.

Overview of the Basics of Evolutionary Theory
Darwin’s and Wallace’s discovery: Evolution by natural selection
Charles Darwin (1809–82) and Alfred Russel Wallace (1823–1913) were the ﬁrst
to clearly and thoroughly articulate and (especially in the case of Darwin) defend
what is referred to as evolution by natural selection. As described later in the chapter,
the route by which Darwin and Wallace came to understand evolution was lengthy,
and it required them to overcome some basic and widespread presuppositions
common in their time – presuppositions largely rooted in older Aristotelian views.
Generally speaking, evolution refers to changes in a population over time.
During Darwin’s and Wallace’s time it was widely accepted that members of a
species shared a set of fundamental, deﬁning, and unchanging characteristics, and
it was also accepted that new species do not emerge. However, eventually Darwin
and Wallace independently became convinced that both of these widely accepted
views were mistaken. This left them with the question of what sort of natural
mechanism might result in fundamental changes in a population, and even result in
new species. Both independently discovered what Darwin would term natural
Worldviews: An Introduction to the History and Philosophy of Science, Third Edition.
Richard DeWitt.
 2018 Richard DeWitt. Published 2018 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.

29 Overview of the Theory of Evolution

selection. Although the route to Darwin’s and Wallace’s discovery was difﬁcult, once
recognized, the basic ideas behind evolution by natural selection can be stated quite
brieﬂy. Using modern terminology (as opposed to the phrasing used by Darwin and
Wallace), the key factors in the evolutionary process they discovered are these:
i) heritable variations
and
ii) differential ﬁtness.
Let’s take a minute to understand these. The idea behind heritable variations is
straightforward – it simply means that there are differences between members of
a population, and moreover, differences that can be passed down from one
generation to the next. Individual humans, for example, differ considerably from
one another, and many of those differences – eye color, hair color, and countless
others – are heritable, that is, they can be passed down to later generations.
With respect to the second ingredient, differential ﬁtness means that not all
members of the population are equally suited to survive and pass down their
traits in the environment in which they ﬁnd themselves. Consider a typical
biological population, say a particular population of deer living in a particular
area. Such a population will usually inhabit an environment in which there are
shortages of important resources such as food, suitable mating partners, and the
like. The environment will also usually include threats to their wellbeing and
survival, such as disease, accidents, predators, and the like. If the differences
between the heritable traits of members of this population are such that some
traits are more likely to aid an organism in surviving and reproducing in that
environment – perhaps some are more resistant to certain newly arisen
diseases, or the microbiome in the digestive system of some make them
more likely to survive with the limited food resources available during a
particularly harsh winter, and so on – then we would say that the population
exhibits differential ﬁtness. In short, the population is such that not all members
are equally suited to survive and reproduce in the environment in which they
ﬁnd themselves.
Darwin and Wallace recognized that if (i) and (ii) are present, and if the traits
responsible for the differential ﬁtness are heritable traits (which will generally but
not always be the case), then what Darwin termed natural selection will occur.
Darwin chose the term to emphasize the analogy with artiﬁcial selection. Artiﬁcial
selection refers to the process where breeders of animals or plants mate orga­
nisms with certain characteristics, characteristics desired by the breeder, with the
result that those characteristic become increasingly common in later generations.
Darwin and Wallace recognized that an analogous process can take place
through a natural mechanism of selection – natural selection. The basic idea is
relatively straightforward (though it was not easy to be the ﬁrst to recognize it): if
there are differences between members of a population where those differences
are heritable, and if the environment is such that certain of those heritable
features are more likely to result in an organism’s survival and reproduction in
that environment, then organisms with those features are more likely to pass such
features on to subsequent generations. In this way, a change in the population

287

288

Recent Developments

over time will result – certain features will be naturally selected and it is likely
those features will be increasingly prevalent in successive generations. In short,
evolutionary change will occur via an entirely natural mechanism.
As a ﬁnal note, Darwin and Wallace both recognized that if within a population
this process of natural selection continues for a long enough time, then changes in
a population can be so substantial that the result will be a population so different
from the original that it can properly be considered a new species. Thus the title of
Darwin’s groundbreaking book: On the Origin of Species by Means of Natural
Selection.
A brief overview of evolutionary theory since Darwin and Wallace
The history of the development of evolutionary theory since the time of Darwin
and Wallace is quite complex. Included in this are Mendel’s famous experiments
concerning hereditary factors in peas, the recognition of genes as “units” of
heredity, the discovery of chromosomes and the recognition that they are
involved in heredity, the subsequent discovery that chromosomes contain
DNA and the discovery of the molecular structure of DNA, the discovery of
the mechanism by which DNA codes for proteins, and in more recent years, the
ability to quickly ascertain the complete, molecule-by-molecule genetic structure
of a vast array of organisms, including the human genetic structure, and much,
much more. With respect to developments in evolutionary theory, the past
roughly 150 years have been remarkably productive.
The complete story of evolutionary discoveries since the time of Darwin and
Wallace is beyond the scope of this chapter. But I do want to note two relevant
items. The ﬁrst item concerns what is commonly termed the modern synthesis. In
the late 1800s and early 1900s there was an enormous amount of disagreement
and contention over central issues involving evolutionary theory, and in addition,
areas of biology relevant to evolutionary theory were working largely indepen­
dently of one another. But by the later twentieth century there was a synthesis of
an extraordinarily wide range of results and data, from ﬁeldwork involving studies
of populations in their natural environments, to controlled laboratory experi­
ments involving model organisms such as fruit ﬂies, to mathematical results
concerning evolution, work involving paleoanthropology (the study of extinct
human and related species) including the quite surprising recent discovery of
Neanderthal DNA, and many other areas. This synthesis – what is now widely
referred to as the modern synthesis – brought evolutionary theory into a uniﬁed
whole encompassing a wide array of ﬁelds of biological research. To cite an oftquoted phrase that comes from the title of a 1973 article by a key ﬁgure in
population genetics, Theodosius Dobzhansky (1900–75), “nothing in biology
makes sense except in the light of evolution.” Dobzhansky’s phrase, while perhaps
a bit of an overstatement, nonetheless nicely sums up how uniﬁed current areas of
biological research have become, with evolutionary theory as a core component.
As a second item before closing this section, we saw earlier that Darwin and
Wallace independently discovered natural selection as a mechanism behind
evolutionary change. However, since Darwin and Wallace several additional
factors contributing to evolutionary change have been discovered.

29 Overview of the Theory of Evolution

These additional factors are best understood in terms of a somewhat more
speciﬁc and more recent deﬁnition of evolution. We saw above that evolution has
long been understood as referring to changes in a population over time, where the
changes were generally understood to be changes in the overt characteristics of a
population. With the understanding of the role of genes in reproduction, and
especially the role of genes in the passing on of an organism’s heritable character­
istics, evolution is now commonly understood as referring to changes in the
genetic makeup of a population. More precisely, evolution is now generally
understood as changes in allele frequencies in a population over time.
The central idea behind this deﬁnition is straightforward. An allele is simply a
variant of a particular gene. For example, the single gene responsible for blood
type in humans has several variants, and these variants are referred to as the alleles
of that gene. (So what blood type you have depends on what variants you inherited
from your parents.) If a particular variant of a gene – an allele – becomes more
common or less common in a population over time, then evolution – change over
time – has occurred, though now we are focusing on changes in the genetic
makeup of a population.
The process of natural selection discovered by Darwin and Wallace is widely
recognized as an extremely important factor inﬂuencing evolution, that is,
inﬂuencing changes in the genetic makeup of a population over time. However,
several additional factors are now widely recognized as contributing to evolution,
these being gene ﬂow, genetic mutation, and genetic drift. Let’s look brieﬂy at
these additional factors.
Gene ﬂow (or gene migration, as it is also called) is perhaps most easily
understood by way of example. Recent evidence strongly suggests that at points
in the not too distant past – likely around 50,000–100,000 years ago – populations
of modern humans (our species) and populations of Neanderthals (a separate
human species that went extinct around 30,000–40,000 years ago) came to
inhabit the same regions. And they interbred. In doing so certain Neanderthal
gene variants became established in the genome (the genetic makeup) of modern
humans, and vice versa. Metaphorically speaking, there was a ﬂow of gene alleles
from one population to another, and this sort of process is referred to as gene ﬂow.
There are a wide variety of scenarios that may result in gene ﬂow. Different
populations of migrating animals, for example, often share similar feeding and
resting spots during migration. In so doing such populations may interbreed, with
the result that a population may have types of genes ﬂow into it that were not
previously present in that population. Gene ﬂow also affects plants, for example, a
population of plants may receive new gene variants by way of pollen that has been
carried a long way on the wind. In general, gene ﬂow refers to this sort of process,
that is, changes in the genetic makeup of a population due to the movement of
new gene variants into the population. And as noted, gene ﬂow is now widely
recognized as an important additional mechanism behind evolutionary change.
Genetic mutation is likewise now recognized as another factor in evolution.
Once the role of DNA in reproduction was understood, and the molecular
structure of DNA was established, it was found that mutations – changes in the
molecular structure of the DNA – may occur. Some such changes result from
internal processes. For example, the replication of DNA is an important factor in

289

290

Recent Developments

reproduction, and the replication process sometimes results in a slightly ﬂawed
copy – a mutation in the molecular structure of the DNA. Or external factors,
such as chemicals in the environment, radiation, and others, might also result in
an alteration of a portion of DNA. While many mutations are neutral, having no
appreciable effect on the organism, some are beneﬁcial in the sense of increasing
the likelihood of an organism’s survival and reproduction in its environment, and
some mutations are harmful. In this way, genetic mutation, often acting in
conjunction with natural selection, can likewise result in changes in the genetic
makeup of a population over time.
The ﬁnal factor to consider, what is termed genetic drift, is best approached by
way of analogy. The analogy differs in certain ways from processes involved in
genetic reproduction, but the analogy does convey the key idea behind genetic
drift. Suppose we have a container of 100 colored balls, where the percentages of
blue, red, and green balls are respectively 60%, 30%, and 10%. Suppose we
randomly choose 20 balls out of this container. Because the sample is random
it is unlikely to be exactly representative of the original frequencies of the colors;
instead, we can expect that the frequencies in our sample will have drifted from
the frequencies found in the original container.
With multiple generations, changes resulting from this sort of random drift
can become quite substantial. For example, Figure 29.1 contains a summary of a
quick computer simulation I just ran of the scenario above involving the
colored balls, but adding additional generations. GEN0 (generation 0) repre­
sents the original population of 100 colored balls with the 60%, 30%, and 10%
frequencies of blue, red, and green balls. GEN1 represents the frequencies
found in a random sampling of 20 balls from that original 100. For GEN2, we
again simulate a container of 100 balls, but this time having the frequencies of
blue, red, and green found in GEN1. Then we again randomly sample 20 balls
from this container, with the results summarized in GEN2 below. Then we
repeat the process for GEN3, GEN4, and GEN5. The results are summarized in
Figure 29.1.
Notice how dramatic the drift is. In only ﬁve generations we have gone from
frequencies of 60, 30, and 10% to frequencies of 80, 0, and 20%. Again it is
worth emphasizing that this drift in frequencies is entirely due to random
processes. The red balls, for example, had a particularly bad bit of luck

Figure 29.1 Results of genetic drift simulation.

29 Overview of the Theory of Evolution

between GEN2 and GEN3, and by GEN4 the red balls had entirely disappeared
from the population. If this had been an allele – a gene variant – then this
would represent an allele entirely disappearing from a population. And
barring the reintroduction of that allele, perhaps by gene ﬂow, that allele
is forever gone from the population.
Although the process of genetic reproduction is more complex than the simple
scenario involving the colored balls, it nonetheless involves the same sort of
sampling, though in the case of genetic reproduction the sampling involves alleles.
Whereas with the colored balls we saw drift resulting in a change in the
frequencies of colors over time (that is, over successive generations), with
biological populations we can expect drift to result in a change in allele
frequencies in a population over time. Or in other words, drift will result in
evolution in that population.
A few additional notes are in order before closing our discussion of genetic
drift. First, population size and the relative size of samples tend to have a
substantial effect on the amount of drift we would expect to occur. The larger
the population, and the larger the sample size, the less drift we would expect.
Likewise, smaller populations, as well as smaller sample sizes, are more likely to
result in dramatic drift. Notice that the sample size in the colored balls scenario
was 20% (that is, we sampled 20 balls out of a total of 100), which is relatively
small, and thus the somewhat dramatic drift we saw was not that surprising.
Nonetheless, as long as there are gene variants (different alleles) in a population,
then unless a sample size is 100% (which for all practical purposes will not happen
in biological populations), genetic drift will occur. And as noted, genetic drift is
now understood to be an important mechanism in evolution.
It is also worth noting that it is difﬁcult (perhaps for all practical purposes
impossible) to determine how much change in a population is due to genetic drift.
This is because changes in a population over time due to genetic drift, versus
changes due to natural selection, are difﬁcult (perhaps practically impossible) to
distinguish. Thus, while there is widespread recognition that both natural
selection and genetic drift play important roles in evolution, there is some
disagreement over how much of a role each plays.
Finally, two subtypes of genetic drift are worth noting, usually termed bottle­
neck effects and founder effects. Both are, in essence, cases where a sample size is
exceptionally small, at least temporarily. As an example of a bottleneck effect,
consider a scenario where a natural disaster results in almost all members of a
particular population being killed. The disaster has, in essence, resulted in an
exceptionally small sample size. As we saw above, an exceptionally small sample
size can be expected to lead to a dramatic amount of drift, and thus it is likely that
the frequencies of traits (or more precisely, the frequency of alleles) in subsequent
generations will differ considerably from the frequency found in the original
population.
As an example of a founder effect, suppose a small number of members of a
population move to a different location, no longer interbreeding with members of
the original population. We once again are dealing with what is an exceptionally
small sample size, though the reason for the small sample is different from the

291

292

Recent Developments

reasons found in cases of bottlenecks. But just as in the bottleneck effect, the allele
frequencies in subsequent generations will likely differ considerably from those
found in the original population.
Darwin’s and Wallace’s discovery of evolution by natural selection was an
extraordinarily important discovery. As noted, the past roughly 150 years have
been a remarkably productive period of research and discovery in ﬁelds related
to evolutionary theory. An enormous amount has been learned about the
additional mechanisms of evolution just discussed, the molecular basis of
heredity, our evolutionary past, and much more. As with relativity theory
and quantum theory, these discoveries have substantial implications for views
we have long held. We will explore some of those implications in the next
chapter, but ﬁrst let’s brieﬂy consider a common misconception concerning
evolutionary theory, and a few historical notes on the route to Darwin’s and
Wallace’s discovery.
A word of caution
There are many, many misconceptions about evolution. Most seem to stem from
a lack of understanding of even the basics of evolutionary theory. But there is one
misconception that I ﬁnd quite often even among those who understand most of
the basic points about evolution, including the key aspects of evolution discussed
above. It is worth taking a minute to discuss this misconception.
Let’s begin with a question one hears not uncommonly: If evolutionary theory
is correct, then why haven’t other animals evolved the sort of traits that humans
have? For example, why haven’t other species developed large brains, sophisti­
cated language, advanced tools, advanced intelligence, and so on? As is so often
the case, it is important to recognize the presuppositions a certain question or
approach makes. In this case, note the only way to make sense of such questions
is to recognize that they presuppose that certain traits such as intelligence,
language, tool use, and so on, are somehow inherently better traits, traits that, if
evolution is correct, we should then expect the evolutionary process to select
for such traits. And thus, the suggestion goes, if evolution is correct, then
organisms should be expected to evolve in the direction of such traits. At
bottom, this (mistaken) view of evolution is of evolution as a process that
pushes organisms in a certain direction, in particular in the direction of
acquiring traits that are inherently “better,” or inherently “higher,” or inherently
“more advanced.”
But evolution is no such thing. The traits that result from natural selection are
the traits that helped organisms survive and reproduce in the environment in
which they found themselves, whatever those traits might be. In this context there
are no traits that are inherently “better” or “worse,” no traits that are inherently
more advanced or less advanced, no species with traits that make them “higher” or
“lower” species in any meaningful sense of these words. There are just traits that
happened to have worked, for the population in question, in the environment in
which that population found itself.
For us, modern humans, certain traits have worked, such as upright posture,
large brains, the ability to harness ﬁre, sophisticated tool use, and more. But those

29 Overview of the Theory of Evolution

traits give no assurance of survival. This is easy to see by considering that there
have been many species of humans over the past roughly two million years
(ranging from about six to 15 or more human species, depending on how ﬁnely
one makes distinctions). Our kin species of humans had the same general traits as
us, but they all went extinct. In short, the overwhelming majority of species
sharing our general traits did not survive.
Evolution is not a process that moves in some preferred direction. To put it in
terms we have used earlier, evolutionary changes in a population are not due to a
teleological, goal-driven process. Rather, evolution is at bottom a goalless, mecha­
nistic process. As noted, mistaking evolution for a goal-directed, teleological
process is exceedingly common. Just yesterday, for example, in a respected
publication I saw this headline: “Sherpas Evolved to Live and Work at Altitude.”
But Sherpas did no such thing. The population of Sherpas did not develop traits in
order to survive; rather, some members survived (and reproduced) because they
had certain traits. And thus those traits became more prevalent in that population.
In my college days, a biology professor teaching a course on genetics hammered
into us the slogan that populations do not adapt to survive; they survive because
they adapt. I have to admit that at the time I did not fully understand the point.
Later, as I came to better understand and better appreciate the differences
between teleological processes and mechanistic processes, and teleological views
of how the universe works versus mechanistic views, I came to understand the
professor’s point – that evolution is not a goal-directed, teleological process, but is
instead a natural, mechanistic process.

Darwin’s and Wallace’s Paths to Natural Selection
As noted, the original key discovery of evolution by natural selection, the
combination of what we generally term heritable variations and differential
ﬁtness, is most famously associated with Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel
Wallace. In this section we explore how they came to recognize the fundamental
ingredients behind evolution by natural selection.
The main focus of this section is historical. If one is mainly interested in just the
basics of evolutionary theory, as described in the sections above, one can skip the
remainder of this chapter and move on to the next. However, Darwin’s and
Wallace’s discovery is of major importance, and I think it is worth understanding,
at least in outline, how that discovery came about. It also serves as another
example of a trend we have seen earlier in this book, namely, how major new
discoveries often require overcoming presuppositions that are common in one’s
time.
The development of Darwin’s views
In the early 1830s, Darwin accepted an offer to sail with the HMS Beagle in what
would be a long (ﬁve-year) journey around the world. This young Darwin began
the trip with reasonably standard beliefs for his time. Most notable among these
beliefs were (i) that God (for Darwin, this would have been the Christian God) had

293

294

Recent Developments

created all species, (ii) that species had essential characteristics that made them
the species they were, (iii) that species were immutable, that is, species did not
change their essential characteristics over time, and (iv) that new species did not
appear.
It is worth noting that these beliefs, with the exception of the ﬁrst, were
largely rooted in the Aristotelian tradition. Recall that on the Aristotelian
tradition, natural objects had a set of internal, essential characteristics that
made them what they were, and this would apply to biological species as well.
We noted in an earlier chapter ways in which various branches of science,
following the adoption of Newtonian physics in the late 1600s and early 1700s,
had become “Newtonized,” in the sense of moving away from the Aristotelian
approach and moving more toward a Newtonian approach. Biology would also
eventually move in this direction, but it came more slowly, and in fact the
development of evolutionary theory was a major factor in this transition. At the
time of Darwin and Wallace, these Aristotelian-based beliefs about species were
still well entrenched. As we will see shortly, overcoming this Aristoteliangrounded view of species would be a major breakthrough for both Darwin and
Wallace.
Back to the Beagle: while serving on this voyage, Darwin made extensive
observations and notes, and collected a huge number of specimens and fossils. His
specimens and observations eventually led him to recognize that organisms
display an extraordinary array of characteristics. This applied even within species,
that is, different members of the same species display an astonishing array of
variation. We can see that this recognition is leading Darwin away from the
standard view that members of a species share a set of essential characteristics.
Upon returning to England Darwin began a series of notebooks, which he
developed for about the next ﬁve years (roughly the last half of the 1830s), in
which he began exploring the idea that species might undergo “transmutation.” In
these notebooks we can see that Darwin is now also becoming convinced that new
species can and do come into existence. In short, Darwin is now doubting the key
presuppositions mentioned above, that is, he no longer accepts that species have
essential characteristics, that species are immutable, and that new species cannot
come into existence.
But this left Darwin with a substantial problem: How? How could members of a
single species come to have such variation? How could new species come about?
A well-known book at the time would help Darwin come to an important insight.
In the 1830s Darwin read Thomas Malthus’ (1766–1834) Essay on the Principle of
Population. Malthus noted that plants and animals, including humans, tend to
reproduce beyond what their environment can support. Malthus was interested in
different issues than Darwin, including arguing for certain social policies, but
Darwin recognized how Malthus’ work could help address the problem on which
he was focused.
The fact that organisms breed in greater numbers than the environment can
support would result in what Darwin termed a “struggle for existence.” That is,
not all organisms can survive in the environment in which they ﬁnd themselves. If
the variations Darwin had come to appreciate during his years on the Beagle
impacted an organism’s chances of survival, the result, he realized, would be

29 Overview of the Theory of Evolution

differences in the survival and reproductive success of individuals having different
variations.
Although he used different terms than we do now, Darwin was recognizing that
heritable variations within a population, coupled with differential ﬁtness (that is,
that different variations affect the odds of an organism surviving, or roughly,
Darwin’s “struggle for existence”), would result in a population undergoing
change over time. And from this it is not a huge step to realize that, given
sufﬁcient time, the slow accumulation of changes resulting from heritable
variations combined with a struggle for existence could result in substantial
changes in organisms, substantial enough to result in a population of organisms
we would classify as a new species.
As mentioned earlier, this process is what Darwin would later term natural
selection. Again, the idea behind this terminology is straightforward: breeders
practice artiﬁcial selection by breeding animals with desirable traits, with the
result being that later populations can differ quite substantially from earlier
populations. In a way somewhat analogous, in nature there is a natural selection
process. But whereas artiﬁcial selection is a goal-directed process, where breeders
try to assure that certain traits are found in later generations, the selection process
found in nature is a natural process, a mechanism by which certain traits come to
be represented more frequently in later populations. And whereas the sort of
selection performed by breeders can appropriately be termed artiﬁcial selection,
the selection produced by the natural process, involving heritable variations
coupled with differential ﬁtness, can appropriately be termed natural selection.
In short, by about 1840 Darwin had found a natural, nondivine mechanistic
process that explained how populations of organisms can undergo substantial
change, and also explained how new species can appear. He had, he was sure, hit
upon an extremely important idea.
But . . . Darwin did not publish. For that matter, he shared his important idea
with only a small handful of trusted friends. He did, in 1844, complete a short (by
his standards) work of just under 200 pages, explaining his key ideas and
providing arguments and evidence in support of those views. But Darwin did
not intend the manuscript to be published, at least, not while he was alive. Instead,
Darwin stashed the manuscript away with a note to his wife, asking her to see to its
publication should he die unexpectedly.
It would be 20 years from the time he ﬁrst recognized the role of the key
ingredients behind evolution by natural selection to the time he ﬁnally published
his ideas. During this period, through the late 1840s and much of the 1850s,
Darwin continued to work incessantly (Darwin was always, up to the day he died,
at work on one project or another). Much of this work would eventually
contribute in important ways, most notably by providing him with a large store
of empirical data to support his eventual writings on evolution.
In short, by the time Darwin did get around to publishing his big idea, he was
able to bring an enormous amount of data to bear on the subject. And it is exactly
this wealth of data in support of his ideas that sets Darwin off from all others.
Whereas others – most notably Wallace – might have independently come up
with the key ideas we call heritable variations and differential ﬁtness, Darwin had
both the ideas and the data to support it.

295

296

Recent Developments

The development of Wallace’s views
In the late 1840s, while Darwin was engaged in various other projects, Alfred
Russel Wallace was beginning the ﬁrst of multiple voyages that would be, in many
respects, reminiscent of Darwin’s voyage on the Beagle. The differences between
Darwin and Wallace, however, are notable. Unlike Darwin, Wallace did not come
from a wealthy, established family. He lacked the connections needed to get into a
university, and for that matter, he could not have afforded a university education.
Unlike Darwin, Wallace had to scrape by, and he did so largely by collecting
specimens to send back to England for sale to wealthy collectors.
Besides lacking the advantages that Darwin had, Wallace was also just unlucky.
For example, after four years of traveling, ﬁlling notebooks on his observations,
collecting specimens and the like, the ship on which Wallace was returning to
England caught ﬁre and sank, along with most of his specimens (save those he had
shipped back earlier) and most of his notebooks containing an enormous amount
of data.
Yet Wallace persevered. And very much like Darwin, during these voyages
Wallace was struck by the extraordinary range of variation he observed among
organisms, including organisms that according to the received view should have
been uniﬁed by a core of essential characteristics. Wallace was beginning to think,
as Darwin had earlier, that populations of organisms can undergo substantial
changes over time, and that new species might emerge.
As had earlier been the case for Darwin, Wallace did not at this time have an
account of how such changes might occur, or of how new species might emerge.
However, he became sufﬁciently convinced that new species do emerge that he
published a short paper, in 1855, saying as much. In essence, as had earlier been
the case for Darwin, at this point Wallace has only the ﬁrst piece of the puzzle, that
is, a recognition that heritable variations are somehow important.
According to Wallace’s account, the second key ingredient came to him in early
1858 while laid up with a malarial fever during another voyage. Wallace says that
during this fever it suddenly came to him that a “struggle for existence” (basically
differential ﬁtness), coupled with the variation he had already noted, would
provide a natural explanation for changes in populations over time. And, like
Darwin, he recognized that this mechanism would provide an explanation for
how new species can appear.
After recovering from the fever, Wallace quickly wrote out a short (roughly 20­
page) explanation of the key ideas. And, in a fascinating twist of history, Wallace
chose to mail the paper to Darwin. I say this is a “fascinating twist” because
Wallace could have had no way of knowing that Darwin shared similar ideas, or
even that Darwin was at all sympathetic to such ideas, much less that Darwin had
been convinced of these ideas for 20 years.
Wallace seems to have sent the paper to Darwin mainly because of Darwin’s
connections. Recall that Wallace and Darwin moved in very different social
circles. Darwin was on close terms with the most prominent names in British
science. Wallace was on close terms with no one of that sort. So Wallace, in his
cover letter with his paper, asked Darwin if he could pass the paper on to these
prominent ﬁgures.

29 Overview of the Theory of Evolution

Darwin’s receipt of Wallace’s paper was, to say the least, upsetting. The title of
Wallace’s paper itself says a lot: “On the Tendencies of Varieties to Depart
Indeﬁnitely from the Original Type.” In this short paper, Wallace does an
excellent job of characterizing both the key ingredients behind evolution by
natural selection. With respect to heritable variations, Wallace argues that
populations can exhibit variations that are indeﬁnitely different from ancestors
of those populations. With respect to the second key ingredient, what we term
differential ﬁtness, Wallace even uses the same phrase as Darwin, that is, Wallace
independently chose to characterize this as a “struggle for existence.” In short,
Darwin’s and Wallace’s key ideas, as articulated in Wallace’s paper and in
Darwin’s earlier unpublished writings, are so close as to be virtually
indistinguishable.
It was a tricky situation, especially for Darwin. Darwin clearly had the key ideas
earlier than Wallace, but Darwin had not prepared anything for publication.
Wallace, in contrast, clearly intended his paper to be published, and as soon as
possible. To make a long story short, the tricky situation was more or less
resolved, more or less to everyone’s eventual satisfaction, when some of Darwin’s
friends arranged to have Wallace’s paper, along with Darwin’s 1844 manuscript
and a new summary prepared by Darwin, presented together in late 1858 at a
meeting of a London scientiﬁc society. This 1858 presentation was the ﬁrst public
presentation of the key ideas of evolutionary theory.
However, this 1858 presentation of Wallace’s and Darwin’s key ideas made little
impact and caused little discussion. Shortly after this, Darwin set to work on an
extended presentation and defense of these key ideas, in a work he would title On
the Origin of Species by Means of Natural Selection.
Darwin’s On the Origin of Species
As noted, immediately following the 1858 presentation of his and Wallace’s
theory, Darwin went to work on a manuscript suitable for publication. This would
turn out to be an extraordinarily inﬂuential book, and the main goal of this section
is to provide an overview of this work.
During various periods over the preceding 10 years, Darwin had worked on a
detailed, scholarly, extremely thorough presentation of his key ideas together with
a detailed presentation, drawing on decades of his research, of empirical data
supporting his views. This manuscript, which Darwin sometimes referred to as his
“Big Book,” had grown to hundreds of dense pages, and was far from complete.
Darwin wisely took a new approach and started on a new, less dense presentation
of his ideas, and one aimed at a wider audience. This work would be completed
and published toward the end of 1859, and would be titled On the Origin of Species
by Means of Natural Selection (now standardly referred to as just the Origin of
Species, or sometimes just the Origin).
It is one thing to articulate a theory clearly, as Wallace did in his 20-page 1858
paper, and another thing to defend and provide compelling arguments for the
theory. The Origin of Species is, to my mind, as pivotal a work as Newton’s
Principia, discussed in earlier chapters. Much as Newton provided a slow,
accumulating amount of support for his key ideas, such that by the end of the

297

298

Recent Developments

Principia you have been treated to an impressive treatment of the explanatory
power of the new ideas, so also is the case with Darwin’s Origin of Species. In the
last chapter, Darwin describes his book as “one long argument,” and in this long
argument Darwin, like Newton, presents the key ideas slowly and carefully. The
cumulative effect is much like that of the Principia, in that by the end you have
seen an impressive display of the explanatory power of these new ideas.
Of the 14 chapters comprising the Origin of Species, it is the ﬁrst four that
contain the key points focused on above. Darwin focuses the ﬁrst chapter,
“Variation under Domestication,” on the almost entirely uncontroversial subject
of artiﬁcial selection, that is, of deliberate cultivation of traits in domestic animals
by means of selective breeding. In this chapter he manages to emphasize, using
well-known examples, the surprising range of variations found within domestic
animals, and how almost endless variations can be produced by artiﬁcial selection.
The next chapter, “Variation under Nature,” is focused on establishing that
there is in fact an astonishing array of heritable variation among wild populations
of plants and animals. Here as elsewhere, Darwin is able to draw upon decades of
his extensive observations and note-taking to establish the degree of variation
found in the wild.
In the third chapter, “Struggle for Existence,” he focuses on what we refer to as
differential ﬁtness. Here too the reasoning and evidence is convincing, so by the
third chapter, Darwin has provided compelling reasons and evidence supporting
that in typical populations we ﬁnd both a wide range of heritable variations and
differential ﬁtness.
As noted earlier in this chapter, any time you have a situation in which these
two ingredients are present, change in a population over time will occur. And in
the fourth chapter, “Natural Selection,” Darwin makes this explicit. He does so
largely by making explicit the analogy between natural selection and the artiﬁcial
selection discussed in the ﬁrst chapter. That is, in just the same way that artiﬁcial
selection produces a wide array of changes within populations of domestic
animals, so too we should expect natural selection to result in a wide range of
changes in populations among wild organisms. And, again drawing on his
experiences and data, he argues that this account explains better than any other
the relationships we see among organisms in the wild.
In short, by the end of the fourth chapter, Darwin has presented a compelling
case that natural selection must occur, and that its effects will be similar to the
effects of artiﬁcial selection, namely, the production of organisms that can differ
to almost any extent from their earlier ancestors. The remainder of the book deals
with a variety of topics, such as objections to the theory, the issue of the age of the
Earth and whether there has been sufﬁcient geological time for small changes to
accumulate sufﬁciently so as to result in the range of organisms we see today, the
issue of the incompleteness of the fossil record, and the like.
As stressed earlier, the key ideas of Wallace and Darwin ran counter to
some deeply entrenched beliefs of the time. The scientiﬁc community needed
a work such as the Origin of Species to provide a compelling case that these
long-held, deeply entrenched views were mistaken. And only Darwin, with his
range of experiences and extensive collection of data, could have written such
a book.

29 Overview of the Theory of Evolution

The reception of the Origin of Species

The Origin of Species sold exceptionally well, and Darwin oversaw six editions of
the work during his life, each with multiple printings. In addition, the work was
translated into a number of languages other than English. The book quickly
became very well known.
However, throughout the remainder of the 1800s, and to some extent into the
ﬁrst decades of the twentieth century as well, Darwin’s key ideas were only partly
accepted. Darwin’s views that evolution occurs, that is, that populations of
organisms undergo change over time, and that new species emerge, were widely
accepted. This itself is a substantial accomplishment, given the standard belief
prior to and during Darwin’s time that species are immutable and new species do
not come into existence.
What may come as a surprise is that Darwin’s and Wallace’s view that natural
selection is the mechanism by which evolution occurs was widely rejected during
the last half of the 1800s. As it turns out, Darwin and Wallace were entirely
correct about natural selection being a key mechanism behind evolution. The
complete story of why, in the late 1800s, natural selection was widely rejected as
the primary mechanism behind evolution is beyond the scope of this chapter.
Sufﬁce it to say that part of the reason had to do with a lack of understanding of
the means of inheritance, part had to do with the widespread appeal of Lamarck­
ian views of evolution (roughly, the idea that traits acquired during an organism’s
life could be passed down), and more. As usual, for those interested in pursuing
this further, some suggestions are provided in the Chapter Notes.
Eventually, a variety of discoveries, notably including Mendel’s pea-breeding
experiments strongly suggesting that heritable traits, rather than being blended in
later generations, were instead passed down via some sort of “unit” of inheritance
(units that would later be termed genes); an important proof published in 1930
showing that, contrary to a widely accepted view, Mendel’s discoveries about units
of inheritance were fully compatible with Darwin’s and Wallace’s view of natural
selection as a key mechanism behind evolution; the discovery in the 1950s of the
structure of DNA and, in subsequent years, the ﬂeshing out of the role of DNA in
genetic inheritance; and much more, would show that Darwin and Wallace pretty
much got it right from the beginning. It is now widely understood that natural
selection is a key factor behind evolution, and that this process, over immense
lengths of time, is responsible for the wide variety of species we see today.

Concluding Remarks
It is difﬁcult to overstate how much we have learned about our origins, and about
the origins of life in general, over the past 150 years. These are remarkable times.
In this chapter we have focused primarily on relatively straightforward and
uncontroversial topics, especially the basics of evolutionary theory and the route
by which Darwin and Wallace came to recognize the role of natural selection. As
is well known, evolutionary theory raises some difﬁcult and controversial issues,
largely issues of a more philosophical and conceptual nature. In the next chapter,
we turn to an exploration of some of these difﬁcult issues.

299

300

30
Reﬂections on Evolution
In the previous chapter we focused on relatively uncontroversial topics. We focused
mainly on the basics of evolutionary theory and the route by which Darwin and
Wallace came to their discoveries. In the roughly 150 years since Darwin’s and
Wallace’s work, the broad outlines of evolutionary theory have become extremely
well conﬁrmed. There is no serious doubt that we, as with all current life on Earth,
got here via a long evolutionary process. The recognition of this has implications for
a variety of issues that are, to put it mildly, rather touchy – religion, for one, ethics for
another, and much more.
The potential implications of evolution are sufﬁciently broad and touch upon
so many topics as to make it impractical, in a chapter such as this, to consider
them all. I have opted to divide the chapter into three general sections. The ﬁrst
focuses on the implications of evolution for religious views; the second focuses on
implications for morality and ethics; and the third presents some empirical
studies that shed light on the likely evolutionary advantages and possible origins
of some of our most basic behaviors.
As this chapter is rather long, each section is self-contained. That is, nothing in
any section presupposes familiarity with material from the other sections.
Depending on your interests, you may wish to focus on all or on only one or
two of the topics. We will begin with a look at implications our understanding of
evolution has for common religious views.

Implications for Religion
The question of whether evolution leaves any room for belief in a religious sort of
deity has received a good deal of attention, especially in recent years. On the one
hand are a number of scholars who, in a nutshell, maintain that any traditional
notion of a creator God, one involved in and inﬂuencing the everyday workings of
the universe, and any traditional notion of humans occupying a special place in a
universe with any sort of overall purpose to it, has been shown by science in
general and evolution in particular to be at least superﬂuous if not almost
certainly false. In contrast, some scholars have argued that one can fully accept
Darwinian evolution and natural science, and still hold to a belief in a God who is
Worldviews: An Introduction to the History and Philosophy of Science, Third Edition.
Richard DeWitt.
 2018 Richard DeWitt. Published 2018 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.

30 Reﬂections on Evolution

involved in meaningful ways in what goes on in the universe, and in a universe
with a purpose and one in which humans, at least in some sense, are special. My
goal in this section is to provide a sense of the arguments presented on both sides
of this issue. At bottom, the question at the core of this debate is this: Does taking
evolutionary theory seriously leave any room for anything like the traditional
western view of God?
Dennett, Dawkins, Weinberg, and others: “no”
A number of prominent scholars from a variety of disciplines, including physics,
biology, and philosophy of science, have argued that the answer to the above
question is a clear “no.” Such scholars would include Daniel Dennett, Richard
Dawkins, E. O. Wilson, Stephen Weinberg, and many others.
For such scholars, our best empirical theories, especially evolutionary theory,
leave no room for anything like the view of God common in the western world.
For example, consider the idea of a creator God who planned the universe with a
sort of detailed blueprint, with a particular focus on the development of life and
especially human life, and then implemented that plan. Such a blueprint-like plan
for the universe and for life in the universe is directly counter to what we have
discovered about the evolutionary origins of life. Thus, a belief in this sort of a
creator God with a blueprint-like plan for the universe and for the development of
life is incompatible with modern science, especially evolutionary theory.
Nor would it be acceptable to have a God that, instead of implementing a
detailed sort of blueprint, played only a kind of minimalist role in the development
of species. It is not uncommon to hear that one can fully accept both evolution
and a God that is importantly involved in the development of life by accepting that
God only occasionally interferes with the natural evolutionary process. The idea is
that this God sometimes steps in to push the evolutionary process in a certain
direction, for example, a direction that assured that humans would develop.
The problem with this view, these and other scholars argue, is that fully
accepting evolution requires fully accepting the central idea of evolution. Con­
sider the central idea of natural selection. The “natural” part of natural selection is
core. So fully accepting evolution requires accepting it as a natural process – no
supernatural forces or supernatural inﬂuences allowed. If one allows for a God to
step into and alter this process, then it is no longer natural selection – it is
supernatural selection.
More generally, the common conception of a God who intervenes and affects
the course of everyday events, say by answering prayers, is not compatible with
taking natural science as a natural process. Science since the 1600s has amply
demonstrated that the universe unfolds according to natural principles, and as
noted, developments in evolutionary theory over the past 150 years show that this
applies to the way life has unfolded as well. Thus, fully accepting the account
provided by modern science, and as regards life the account provided by modern
evolutionary theory, leaves no room for a God who intervenes in and affects the
course of everyday events. Since the beginnings of natural science around 600 BCE,
natural science has had at its core the central notion that the world proceeds
according to natural principles. Thus accepting supernatural inﬂuences is not

301

302

Recent Developments

compatible with the most important characteristic of natural science, nor is it
compatible with the most important feature of evolution by natural selection.
As a corollary to what has already been said, the evolutionary account is
incompatible with the view that humans are special in any interesting sense of the
word. The evolutionary account entails that humans are just one of millions of
species currently still existing, but as discussed in the previous chapter, there is no
meaningful sense in which humans, or human traits, are any “higher” or better or
more special than, say, the traits of dust mites that allow them to survive for long
periods of time on a few ﬂakes of dead skin. In short, accepting the evolutionary
account requires us to give up the belief, common in traditional western religion,
that humans might be special in any interesting sense of the word.
In summary, the scholars whose views we have been discussing argue that
evolutionary theory provides an important ﬁnal piece of a picture that has been
developing for some time. In particular, evolutionary theory provides a natural
explanation for the last remaining phenomenon that seemed previously to call for
a supernatural explanation, that is, evolutionary theory provides a completely
natural mechanism that accounts for the complexities we ﬁnd in living things. At
this point, then, in a scientiﬁcally informed and intellectually honest worldview
there is no longer any room left for a belief in a God with the key characteristics
typically associated with the western view of God, nor any room for belief in a
universe with any large-scale grand purpose.
These scholars emphasize that the sorts of questions we are concerned with
here – questions about the origin of the universe, about the way events in the
universe unfold, about the development of life – are empirical questions. As
empirical questions, we should look to the empirical evidence, and our best
supported empirical theories, to decide on the most reasonable views about such
matters. And if the empirical evidence and our best empirical theories suggest, as
they seem to, that there cannot be a God of the sort common in western religions,
then so be it. We have to accept this, and move on.
Haught, process philosophy, and process theology
The reasoning above seems compelling. But some scholars have argued that one
can, in an intellectually honest way, fully accept natural science in general and
evolutionary theory in particular, and still believe in a God that has, at least in
some sense, some of the important characteristics envisioned by most western
religions. The main goal of this section is to outline the views of one such scholar,
who in recent years has articulated this position more thoroughly than most.
John Haught is a modern theologian, well acquainted with evolutionary theory
and willing to fully accept the general correctness of the evolutionary account.
Haught would agree with a good deal of what has been said in the previous two
sections. For example, he would agree that the “natural” part of natural science
and natural selection is core. If one is to fully accept the natural part of natural
science and natural selection, Haught argues, one must accept that modern
science, especially evolutionary theory, leaves no room for a God that interferes
with the evolutionary process or that directly interferes with the natural workings
of the universe.

30 Reﬂections on Evolution

Likewise, Haught agrees that modern science, and evolutionary theory in
particular, leaves no room for belief in a universe that develops according to a
detailed, blueprint-like plan. And he also accepts that humans cannot be viewed
as special in the way western religions have traditionally viewed humans as
special. In particular, humans cannot be thought of as the intended product of the
evolutionary process.
In short, Haught certainly thinks modern science, especially evolutionary
theory, has substantial implications for religious views. And he thinks such
considerations call for substantial changes in the way God has been conceived
in past centuries. But he sees the changes required by taking evolution seriously as
being good changes. Haught argues that careful consideration of the implications
of evolution leads to a conception of God that is better than any the western world
has had previously. Thus, Haught speaks of “Darwin’s gift to theology.” Haught’s
views are subtle and nuanced, and I should note that what follows is but an
overview of some aspects of those views.
First, Haught argues that giving up the typical notion of a creator God who,
working from a detailed blueprint, produced a ﬁnished (or nearly ﬁnished)
universe ages ago, is a good thing to give up. To get an idea of his reasoning
here, consider the following analogy. Suppose I decide to build something, for the
sake of the discussion suppose it is a small pool for our back deck, built from
natural stone and maybe with a nice fountain in the center. I acquire some
blueprints, assemble the materials, and over the course of some days or weeks
build it. Once I am ﬁnished, what is left to do? My wife and I would presumably
enjoy it, probably a lot at ﬁrst and less so as the years went on and the novelty wore
off. We could perhaps stock the pool with some goldﬁsh or similar species, and
occasionally restock the pool as needed. But in general, with this sort of creation,
one that is essentially completed shortly after it is begun, there is not much to do
once the creation is ﬁnished.
This sort of creation seems to be a common view among adherents of typical
western religions. For example, recent polls indicate that in the United States
close to half the population believes the creation of the universe was begun and
ﬁnished by God within the last 10,000 years, with the creation process being
essentially completed at the beginning. And if one asks what goes on in the world
following this initial act of creation, a common view is of the world as a sort of
testing place where one proves, for example, one’s worthiness for salvation.
Haught does not describe it quite this way, but one gets the sense that he thinks
this notion of a God, one who produced a ﬁnished universe shortly after the
beginning of creation, with subsequent time spent with the world as a testing
place, is, well . . . boring. It is just not a very interesting conception of God or of
creation. God creates and ﬁnishes the universe at the outset, then century after
century after century the same thing happens. People are born, are tested, they
pass the test or not, and then the same process is repeated over and over and over.
This is not an interesting conception of God, or of creation, or of the overall
purpose of the world. And this is, I think, part of what Haught has in mind when
he speaks of Darwin providing a gift to theology. Darwin, Haught says, has forced
theologians to rethink these big questions about God, creation, and the purpose of
the universe.

303

304

Recent Developments

Haught envisions a very different sort of God and different sort of creative
process. He argues that this differently conceived God, and this creative process,
are not only compatible with modern science but also more interesting and more
in keeping with key principles underlying western religion. His theological views
have ties to the work of the philosopher Alfred North Whitehead (1861–1947),
and also to the scientist/theologian Pierre Tielhard de Chardin (1881–1955). A
few brief words on Whitehead and de Chardin are in order.
In addition to some important foundational work in logic and mathematics
early in the twentieth century, Whitehead is most closely associated with what is
termed process philosophy. In a nutshell, process philosophy views processes as
more fundamental than objects. That is, instead of viewing objects as the
fundamental constituents of reality, and then viewing events, change, and other
processes as arising out of the interactions of those basic objects, process
philosophy reverses this order. Processes are now viewed as fundamental, and
objects are viewed as arising out of processes and events.
This is, of course, nothing more than the most basic sketch of one aspect of
process philosophy. But it is enough to see how from such a perspective, the
world, including objects in the world, can only be understood in terms of the
constantly ongoing events and changes and processes and relationships out of
which objects in the world arise. The world, then, and things in the world, are not
static entities, but rather are constantly evolving processes.
Whereas Whitehead is closely associated with process philosophy, de Chardin
is closely associated with process theology. Different process theologians differ on
the details, but in general, they tend to agree that the old conception of God as an
independent agent who created the world and now stands separate from that
creation should be replaced with a notion more in keeping with process
philosophy. In such a conception, God is seen not as a thing existing separately
from the world, but instead is viewed as part of (or perhaps the sum of, or future
of) the ongoing, constantly changing, constantly evolving processes that underlie
the world. In this way, God is viewed as being a constant participant in the world.
Not a participant in the sense of a being who coercively interferes with how events
unfold in the world, but rather, by being involved in the processes that are
constantly unfolding, and unfolding according to natural principles.
Again, this is nothing more than the most cursory sketch of one aspect of
process theology, but it is enough to see how process theologians view God quite
differently from the way God has traditionally been viewed in western religions.
Haught is working within this broad tradition of process philosophy and process
theology. And, he argues, as an ongoing creative process in which new types of
organisms and new species are constantly evolving, evolutionary theory not only
ﬁts in with this sort of theology but enhances it.
Moreover, Haught argues that evolution could only take place in a universe that
has the right sort of balance between order and randomness. Too much order
would not allow the sort of chance events required for the evolutionary develop­
ment of life on Earth. On the other hand, a universe with too much chance, too
much randomness, would not allow for the regularity needed for life to develop as
it has in our evolutionary past. And given the sort of balance between order and
randomness we see in the universe (which presumably can be traced back to the

30 Reﬂections on Evolution

conditions present at the beginning of the universe), the universe could be
expected to unfold in a certain broad way. Not unfold in a precise, predetermined
direction, but rather, unfold at least roughly in a certain direction, and a direction
that includes the eventual development of some sort of complex, intelligent life.
Not necessarily (or even probably) human life, or any of the particular species that
happen to be alive today. But given the initial conditions of the universe, Haught
argues that life was likely to develop somewhere in the universe, and among the
varieties of life that could be expected to appear, some sort of intelligent life,
capable of understanding and appreciating the universe, was likely to appear.
So in Haught’s theology, the universe was not created by God such that it was,
like the pool and fountain on my deck, fully planned and nearly fully completed
shortly after the creation. Nor was it created so that it would unfold according to a
speciﬁc, blueprint-like plan. Nor are the day-to-day workings of the universe
planned by God, nor does this God interfere in the day-to-day workings of the
universe. God is not some sort of “thing” existing separately and independently
from the universe. Rather, the ongoing unpredictable processes, including evolu­
tionary processes, that make up the universe mean that each moment the universe
is different, each moment there is an ongoing creative process. And as Haught
phrases it, this is a universe that is always being drawn, so to speak, toward the
future. And God, Haught argues, is the future toward which, somewhat meta­
phorically, the universe is constantly being drawn.
Conceived of in this way, God is intimately involved in the universe and in the
ongoing creative processes that arise from the balance between chaos and order.
The universe, then, is not engaged in just some sort of “meaningless rambling.”
Rather, the universe has a purpose to it, tied to the way it is unfolding roughly in a
certain direction, and unfolding entirely in accordance with natural principles
including the principles at the core of evolutionary theory.
The above is but a brief sketch of parts of Haught’s views. It is a view that clearly
goes beyond empirical science and empirical evidence (more on this below). But it
is a view, Haught would maintain, that fully accepts evolutionary theory, and fully
accepts the implications of modern natural science in general. Yet within this
framework, he argues, there is still room for a God who participates in the
universe. Not by interfering with what takes place, but rather by being part of
the processes that underlie the universe. And humans are in a sense special. Not
in the sense that the universe was made for humans, or that humans were the
intended outcome of the evolutionary process. But rather, given the initial
conditions at the beginning of the universe, and the appropriate balance between
randomness and order, some sort of intelligent beings were likely to result. And at
least one intelligent being that emerged happens to be us.
Discussion
We have largely focused in this section on the questions of whether one could
fully accept the implications of evolutionary theory, and more generally fully
accept the implications of natural science, and still believe in some sort of God
who is involved in the workings of the universe in an interesting way, and in a view
of humans as in some way special, and in a universe that has some sort of overall

305

306

Recent Developments

purpose to it. Above we looked at the views of a number of prominent scholars
who have argued, in a nutshell, that the answer is “no.” In contrast, Haught argues
that the answer is “yes.”
What are we to make of this disagreement? I think much of it stems from
differing views on what emphasis one thinks should be put on empirical evidence.
Is empirical evidence the only, and ﬁnal, word? The scholars in the ﬁrst camp
would argue that beliefs about the universe are empirical beliefs, and that the only,
or at least the primary, evidence for such beliefs must be empirical. And they
argue that the empirical evidence does not leave much if any room for a
traditional sort of God.
Haught agrees that empirical evidence is very important. However, he is quite
clear that he thinks some beliefs about the universe – for example, providing what
he calls an “ultimate explanation” for certain features of the universe – is outside
the realm of natural science and is a legitimate function of theology, rather than
being solely a matter of straightforward empirical evidence.
Importantly, note that the two camps are not arguing about the empirical
evidence. By and large they agree on that. Rather, the disagreement stems from
differences in fundamental beliefs about how much emphasis to put on empirical
evidence. Those in the ﬁrst camp would say that when it comes to beliefs about
the nature of the universe, empirical evidence trumps. Beyond empirical evidence,
there is no further inquiry to engage in. Haught, on the other hand, would not
accept that inquiry must stop where the empirical evidence stops.
If this situation sounds familiar, it should. Recall the disagreement, discussed in
Chapter 17, between Galileo and Bellarmine over what evidence trumps what in
considerations about whether the sun moves around the Earth or the Earth moves
around the sun. If one has at the core of their beliefs, as Galileo did, that when
forming one’s beliefs about the universe empirical evidence should overwhelm­
ingly have priority, then I think one cannot consistently accept both a Darwinian
evolutionary picture and any sort of traditional God, or even the sort of
nontraditional God that Haught envisions.
But if one has a different set of core beliefs, then perhaps one can consistently
accept both the evolutionary picture and the sort of God that Haught envisions.
That is, if one has the sort of core jigsaw puzzle pieces similar to what are almost
certainly some of Haught’s core pieces, then perhaps one can fully accept the
Darwinian evolutionary account, and accept natural science in general, and still
believe in a God that, while certainly far from the traditional God of western
religions, is nonetheless involved in (or better, is part of) the universe.
As a ﬁnal note, it is perhaps worth pointing out that the disagreement between
the two camps is probably unresolvable, at least unresolvable by means of rational
argument. The central problem is an old one, recognized at least as early as the
time of the ancient Greeks, and one that is sometimes referred to as the “problem
of the criterion.” This problem is explained more fully in the Chapter Notes, but in
a nutshell the problem arises when two sides disagree on the proper criteria to use
in evaluating beliefs. In this case, one camp views empirical evidence as the only
(or at least as the overwhelmingly primary) criteria to use in arriving at correct
beliefs; the other camp does not. And when two sides disagree on an issue as basic
as how to go about arriving at proper beliefs – that is, they disagree on what

30 Reﬂections on Evolution

criteria should be used to evaluate beliefs – there is generally not much chance of
either side convincing the other, at least not by rational argument.

Morality and Ethics
A great deal has been written about implications our understanding of evolution
has for our views of morality and ethics. We cannot explore all such views, but
instead we will look at two general claims about evolution and morality, with an
eye toward getting a ﬂavor of some of the substantial implications recent scholars
have argued result from our understanding of evolution.
Some scholars have argued that our understanding of evolution requires we
accept that key aspects of our ethics and morality are, in a certain important sense,
an illusion. Consider, for example, the sense of objectivity that accompanies many
of our moral judgments, especially on controversial topics. Abortion provides a
good example. Opponents of abortion tend to feel that abortion really is wrong,
objectively wrong. In expressing opposition to abortion, the sense is that they are
not merely expressing a subjective preference, as with a preference for certain
ﬂavors of ice cream; rather, they are expressing, they say, something that is
objectively correct.
In the times we are feeling a deep moral sentiment, for example a deep sense of
outrage at a rape, or a brutal murder, or child abuse, I think it is safe to say we all
have this sense of objectivity – we have the feeling that the action really is wrong.
This sense of objectivity is strong, and widespread. How are we to understand this
sense of objectivity, especially since it seems increasingly clear that our moral
sentiments are as they are largely because in some way they provided an evolu­
tionary advantage, and not because our moral sentiments reﬂect an objective
feature of the world?
A number of writers, most notably Michael Ruse and E. O. Wilson, have argued
that our sense of the objectivity of moral judgments was crucial in our evolu­
tionary history, in particular, crucial for morality to have performed the evolu­
tionary task that it has. And, presumably, that sense of objectivity is necessary for
morality to perform the task it continues to do.
The idea is that moral judgments play an important role in shaping ours and
others’ behavior, and in order to play this role, such judgments need to have a
sense of objectivity about them. Judgments that we view as subjective do not carry
the weight, the ability to mold behavior, that objectively viewed judgments have.
Take subjective ice cream preferences, for example. I may disagree with you about
your actions in choosing a certain ﬂavor of ice cream. But the subjectivity of such
preferences means I do not care about trying to change your behavior. In contrast,
I and others have a great deal of interest in changing behavior of yours that we
judge to be morally offensive, and the seeming objectivity of our judgments is
crucial in motivating us to get you to change such behavior. This is the sense in
which the seeming objectivity of moral judgments was and is crucial in morality
playing the evolutionary role it has.
But we can now see, from understanding the evolutionary origins of our moral
sentiments, that this sense of objectivity is an illusion. Strong moral sentiments do

307

308

Recent Developments

not reﬂect something that is objectively real, but it seems to be objectively real. So
this sense of objectivity is an illusion. But it is an important illusion. And unlike
some illusions, it is not one that disappears once it is pointed out.
An analogy might help clarify this. There is general agreement that phenome­
nal colors, for example, our experience of red, the redness of red, so to speak, is
not an objective feature of objects. Rather, objects appear red as a result of the way
our particular visual systems evolved and the way our visual systems respond to
characteristics of the light striking our retinas. Had organisms such as us, with the
types of visual systems we have, never appeared, there would be no redness. Thus
redness is a subjective feature of the way our visual systems respond to certain
features of light, rather than an objective feature of the world.
So from modern science we can understand that our experience of redness is
not an objective feature, but rather a subjective feature, and one with its roots in
our evolutionary history. But even if we understand all this about the lack of
objectivity of a color such as red, we continue, and must continue, seeing certain
types of objects as being red. We are simply built that way.
Scholars such as Ruse argue that our morality is likewise part of how we are
built. We can no more stop viewing certain acts as morally wrong – and feeling
that they are objectively morally wrong – any more than we can stop viewing
certain objects, such as a typical ripe apple, as red. In this way, then, this new and
insightful understanding of the illusory nature of the sense of objectivity that
accompanies moral judgments will not, these writers argue, lead us to behave in
morally different ways. But it is an important insight into our sense of morality.
Another important point in properly understanding these views – and this is a
distinction that is easy to miss – is recognizing that such scholars are not arguing
that morality is not, in any sense, real. There is an important sense in which our
morality, and redness, are real. What redness is not, and what morality is not, is
objective, that is, they do not reﬂect features of the world that exist independently
of humans. Instead, redness and morality are subjective features, dependent on
humans, and resulting in large part from of our evolutionary history. Had humans
