In fall 2015 my sister and I communicated about our observations of a particularly
nice total lunar eclipse that had occurred a few days earlier. She lives about 3,000
miles west of me and, for example, she described totality (when the entirety of the
moon is in the Earth’s shadow) as beginning at 7:30 p.m. local time with the moon
very low on the horizon. In my location, I observed totality beginning at 10:30 p.m.
local time with the moon quite high in the sky. From this we can infer not only
that the times of lunar eclipses differ, but also that the time the moon rises differs
depending on where one is located east and west on the Earth. And since the
moon is reported in both locations as in the same region of the sky relative to the
stars, sun, and planets, from this we can also ﬁgure out that those must rise and set
at different times depending on one’s east–west location. The ancients would
have made the same inferences, using records from various parts of the world of
the times of events such as eclipses.

Chapter 11: Astronomical Data: The Empirical Facts;
Chapter 12: Astronomical Data: The Philosophical/
Conceptual Facts
For further discussion of the material discussed in Chapter 11 and Chapter 12,
Dreyer (1953) and Kuhn (1957) are good starting points. More general discus­
sions of these topics can be found in Cohen (1985) and Pine (1989).

Chapter 13: The Ptolemaic System; Chapter 14: The
Copernican System; Chapter 15: The Tychonic System;
Chapter 16: Kepler’s System
Many of the original sources for the material discussed in Chapter 13 through
Chapter 16 are readily attainable in new translations. See Ptolemy (1998) for a
new translation, with commentary by the translator, of Ptolemy’s Almagest.
Likewise, Copernicus (1995) is a new translation, again with commentary, of
Copernicus’ major work On the Revolution of Heavenly Spheres. And Kepler
(1995) is a new translation of some of his key works.
Dreyer (1953) and Kuhn (1957) are the best secondary sources for general
descriptions of these systems. Gingerich (1993) is a collection of much more
speciﬁc and detailed papers by a leading scholar in this area, and this work also
provides good examples of the sort of more detailed research done in the history
of science.
As mentioned at the end of Chapter 15, the Tychonic system (or more
precisely, the Tychonic system modiﬁed so as to incorporate elliptical orbits
with planets moving at varying speeds) is the preferred system for those still

337

338

Chapter Notes and Suggested Reading

maintaining (generally for religious reasons) that the Earth is the center of the
universe. Further information on modern-day defenders of the Tychonic system
can be found at www.geocentricity.com. The writings of the defenders of geo­
centricity provide interesting illustrations of many of the issues discussed in Part I,
especially issues involving competing worldviews, falsiﬁability, evidence, and
conﬁrmation and disconﬁrmation reasoning.
Finally, an inexpensive digital orrery (that is, a computer-generated model of
the solar system) is available for most common electronic devices (smartphones,
computers, etc.). Digital orreries are quite useful for viewing simulations of the
solar system, and I recommend ﬁnding one for your favorite electronic device.
Many of these will allow you the option of having the Earth be the center of
planetary rotation, thereby providing a model of the modernized Tychonic system
mentioned several times in the book.

Chapter 17: Galileo and the Evidence from the Telescope
Galileo’s own writing is quite accessible, and translations of Galileo’s main
writings involving his work with the telescope, as well as his views on the Earthcentered versus sun-centered issue, can be found in Galileo (1957) and Galileo
(2001). Fantoli (1996) is a detailed and extremely well-referenced work on
Galileo, especially on the issues involving the church, and highly recommended
for additional reading on Galileo. Santillana (1955) is a more general account of
Galileo’s work, also with emphasis on issues involving Galileo and the church.
Machamer (1998) is a collection of more narrowly focused papers on aspects of
Galileo’s work, and provides a good sense of the sort of more detailed
scholarship involving Galileo. Biagioli (1993) and Sobel (2000) are somewhat
different, but quite enjoyable, approaches to Galileo’s life and work. The former
focuses on the role of court politics in Galileo’s work (as noted in the chapter,
Galileo was a member of the Medici court), while the latter emphasizes his
relationship with his daughter, using the surviving letters from his daughter to
provide a somewhat different look at the life and work of Galileo and his
daughter. For those interested in looking further into the Inquisition’s 1615–16
investigation into the Earth-centered versus sun-centered issue, as well as the
Inquisition’s later trial of Galileo, Mayer (2012) is a convenient collection of the
key original documents.
Finally, I would like to thank Greg Welty for bringing to my attention an
inconsistency in my earlier (second edition) description of Galileo’s views on the
reliability of scripture.

Chapter 18: A Summary of Problems Facing the Aristotelian
Worldview
Cohen (1985) and Kuhn (1957) provide good general accounts of the sorts of
problems facing the Aristotelian worldview in the early 1600s. A more detailed

Chapter Notes and Suggested Reading

account of developments at this time can be found in Dijksterhuis (1961) and
Mason (1962).

Chapter 19: Philosophical and Conceptual Connections in the
Development of the New Science
Kuhn (1957) provides further discussion on many of the topics discussed in this
chapter. Mason (1962), although focusing primarily on the history of science,
provides good detailed discussion of some of the broader issues discussed in this
chapter. Dijksterhuis (1961) and Toulmin and Goodﬁeld (1961) likewise provide
somewhat more detailed accounts of many of the topics touched on in this
chapter, and are good sources for further investigating these topics.

Chapter 20: Overview of the New Science and the Newtonian
Worldview
Newton’s Principia is highly recommended, and Newton (1999) is a new
translation, with extensive commentary provided by the translators. Cohen
(1985) again provides a general overview of these developments, while Dijkster­
huis (1961) and Mason (1962) provide more detailed accounts. Also, I want to
thank Charles Ess for his suggestion to include the discussion of instrumentalist
and realist attitudes toward Newton’s notion of gravity.

Chapter 21: Philosophical Interlude: What Is a Scientiﬁc Law?
An early and still classic work on issues involving laws is Hempel and Oppenheim
(1948). Armstrong (1983) and Carroll (1994) provide thorough treatments of these
issues, and Lange (2000) provides a good summary of standard views and offers an
alternative account. Cartwright (1983) provides an interesting and somewhat
different perspective on some of the common attitudes toward laws, as does Gierre
(1999). For early discussions of problems involving counterfactuals, Goodman
(1983) and Quine (1964) are good sources, as is Lewis (1973). For more on ceteris
paribus clauses, see Earman, Glymour, and Mitchell (2003), and Lange (2002a).

Chapter 22: The Development of the Newtonian Worldview,
1700–1900
Mason (1962) provides a good account of the scientiﬁc developments in the
period covered in this chapter, and Kragh (1999) is a good source for the situation
in physics at the end of the 1800s. Cushing (1998) likewise provides a good
investigation of many of the topics covered in this chapter, with more of an eye

339

340

Chapter Notes and Suggested Reading

toward the interactions between scientiﬁc questions and philosophical issues.
Everitt (1975) contains a detailed account of Maxwell’s contributions.

Part III: Recent Developments in Science and Worldviews
For general sources dealing with the material in Part III, Kragh (1999) is a
relatively new and quite detailed history of physics in the twentieth century,
covering the situation in physics ranging from the late 1800s to the end of the
1900s. Mason (1962) likewise covers developments in science, both biology and
physics, albeit somewhat more brieﬂy. The interplay between philosophy and
physics in recent years is nicely illustrated by many of the case studies in Cushing
(1998).

Chapter 23: The Special Theory of Relativity
The original source for the special theory of relativity is Einstein (1905), and
Einstein (1920) provides an accessible account of the theory. Mermin (1968) is an
excellent presentation of special relativity, which is thorough and precise while
presupposing nothing beyond basic algebra. A recent revision of this book,
Mermin (2005) is likewise a good source. D’Abro (1950) is another good
presentation of special relativity, while Kosso (1998) provides a general overview
of special relativity with an emphasis on certain philosophical consequences.
It is worth noting that “absolute space” and “absolute time” are often used in a
different way than I use them in this chapter. From the time of Newton and
Leibniz, and continuing today, there has been debate over whether space is an
entity existing separately from the objects that (presumably) exist within that
space. That is, is space a substance existing independently of objects, or does
space consist of nothing over and beyond the relations between objects? The
former view is generally considered a substantivalist view of space, with the latter
view a relational view of space. To use a common analogy, the substantivalist view
maintains that space is like a container, and objects exist within that container.
Importantly, on this view the container – that is, space – exists independently of,
and has properties independent of, the objects within the container. The
relational view of space rejects this “container” view of space, maintaining instead
that space consists of nothing more than the relations between objects. The term
“absolute space” is sometimes used to refer to the substantivalist view of space,
and again, this is a somewhat different use of the term than the way I use it in this
chapter. Similar questions arise for time as well – is time something that exists
independently of objects and events, or is time nothing over and above the
relations between objects and events?
Finally, in the body of the chapter I made reference to the Lorentz transfor­
mations, without specifying what those transformations are. For those who are
curious, suppose we let x, y, z, and t represent spatial and time dimensions in a
stationary coordinate system, and x´ , y´ , z´ , and t´ represent spatial and time

Chapter Notes and Suggested Reading

dimensions in a coordinate system moving in the x direction with velocity v (that
is, moving relative to the ﬁrst coordinate system, and as usual, moving in a straight
line with uniform speed). Deﬁne γ as follows:

These are the transformations used, in the discussion of Joe’s spacetime

Chapter 24: The General Theory of Relativity
The original source of the general theory of relativity is Einstein (1916), and
Einstein (1920) again is an accessible discussion of the theory. D’Abro (1950) is
likewise a good source for discussion of the general theory.
As mentioned in the body of the chapter, ﬁgures such as Figure 24.2 are
typically two-dimensional “slices” through the four-dimensional spacetime. It is
not crucial for the discussion in the chapter, but you might notice that, in the
diagram, this two-dimensional slice is “embedded” in a three-dimensional
space, and for this reason such diagrams are typically referred to as embedding
diagrams. Also, I want to thank an anonymous reviewer for catching a
substantial mistake in my description of geodesics in an earlier draft of this
chapter.

Chapter 25: Philosophical Interlude: Are (Some) Scientiﬁc
Theories Incommensurable?
As noted in the chapter, in roughly the past half century Kuhn and Feyerabend
have been most closely associated with the notion of incommensurability. Kuhn
(1962) is the best source for his early views on the subject, and as mentioned
previously, this is one of the best-known works in the history and philosophy of
science, and well worth being familiar with. This book was reprinted in 1970 with
a worthwhile postscript in which Kuhn elaborates on his views on
incommensurability, progress in science, and more. Conant and Haugeland
(2000) is an excellent collection of papers, including several by Kuhn, comment­
ing on developments in topics raised in Kuhn (1962).

341

342

Chapter Notes and Suggested Reading

Feyerabend (1962) is a good source for his early views on incommensurability,
as well as his views on a variety of related topics. It is worth noting that
Feyerabend’s views on incommensurability are often presented in the context
of works that are primarily focused on theses other than incommensurability, for
example, arguments against there being any robust notion of unity among the
various sciences, arguments against the view that there is anything like a basic
methodology shared among the sciences, and more. Among the array of works by
Feyerabend that touch upon incommensurability, in addition to Feyerabend
(1962) I would recommend Feyerabend (1965, 1975, 1981).
In the chapter I noted that Duhem was one of the early ﬁgures, in the late 1800s
and early 1900s, to begin applying the notion of incommensurability beyond its
more rigidly deﬁned use with mathematics. Duhem (1954; originally published
1904) is a good source for his views.
For the original use of the notion of incommensurability by ancient Greek
mathematicians, and the important role this concept played in the discovery of
irrational numbers, Kline (1972) is an excellent three-volume work tracing the
development of mathematical thought from the ancient Babylonian and Egyptian
civilizations to modern times. Boyer (1968) is another good (single-volume) work
tracing the history of mathematics, and Klein (1968) is a good source for ancient
Greek mathematical developments.
Finally, the original source for the experiment involving anomalous playing
cards mentioned in the chapter is Bruner and Postman (1949).

Chapter 26: Introduction to Quantum Theory: Basic Empirical
Facts and the Mathematics of Quantum Theory
The emphasis on keeping clear the distinction between quantum facts,
quantum theory itself, and the interpretation of quantum theory is drawn
largely from Herbert (1985). The distinction is, I think, a very useful one to
keep in mind when reading the vast and often uneven literature on quantum
theory.
The quantum facts described in this chapter are fairly standard examples
widely used to illustrate some of the oddities associated with quantum facts. Pine
(1989) discusses similar experiments to those discussed here.
A general “descriptive” overview of the mathematics of quantum theory, similar
to that in this chapter, can be found in Herbert (1985). For more detailed accounts
of the mathematics, the best approach probably depends largely on one’s
mathematical background. My favorites are Hughes (1989), Baggott (1992),
and Baggott (2004).
In the body of the chapter I mentioned that I would provide a more detailed
summary of the mathematics of quantum theory. In summary: (a) a (pure) state
of a quantum system is represented by a vector in a Hilbert space; (b) each type of
measurement one might perform on a quantum system is associated with a
particular operator in the Hilbert space; and (c) predictions about the outcome
of a measurement on the quantum system are arrived at by ﬁnding the

Chapter Notes and Suggested Reading

eigenvalues associated with that operator (that is, the operator associated with
that measurement).
To understand (a), consider the two-dimensional Cartesian coordinate system
we studied in our earlier mathematical training, and suppose we have a line drawn
from the point (0,0) to some other arbitrary point, say the point (11,7). Such a line
would be an example of a vector, and a collection of such vectors is a vector space
over the two-dimensional space of the real numbers. Vector spaces can involve
three, four, or any (including inﬁnitely many) dimensions. Vector spaces can
likewise involve numbers other than the real numbers, and in the mathematics of
quantum theory, some particularly important vector spaces involve complex
numbers (that is, a number of the form a + bi, where a and b are real numbers,
and i is the imaginary number equal to the square root of 1)
Let’s use an analogy to get a rough idea of a Hilbert space. Consider the set of
vector spaces over the two-dimensional space of the real numbers. Note that
some of these vector spaces will meet various criteria. Some of these vector spaces
consist only of vectors that are speciﬁed by pairs of even numbers, some might
consist only of vectors speciﬁed by positive numbers, and others might consist
only of vectors for which certain mathematical operations can be applied. A
Hilbert space is a vector space that meets certain well-deﬁned, well-understood
criteria, and admits of certain types of mathematical operations. The speciﬁc
criteria are beyond the scope of our discussion, but this should sufﬁce to give at
least a general idea of a Hilbert space.
An operator in a Hilbert space is a function that operates on vectors, trans­
forming one vector into another. To understand the idea behind an eigenvalue,
consider again the set of all vectors over the space of the two-dimensional space of
the real numbers. Consider a particular operator O and a particular vector v.
Suppose Ov results in a vector twice the length of v, which we will write as 2v.
Note that O may not simply be a doubling operator, that is, it may not double the
length of all vectors. But there may be certain vectors, such as v, for which O
results in a vector twice the length of the original. That is, for this particular
vector, Ov = 2v. In this case, v is called an eigenvector of O, and 2 is the
corresponding eigenvalue. Likewise, if Ov = 3v, then v is an eigenvector of O
and 3 is the corresponding eigenvalue. Also, some operators have no eigenvectors
and hence no corresponding eigenvalues. Hilbert spaces are more complicated
vector spaces, and the notion of eigenvectors and eigenvalues for such spaces is
more difﬁcult to picture. But the analogy with the simpler vector space over the
space of the two-dimensional Cartesian coordinate system should give a ﬂavor of
what these are like.
Recall that an operator is a function that transforms one vector into another,
and, from (b) above, we know that possible measurements on a quantum system
are associated with particular operators on the Hilbert space. With respect to (c)
above: most of the time (though not always) the operators associated with
measurements will have eigenvectors and corresponding eigenvalues. The eigen­
values represent the possible results of the measurement associated with the
operator. In particular, from the eigenvalue and the vector representing the state
of the system, together with a special operator called a projection operator, one
can calculate a probability between 0 and 1. And that probability represents the

343

344

Chapter Notes and Suggested Reading

probability of observing the particular measurement outcome associated with
that eigenvalue.

Chapter 27: The Reality Question: The Measurement Problem
and Interpretations of Quantum Theory
There are an enormous number of books, of quite uneven quality, generally
revolving around the interpretation of quantum theory and including discussions
of the measurement problem. Here I will note a few of the books I think are of
better quality. As mentioned in the chapter, the physicist John Bell has articulated
concerns about the measurement problem and issues involving interpretations
more thoroughly and clearly than probably anyone else. Bell (1988) Speakable
and Unspeakable in Quantum Mechanics is an excellent collection of his writings
on these and related issues. Herbert (1985) is another good source, written by a
physicist but for a general audience, and although he has his preferred interpre­
tation, Herbert provides an even-handed treatment of the options. Baggott (1992)
likewise is a good discussion of quantum theory and issues involved in the
interpretation of quantum theory. Baggott’s book, incidentally, is subtitled A
Guide for Students of Chemistry and Physics. I would ignore the subtitle, in that
the book is a good guide to quantum theory and the interpretation of quantum
theory, regardless of whether you are a student of physics or chemistry. Baggott
(2004) is a substantial revision and expansion of this work, and also recom­
mended. The last chapter of Lange (2002b) likewise provides a good discussion of
these issues. Also, I would like to thank Marc Lange for catching a substantial
mistake in my discussion of Bohm’s interpretation in an earlier draft of this
chapter.
In the body of the chapter I broadly distinguish between collapse interpreta­
tions and non-collapse interpretations. Collapse interpretations, as used in the
chapter, should not be confused with what are sometimes referred to as collapse
theories. The phrase “collapse theories” has come to be used to describe relatively
recent research projects that attempt to provide a modiﬁed mathematics for
quantum theory, but a mathematics that, unlike Bohm’s approach, does not
proceed by way of hidden variables. Collapse theories, then, are not interpreta­
tions of the standard mathematics for quantum theory, but rather are attempts to
provide a modiﬁed mathematical framework. Notably, collapse theories, unlike
Bohm’s approach, appear to provide in some cases predictions that differ from the
predictions of the standard approach, though at present these seem unable to be
tested due to practical considerations.

Chapter 28: Quantum Theory and Locality: EPR, Bell’s
Theorem, and the Aspect Experiments
Herbert (1985) provides a good general discussion of the topics in this chapter,
and as noted, my explanation of Bell’s theorem draws substantially from Herbert’s

Chapter Notes and Suggested Reading

presentation. Baggott (1992, 2004) also provide excellent, and somewhat more
detailed, accounts of these issues. For issues involving locality, Maudlin (1994) is a
detailed and careful analysis of the quite complex issues involved, and is highly
recommended for anyone interested in further pursuing topics involving locality
and nonlocality.
Since the original Aspect experiments in the early 1980s, experiments
involving tests of Bell-like inﬂuences have become commonplace (such experi­
ments are often referred to as Bell tests or Bell experiments). Generally, these
experiments go beyond the experimental setup used in the original Aspect
experiments. For example, whereas in the original Aspect experiments the
detectors were separated by about 10 meters, more recent experiments have
increased this distance substantially, including some with detectors over 100
kilometers apart. There is currently a proposed experiment for the Interna­
tional Space Station (ISS), where one detector would be on the ISS and the other
in a ground-based laboratory (so the distance would be about 400 kilometers).
China has recently launched a satellite dedicated to experiments involving
quantum theory, including planned tests of Bell-like inﬂuences at distances of
over 1,000 kilometers.
Another category of Bell tests involves closing what are commonly referred to
as loopholes. In this context, loopholes are essentially auxiliary hypotheses of the
sort discussed frequently in this book. As noted, Bell tests to date have strongly
suggested that reality must be nonlocal. However, in such experiments it is
important that the setting of the detectors be random, and thus one could try to
preserve locality by questioning whether the settings genuinely were random. For
example, perhaps something in the common past of the devices used to generate
randomness resulted in the settings not being truly random. To help close this
loophole, a team of physicists have recently been conducting a series of Bell
experiments using increasingly distant sources to generate randomness. Their
most recent experiments (a description can be found at https://phys.org/news/
2017-02-physicists-loophole-bell-inequality-year-old.html) generated random
settings based on light from stars hundreds of light years away. These stars
have not shared a common past for over 600 years, and thus if there is anything in
their past that prevented them from being sources of genuine randomness, it had
to have happened over 600 years ago. The next series of experiments will use
quasars billions of light years away as a source of randomness, and if, as expected,
the results are the same as with previous Bell tests, any common cause preventing
genuine randomness would had to have happened billions of years ago. And this,
it is widely agreed, would be extraordinarily implausible. In short, such experi­
ments are increasingly closing various loopholes. Or in other words, they are
showing that attempts to save locality by appeal to such auxiliary hypotheses are
quite implausible.

Chapter 29: Overview of the Theory of Evolution
Desmond and Moore (1991) is a relatively recent, thorough, and highly recom­
mended biography of Darwin and his work. Quammen (2006) is a much shorter

345

346

Chapter Notes and Suggested Reading

account, though informative and accessible, and also recommended. For Darwin’s
key work, Darwin (1964) is a good facsimile of the ﬁrst edition of the Origin of
Species.
It is worth mentioning that some of Darwin’s and Wallace’s key ideas had
been, at least in a limited way, hinted at earlier. For example, Darwin’s
grandfather Erasmus Darwin had hinted (somewhat obscurely) at ideas
similar to the key ingredients of natural selection. Likewise, an expert in
wood products for shipbuilding named Patrick Matthews, 30 years before
Darwin and Wallace published their key works on evolution, had articulated
principles quite similar to the basic ideas behind natural selection. Darwin’s
grandfather, however, suggested his ideas mainly in some of his published
poetry rather than in any sort of scientiﬁc publication. And Matthews men­
tioned these principles in the context of a book devoted to the best sources of
wood for naval purposes, and beyond this, he never promoted or defended these
key ideas (at least not until after the publication of Darwin’s and Wallace’s key
works). In short, Darwin and Wallace deserve primary credit, if not for being the
absolute ﬁrst to develop the key ideas, then at least for being the individuals
responsible for thoroughly articulating and defending them. And of these two,
for reasons explained in the body of the chapter, Darwin deserves the major
share of the credit.
Mayr (1982) is an extensive and detailed account of developments in biology in
recent centuries, including evolutionary theory. Provine (1971) is likewise a good
source. For briefer overviews, Mason (1962) provides a quick overview of key
developments, as does Silver (1998). Wilson (1969) and Greene (1969) provide a
more detailed discussion within the context of broader developments in biology
during the time surrounding Darwin’s and Wallace’s key works.
Fisher (1999) is a key work in the eventual development of population genetics,
and Williams (1966) and Hartl (1981) provide thorough accounts of this ﬁeld. The
relevant chapters in Mayr (1982) also provide good additional information on this
subject.
Watson and Crick (1953) is a classic paper announcing the discovery of the
structure of DNA, and well worth reading. Olby (1974) is a good thorough
account of this discovery. This time is also a rich period for exploring broader
questions on the conduct of science, such as the reluctance to acknowledge
important contributions of women working in this ﬁeld. Watson and Crick, for
example, failed to acknowledge the work of Rosalind Franklin, even though their
discoveries concerning the structure of DNA hinged crucially on her work. Fox
Keller (1983) and Sayre (1975) provide good starting points for further explora­
tion of these topics.
As noted in the body of the chapter, it is difﬁcult to overemphasize the areas of
research that recent discoveries have made possible, especially the discovery of
restriction enzymes and subsequent tools for manipulating DNA. Abzhanov et al.
(2006), Bergman and Siegal (2003), and Mecklenburg (2010) are good examples.
Finally, I’d like to thank my colleague Jim Long for assistance and helpful
discussions of possible translations of Latin phrases from Darwin’s notebooks
mentioned in the chapter. Darwin’s handwriting was terrible, and so this was no
easy task.

Chapter Notes and Suggested Reading

Chapter 30: Reﬂections on Evolution
Good sources for the views of those discussed in the chapter making the case that
evolution, and modern science in general, leaves little or no room for anything like
a traditional God are Dennett (1995, 2006) and Dawkins (2006). To a lesser
extent, but still foreshadowing writings to come later, Dawkins (1976) and
Weinberg (1992) are interesting reads. Two prominent writers taking a somewhat
different line of argument, though not mentioned in the text, are Harris (2004,
2007) and Hitchens (2007). For Haught’s views, see primarily Haught (2008a,
2008b), but also Haught (2001). For other related approaches to reconciling
religion and evolution, see Mooney (1996) and Miller (1999). A work somewhat
further aﬁeld of the material in this chapter, but still an interesting take on
evolution and religion, is Miller (2008).
For the discussion of morality and ethics, Ruse and Wilson’s views are most
thoroughly articulated in Ruse (1998) and Wilson (1978). Ruse (2009) is an
excellent collection of articles on these and related matters.
For early work on the iterated prisoner’s dilemma, the best sources are Axelrod
(1980a, 1980b, 1984). Studies involving the ultimatum game, trust game, and
others can be found in Gintis et al. (2004), Kosfeld et al. (2005), and Bohnet and
Zeckhauser (2004). A detailed and thorough treatment of a range of issues
involving altruism and unselﬁsh behavior can be found in Sober and Wilson
(1998).
Regarding the naturalistic fallacy, the classic source is Moore (1962), which was
ﬁrst published in 1903, though the version discussed in this chapter dates back to
Hume (1992), ﬁrst published in 1739. The other version of what is often termed
the “naturalistic fallacy,” and the version of primary interest to Moore, is an
argument that any attempt to provide a naturalistic foundation for normative
ethical claims is misguided. In support of this, Moore notes what is now
commonly referred to as the open question argument: given any alleged identiﬁ­
cation of something morally good with some natural property, it will still make
sense to ask of something having that natural property whether it is good. In other
words, given that something has the property is question, it is still an open
question as to whether it is good, and if it is still an open question, good must not
consist in having that natural property.
In the body of the chapter I mention the “problem of the criterion,” which is a
problem concerning the justiﬁcation of beliefs that arises when different groups
disagree on the proper criteria to use in evaluating beliefs. The problem was
recognized as early as the ancient Greeks. A classic example can be found in the
Protestant Reformation beginning in the 1500s. Followers of reformation leaders
such as Martin Luther, and defenders of the traditional approach of the Catholic
church, disagreed on what the correct criteria were for determining whether
certain religious views were or were not correct. When there is this sort of deep
disagreement over what the proper criteria are for evaluating beliefs, there really is
little chance of the differing sides resolving the dispute rationally – the two sides
lack any common understanding on how to rationally resolve the dispute.
Note that most situations involving disagreements are not situations involving
the problem of the criterion. For example, suppose you and I are involved in a car

347

348

Chapter Notes and Suggested Reading

accident, and we disagree over who is responsible. While we disagree on who is
responsible, generally we will agree on the proper criteria to resolve the dispute.
For example, we will likely agree that the law is the proper criterion to use, and we
will resolve the dispute through the court system.
Consider the situation discussed in the chapter. Here, one side considers
empirical evidence to be the only (or at least by far the primary) criteria to use in
evaluating beliefs, whereas the other side believes criteria going beyond empirical
evidence are proper. This seems a classic problem of the criterion situation, and
thus unlikely to be resolved by rational debate.

Chapter 31: Worldviews: Concluding Thoughts
For those interested in the role of metaphors and analogies in science, Hesse
(1966) would be a good starting point. Other than this, the chapter is somewhat of
an overview of topics we have discussed, and of what the future might bring, and
so there is not a great deal to mention in the way of notes, sources, and suggested
reading. Instead I’ll close with a note I’ve mentioned several times. I hope in this
book to have provided a foundation solid enough to enable you to explore these
topics further. It’s a fascinating ﬁeld – enjoy!
